articles.title: Holly Trees
articles.author: Emma Bartley
articles.description: A speaker describes playing under holly trees.
articles.intro: Emma Bartley is an American poet. Her writing often focuses on childhood and memory. In this poem, a speaker describes playing under holly trees.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the words the speaker uses to describe their play.
articles.permissions: "Holly Trees" from American Literary Magazin e by Emma Bartley. Copyright © 2017 reprinted with permissions of the author and American Literary Magazine (amlitmag.com). All rights reserved.

blocks.text: We were small enough to stand
and not get pricked by the leaves.
So, in the thin, green tunnel
at the base of the holly trees, Maggie and I
made our own home. All day was a hunt for food
gathering smooth potato sized stones and meaty
pieces of mulch. Sometimes, while we sat there cooking
side by side in the dirt, I wondered if we were married.
And I thought she caught me once — snapping her neck
around, eyes widening, startled. She squealed, Emma?
But she was pointing at the muscular, hairy legs
outside the trees’ small opening. Emma,
who is knocking on our front door?

options.label: Adults often discourage children from engaging in pretend play.
Friendships formed during childhood are nearly unbreakable.
Adults don't have the same imaginative capabilities as kids do.
Children often imagine and pretend they are grown up.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following describes the main theme of the poem?

options.label: "We were small enough to stand / and not get pricked by the leaves." (Lines 1-2)
"at the base of the holly trees, Maggie and I / made our own home." (Lines 4-5)
"And I thought she caught me once—snapping her neck / around, eyes widening, startled." (Lines 9-10)
"But she was pointing at the muscular, hairy legs / outside the trees' small opening." (Lines 11-12)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the poem best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: Maggie is Emma's close friend.
Maggie is Emma's classmate.
Maggie is Emma's imaginary friend.
Maggie is Emma's wife.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best summarizes the Maggie's relationship to Emma?

options.label: to show that an adult is interrupting their imaginary play
to show that adults are not able to engage in imaginary play
to show that Emma and Maggie truly live inside a tree
to show that Emma and Maggie are afraid of adults

questions.prompt: Which option describes the main purpose of the final stanza of the poem (Lines 9-13)?
How does the word choice in stanza 3 contribute to the development of the theme (Lines 5-8)?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the poem, Maggie and Emma imagine they are living in a house together. Do you think adults are capable of the same imaginative play as children are? Why or why not? Do you still use your imagination to play?

In the poem, Maggie and Emma are pretending to lead adult lives. How do you think imaginative play helps children better understand the world and growing up? What kind of imaginative games did you play when you were younger?
Maggie and Emma play their pretend games under Holly trees. Why do you think they play their games out in nature? How does nature encourage children to use their imagination?

articles.title: Love and Friendship
articles.author: Emily Brontë
articles.description: Brontë uses figurative language to argue that friendship - not love - is everlasting.
articles.intro: Emily Brontë (1818-1848) was an English poet and novelist. While growing up, Brontë spent much of her time creating stories with her siblings, of whom two were also famous authors. Brontë was known for her solitary nature, and so it is interesting to consider her perspective on love and friendship.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the poet's use of figurative language.
articles.permissions: Love and Friendship by Emily Brontë is in the public domain.

blocks.text: Love is like the wild rose-briar,
Friendship like the holly-tree
The holly is dark when the rose-briar blooms
But which will bloom most constantly?
The wild rose-briar is sweet in spring,
Its summer blossoms scent the air;
Yet wait till winter comes again
And who will call the wild-briar fair?
Then scorn the silly rose-wreath now
And deck thee with the holly’s sheen,
That when December blights thy brow
He may still leave thy garland green.

options.label: The rose-briar is considered far more beautiful than the holly-tree.
The holly-tree is more resilient during winter than the rose-briar.
The rose-briar smells sweeter in spring than the holly-tree does in winter.
The holly-tree has prickly leaves for more of the year than the rose has thorns.

questions.prompt: In the poem, what is the most significant difference between the rose-briar and the holly-tree?

options.label: Nature has many different elements, but they are all equally important.
The best friendships and romantic relationships are grounded in trust and loyalty.
Friendships are more reliable and therefore more valuable to pursue than romantic love.
Most people will experience challenging moments in life and will need love and friendship to survive them.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following best identifies a theme of the text?

options.label: "Friendship like the holly-tree / The holly is dark when the rose-briar blooms" (Lines 2-3)
"The wild rose-briar is sweet in spring, / Its summer blossoms scent the air" (Lines 5-6)
"Yet wait till winter comes again / And who will call the wild-briar fair?" (Lines 7-8)
"Then scorn the silly rose-wreath now / And deck thee with the holly's sheen" (Lines 9-10)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which of the following quotes best supports the answer to Part A?
How does the poem's rhyme scheme contribute to the overall tone and theme?

discussionQuestions.prompt: According to the text, what are the qualities of the holly-tree? And how does that answer the question: What is friendship? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other art or literature in your answer.
Which is more lasting, love or friendship? Support your answer with evidence in the poem, and pay special attention to the symbolism the speaker uses.

articles.title: What Happened Here
articles.author: Mikala Rempe
articles.description: A speaker describes going hunting with their father.
articles.intro: Mikala Rempe is a poet originally from Nebraska, but is now based in D.C. Her work often centers around American regionalism, the Midwest, and the feminine relationship to trauma. Her work has previously been published in American Literary , Lines and Stars , and Viator Project . In this poem, a speaker describes going hunting with their father.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the speaker feels and acts on the hunting trip.
articles.permissions: "What Happened Here" from American Literary Magazine by Mikala Rempe. Copyright © 2016 by Mikala Rempe, reprinted with permission of author and American Literary Magazine . All rights reserved.

blocks.text: The green of the pheasant’s tailfeather screams violently
from behind the grey chalky thistle.
And my father tells me
“We don’t hunt for sport
what the Lord has given us
we honor and respect”
I carry my toddler innocence
in the oversized neon hunting vest
back to the truck. My father has
two roosters by the neck in each hand.
Their beaks close enough to kiss.
I try my best
not to wet myself.
Once home, I show my mother
all that I have learned. Aiming an empty
squirt gun at the top of our refrigerator.
“bam bam” “Woah” she whispers
pretending to hide behind an open cupboard door.
I run to my father in the garage, skinning
the birds. With a few lingering feathers
pressed down on his forearms. The headless
pheasants are resting in a pool of salt water
in the family dutch oven — a wedding gift.

options.label: Children learn their values from the lessons that their parents teach them.
Violence is a foreign concept to children.
Children will do anything to please their parents.
Adults don't understand how delicate a child's innocence can be.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following identifies a theme of the poem?

options.label: "'what the Lord has given us / we honor and respect'" (Lines 5-6)
"I carry my toddler innocence / in the oversized neon hunting vest / back to the truck." (Lines 7-9)
"Once home, I show my mother / all that I have learned." (Lines 14-15)
"pheasants are resting in a pool of salt water / in the family dutch oven — a wedding gift." (Lines 22-23)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the poem best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: The speaker is proud to accompany their father on a hunting trip.
Hunting makes the speaker mature more quickly.
The speaker has grown more mature by going hunting.
Hunting has made the speaker stronger.

questions.prompt: What do the lines "I carry my toddler innocence / in the oversized neon hunting vest / back to the truck" suggest about the speaker (Lines 7-9)?
How do the speaker's feelings about going hunting compare to their actions?

discussionQuestions.prompt: What are some activities that are particularly important to your family? Why are they important to your family?

In the context of the poem, how does fear drive action? How was the speaker affected by fear on the hunting trip? How did it impact the speaker's actions in the poem? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

What role does hunting play in the speaker's family? Do you think hunting is as important to the speaker as it is to their parents? Why or why not? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

In the context of the text, how do parents' values influence the way that their children understand the world? How strong do you think this influence is? Do you think children are free to choose their own values? Why or why not?

articles.title: Sonnet
articles.author: James Weldon Johnson
articles.description: Civil Rights Activist and NAACP leader James Weldon Johnson reflects on bravery, courage, and hope.
articles.intro: James Weldon Johnson (1871-1938) was an American writer and civil rights activist. Johnson served a vital role in the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP). The following poem by Johnson is a sonnet, which consists of fourteen rhyming lines. Purpose for Reading: To learn how a poet uses imagery and the juxtaposition of ideas to emphasize his message about bravery, courage, and hope.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the speaker's use of imagery in the poem.
articles.permissions: "Sonnet" by James Weldon Johnson (1893) is in the public domain.

blocks.text: My heart be brave, and do not falter so,
Nor utter more that deep, despairing wail.
Thy way is very dark and drear I know,
But do not let thy strength and courage fail;
For certain as the raven-winged night
Is followed by the bright and blushing morn,
Thy coming morrow will be clear and bright;
’Tis darkest when the night is furthest worn.
Look up, and out, beyond, surrounding clouds,
And do not in thine own gross darkness grope,
Rise up, and casting off thy hind’ring shrouds,
Cling thou to this, and ever inspiring hope:
Tho’ thick the battle and tho’ fierce the fight,
There is a power making for the right.

options.label: People fail to appreciate peace until it is disrupted by violence.
Hope and strength of character can help people through hard times.
An abundance of caution can help people avoid unpleasant experiences.
People who have experienced hardship find overcoming challenges easier than those who have not.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes the main theme of the poem?

options.label: "Thy way is very dark and drear" (Line 3)
"do not let thy strength and courage fail" (Line 4)
"Thy coming morrow will be clear and bright" (Line 7)
"'Tis darkest when the night is furthest worn." (Line 8)

questions.prompt: Which quotation best shows how the speaker wants to respond to the challenges they face?

options.label: The poem uses phrases such as "bright and blushing morn," suggesting that the speaker is excited for what will happen in the future, contributing to a gleeful tone.
The poem uses words such as "despairing" and "drear," revealing that the speaker is upset about the current situation, conveying a pessimistic tone.
The poem uses phrases such as "my heart," implying that the speaker is addressing a loved one, contributing to a affectionate tone.
The poem uses phrases such as "rise up" and "fierce the fight," suggesting the speaker feels inspired to act, contributing to an impassioned tone.

questions.prompt: How does the word choice in the poem contribute to its tone?
How does the poem's use of imagery develop the theme of the poem? Cite evidence from the text in your answer.
How does the structure of the poem contribute to its meaning? Cite evidence from the text in your answer.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Why do you think the speaker cautions the reader not to "grope" in their own "darkness"? What does this mean?
In the context of this poem, how does a person overcome adversity? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.
In the context of this poem, what does it mean to be brave? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

articles.title: The Journalist
articles.author: J. Patrick Lewis
articles.description: The poet assumes the voice of journalist and civil rights activist Helen Zia.
articles.intro: J. Patrick Lewis is the author of more than fifty books of poetry for children. "The Journalist" is a tribute poem to award-winning Asian American journalist, Helen Zia, which is featured in Lewis's 2013 book When Thunder Comes: Poems for Civil Rights Leaders. In this poem, the speaker describes how she uses journalism to address social issues.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the figurative language the poet uses to reveal the speaker’s perspective.
articles.permissions: "The Journalist" from When Thunder Comes by J. Patrick Lewis. Copyright © 2013 by J. Patrick Lewis. Used with permission. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: I am a woman with a foreign face —
Apple-pie American (born Chinese).
Nothing I do will ever hide my race.
Nothing I am bears those parentheses.
I wield a pen, this fine and fearless sword,
To open doors for which there are no keys.
The written word’s the law, the law’s the word.
No one I know deserves parentheses.
The newspaper I work for is the place
I bring these hate-crime villains to their knees —
Writing the wrongs that plague the human race.
Nothing I am wears those parentheses.
Whenever foreign faces take the stand
Against injustice, fear deadens their pleas.
Nothing I am, except a helping hand.
Nothing we are, chained by parentheses.
That foreign face, my countryman, is you,
Whose ancestors once settled overseas.
Congratulations, you are foreign too,
Though seldom have you worn parentheses.

options.label: The responsibility of a journalist is to provide solutions to social and political concerns in a society.
People who are not in influential positions are afraid to challenge America and have the tendency to ask powerful people to represent them.
Americans have abandoned the original principles of welcoming immigrants because they do not respect other cultures.
Despite discrimination in America, immigrants are not powerless and they have the capacity to create social change.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which statement best expresses a theme in the poem?

options.label: "Against injustice, fear deadens their pleas" (Line 14)
"Nothing we are, chained by parentheses." (Line 16)
"That foreign face, my countryman, is you" (Line 17)
"Though seldom have you worn parentheses." (Line 20)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the poem best supports the answer to PART A?

options.label: It creates a confused tone by highlighting how the speaker identifies with two different cultures.
It introduces the poem with a critical tone to show how Chinese people are not considered equal to Americans.
It develops a regretful tone because the speaker abandoned her Chinese identity to adopt American customs.
It establishes a humorous tone because the speaker is amused by the way Americans view Chinese people.

questions.prompt: How does the phrase "Apple-pie American (born Chinese)" in stanza 1 contribute to the speaker's tone toward her experience in America?

options.label: It points out the irony that descendants of immigrants discriminate against people who migrate to America today.
It suggests that Chinese immigrants are more patriotic than people born in America.
It argues that people should not be judged by their race because no one can change their ancestry.
It explains how the speaker chooses to handle racial prejudice in America.

questions.prompt: How does stanza 5 contribute to the meaning of the poem?
How does the speaker's use of repetition contribute to the overall message of the poem? Cite evidence from the poem to support your answer.

discussionQuestions.prompt: "The Journalist" is written from the perspective of a woman who is a Chinese American journalist, but the author of the poem is a European American man. What is the role of a writer's identity in the work they produce? How does knowledge about an author's background shape how you view their work, especially when they are writing about someone from a different background?
This poem reveals the journalist's bias in her reporting. Is her bias justified? What is the responsibility of a journalist?

The last stanza of the poem addresses the irony that many Americans reject immigrants, but they are descendants of immigrants. What does it mean to be American? Why do you think this contradiction exists?

The poet uses parentheses as a metaphor for oppression and marginalization. When have you or someone you know felt like someone did not respect a part of your identity? How does this poem relate to that experience?

articles.title: A House without a Gold Star
articles.author: Gary D. Schmidt
articles.description: A young boy's close-knit community is torn apart during World War II.
articles.intro: The military draft, commonly known as "the draft," is a process where individuals are selected for military service without choice. During World War II, all men in the United States aged 18-64 were required to register for the military draft. By the end of World War II, about 34 million men had registered, and 10 million served with the U.S. military. Additionally, anyone with German heritage, regardless of where they lived, could be drafted to serve in the German military. In this text, a community grapples with the impact of World War II.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the characters’ actions towards Mrs. Müller.
articles.permissions: "A House without a Gold Star" by Gary D. Schmidt. Copyright © 2024 by Gary D. Schmidt. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: The house where Augie Marx lived on 107th Street in Flushing, New York — a city name that sent his cousins into fantastic laughter but that had always seemed sort of normal to him — lay almost exactly in the middle of his block, five houses down from St. Paul’s German Lutheran Church and five houses up from Public School 107, where Augie was in fourth grade. It was, to Augie, the best place to live on all the earth — and being smack in the middle of it made it perfect.

In the house beside St. Paul’s lived Franzie Schneider, who was Augie’s co-explorer, best bike racer, and radio partner. Next to Franzie lived the Wagners, where Tommy and Timmy lived, identical twins who used to be able to pass as each other with no trouble at all until third grade when Tommy got beaned by a hardball that pushed his nose more than a little toward his right ear. Next to Tommy and Timmy lived Mrs. Müller, who lived in a small and smart house with black shutters and whitewashed brick walls. And from her house all the way down to P.S. 107 were houses with at least two kids per driveway — eight kids at the Lehmanns’ — including Augie’s house, where Augie’s older brother John was the fourth oldest kid on the block, having just turned eighteen. Mrs. Müller’s son, Friedrich, who was older than John and who never let him forget it, was the third oldest. Their birthdays were almost exactly six months apart: June 13 for Friedrich, December 13 for John.
To all the kids on 107th Avenue, John and Friedrich were gods.

They had been the fastest two kids in P.S. 107, and then the fastest in Washington B. Irving High School. They had the sharpest, quickest hands on Irving’s varsity basketball team, the strongest freestyle stroke in all the district’s swimming teams, and while Friedrich had the sweetest shortstop-to-first-base throw anyone had ever seen, John had the sweetest swing since Lou Gehrig had died two years earlier. In high school they had good jobs at the A & P, so they always had money in their pockets. If one of them was nearby when the ice cream man pedaled down 107th Street, no kid in sight ever left without an orange creamsicle. That’s the kind of guys they were.
Augie loved his brother like he loved the sun. Friedrich, too.

It was a block where as a kid, you had a ton of rules to think about — most to keep, some to break. On the one corner was P.S. 107, where Augie had to stand in line, keep his mouth shut, eat lunch quickly and quietly, learn to swim without trunks (no kidding), use the Palmer method for handwriting, and know the multiplication tables up to twelve. (Franzie Schneider could do them up to fifteen.) His fourth-grade teacher Miss Hatchet — that really was her name — had Augie in the corner whenever he forgot about the “Keeping-Your-Mouth-Shut” rule. She had Franzie in the Staff Bathroom with soap in his mouth whenever he forgot about the “A-Clean-Mouth-is-a-Clean-Heart” rule. There were rules about wasting cafeteria food, only taking one sheet of paper at a time, keeping your desk tidy, and filling the ink well only halfway so it didn’t spill. “There’s a war on,” Miss Hatchet would say. “We don’t waste our resources when our boys are fighting overseas.”
So Augie kept these rules.

On the other end of the street was St. Paul’s German Lutheran Church, and lots of the church rules were about being quiet too. You didn’t swing your legs and bang into the pews. You knelt without making a big fuss about pulling out the benches. You didn’t fidget during Father Fischer’s sermon, and when you stood on one of the lines to take holy Communion — Friedrich and his mother always stood in the line on the left side of the church, but Augie’s family stood on the right (John and Friedrich kept score about which of them had gotten to their chalice first) — you didn’t talk or smile or look around you. You stood with hands clasped and God on your mind. Father Fischer was very high church, Augie’s mother explained.
Augie thought he lived with a lot of rules.

There were rules on the street, too. In stickball, you never argue over a foul: that is always the catcher’s call. Ties go to the runner. The pitcher calls everyone back into play after a car drives through. The youngest kid on either team has to get any ball hit into the Neals’ yard because you were going to get yelled at and you had to take your turn. John and Friedrich had to play on opposing teams, and Neil Schneider — Franzie’s brother and the oldest kid on the block — had to keep score because he was the fairest kid on the planet and knew math like anything and was already practically engaged to Annalise Fischer so he could be trusted.

Neil Schneider was the first kid on the block to get drafted into the United States Navy after high school. He was killed in the Battle of the Coral Sea on May 7, 1942. Everyone on 107th Street stood in the Schneiders’ front yard the day his father and mother hung the gold star in their front window. Augie’s mom held his hand so tightly, it hurt. No one spoke. No one moved. The old men wore their World War One uniforms and saluted when the star was hung. Annalise Fischer bawled. Franzie did not come out of his house for two weeks, and when he did, he didn’t talk to anyone. Not even Augie.

Standing in the Schneider yard on that gray and drizzly afternoon, looking at the gold star, Augie figured this was all about another rule — maybe one you never, ever broke. When you watch someone put a gold star in their window, you stand still and shut up. There are no words that anyone needs to speak.
Annalise Fischer had no words to speak either that day. Or on any of the Sundays afterwards when Augie saw her in the family pew at St. Paul’s. Most Sundays, she bawled until her mother took her out.
She couldn’t help it.

Augie had only heard his brother John bawl once. He hadn’t figured he ever would. In his freshman year of high school, three years ago, John had broken a leg, sliding into home while trying to stretch a triple into an in-the-park home run. He’d made it before, but not this time. He had rounded third, and even though Friedrich had told him to hold, he was still running like a banshee. Then, out of the corner of his eye, John saw the ball coming in, and he ran even faster, and the catcher for Jackson Heights caught the ball and held it out in his mitt, and John angled his leg to slide under the tag, and their shins caught but the catcher was the one wearing the shin guard, and the umpire called “Out,” but that wasn’t what Augie heard. He heard what everyone in the park that day heard: the break.

Both teams gathered around John. One guy threw up when he saw John’s leg. Augie’s mother held his face against her while his dad went to John. He picked him up — John almost bawled then, but he didn’t — and carried him to a neighbor’s car, and John wore a cast the rest of the summer and a little bit into the fall.
But that wasn’t the day John bawled.

That day came after high school, in late 1942, when Mrs. Müller and Friedrich received the letters announcing the Heim ins Reich , calling German immigrants and the sons of German immigrants back from America and home to Germany. The letters came to just about every guy on Augie’s block, not long after Neil Schneider was killed. Augie didn’t really understand what they meant. Why would anyone go back to Germany? But John knew, and he took the letter he’d been sent and walked out to the curb in front of their house and burned it on 107th Street. When his neighbors saw him doing it, they came out and burned their letters too.
But no one came out of the Müller house.
The next day, John found out why.
That was the day he bawled.
Friedrich came over before supper, when John and Augie were listening to the radio news. He stood in the living room and told them. He was going back to Germany, he said.
“You can’t do that,” said John.
He was German first, Friedrich said.
“You’re American first,” said John.
His family back home needed him.
“Your family is here,” said John.
His country needed him. He had to go back to the fatherland.
“Bull,” said John.
There was a long silence — except for the radio.
Then John said, really quiet, “Suppose, when I get drafted… suppose we… we might be… ”
“Do you think I haven’t thought of that?” said Friedrich. “Do you think I haven’t?”
John didn’t say anything.
“I gotta go pack,” said Friedrich.
Nothing from John.
“Look in on my mother every so often.”
Nothing from John.
“See you, kid. Keep your nose clean,” Friedrich said to Augie.
“See you,” said Augie.
That was all.
That night, Augie heard John bawling in his bedroom.
The next morning, Friedrich left with a suitcase. No one from 107th Street came out to say goodbye.
The next Sunday, at St. Paul’s Lutheran, Mrs. Müller sat in the very back pew. She came in late. She left before holy Communion.
Augie didn’t see anyone talk with her at all.
Not even Father Fischer.

All that winter and early spring, the war was a confusion of newspaper maps with bold arrows showing which side was doing what. Augie couldn’t really follow it much, even when his father tried to explain it all — until his mother told them to stop. It was a wet spring, so not much stickball, especially since John didn’t want to play anymore, and the mood on the block had changed. Somehow, stickball didn’t seem to matter much. In P.S. 107, Miss Hatchet did the best she could for her fourth graders, but memorizing state capitals didn’t seem all that important, really. And Augie had never been all that excited about memorizing poetry, even when he had to recite it in front of his class. The war blocked out everything. It’s all anyone talked about in all the homes on 107th Street.
And soon there were more gold stars in the windows.
People would stop outside those homes and hold their hands up to their faces.
Before the war, Augie had never imagined that anyone he knew could, you know, die. Now he knew they could. The world wasn’t the same.

After church on Sundays, the neighbors of 107th Street gathered together in groups to walk slowly home. They stopped at the gold star houses up and down the street and talked for a long time, the dads holding their hats in their hands, the moms holding each other’s hands, talking quietly. Sometimes they just stood there together, because there were no words to say, but it was important to be together. Even Augie, who was usually pretty hungry after church, waited on the outside of the groups, quiet and still. Always some of the women gathered around Annalise, and held her hand too.
No one held Mrs. Müller’s hand. She was always gone before the service ended. If you saw her on the block, you looked away. If you saw her in the A & P, you moved over to the next aisle.

Everyone that Augie knew thought she deserved it. Her son Friedrich was a traitor. He wasn’t fighting for his country; he was fighting for the enemy! He had deserted his country — and probably she wanted to desert as well. Unless she was a spy!

Franzie thought for sure Mrs. Müller was a spy. Why else would she stay behind? Tommy and Timmy figured he was right. Probably she was sending coded messages to the Third Reich. Probably she was getting them information on the exact location of P.S. 107. Probably she was part of a network of spies! Maybe the ringleader! That’s why she kept her door shut all the time: to hide away from anyone who might suspect her. It’s why she came late to services at St. Paul’s. And maybe that’s why she sat in the back all the time, almost in the darkest part of the church. She was probably taking notes and radioing them back to Berlin. Franzie was sure that once, maybe twice, he had chanced upon her signal, and he’d heard her whispering American secrets to radio operators in Berlin. He was sure of it.

So everyone thought Mrs. Müller deserved to be ignored, suspected, hated — except for John. “Look in on my mother,” Friedrich had asked, and so John did. It was John who bagged her groceries for her when she shopped at the A & P. It was John who ran to catch up with her when she left St. Paul’s so he could say hi. It was John who replaced the broken gate someone had kicked in. It was John who whitewashed the brick wall to cover the red swastika someone had painted there. It was John who fixed the smashed window on the second floor.
It was John who said 107th Street wasn’t 107th Street anymore. The war had ended all that.

And it was John who told Augie one night when he came home with a shiner from a sixth grade kid who told him his brother was a traitor for being good to Mrs. Müller that you weren’t a traitor for keeping a promise, and you certainly weren’t a very good American if you broke one.
“But you didn’t promise Friedrich that you’d look in on her,” said Augie. “I was there. You didn’t promise.”
“He knew I would do it,” said John. “He asked, and that’s enough. And Augie, if I was gone and I told Friedrich to look in on Ma, do you think he’d do it?”
“You’re not gone,” Augie said.
“That’s right. But suppose I was?”
“Then I’d still be here.”
“That’s right too. But suppose the two of us were gone, would Friedrich do it?”
Augie thought for a while. “Yeah,” he finally said. “He’d do it.”
“That’s why I’m doing it,” said John.
Augie understood that, but deep down, he suddenly understood something else as well. What would happen if John was gone?
By the end of that wet spring, on a beautiful day that smelled like green grass and new maple leaves and lilies and lilacs, Augie knew he’d find out.
Two things happened on that day.
First, John got the letter from the War Department, telling him to report to the naval station in Brooklyn in two weeks.

Second, Mrs. Müller got the letter telling her that her son Friedrich had been found by Allied forces lying in a wheat field near the French border. He had been badly wounded. He had been brought to a field hospital and had given the doctor this address. The doctor regretted to say Friedrich had not survived his injuries.
Mrs. Müller had handed John the letter when he came to say goodbye. It was written in French, but they both understood enough to know that Friedrich was gone.

Two weeks later, John left for the United States Naval Station in Brooklyn. A week after that, he shipped off to Europe. In his last letter before he left, he told his folks and Augie that he couldn’t let on where he was heading, since loose lips sink ships, but he wanted them to know he loved them, that he’d stay safe, that he’d see them again once he settled the Third Reich’s hash, and that they shouldn’t worry.
At the end of the letter, he added a “P.S. August, keep my promise for me, will you?”

Augie wasn’t sure he could do that. Watching his brother walk down 107th Street on the morning he left, a duffel bag slung over his shoulder, his white uniform gleaming, passing the gold stars on the houses, Augie just wasn’t sure he could do that.
He hated Friedrich, who was, if nothing else, one of the reasons John was leaving.

Weeks went by. They heard from John about once a week or so, in letters blacked out by the censors. They figured he’d been in London, since he mentioned Big Ben. But everything else was pretty general. He hoped everyone on 107th Street was doing okay. How was Mrs. Müller? he asked.

Augie didn’t know. Since the day she’d received the letter from the French doctor, Mrs. Müller had not been to St. Paul’s German Lutheran Church. Not once. Augie didn’t think about her much, though. He had a whole new reason for figuring out the maps in the newspaper and following the news from the front. When he paid his two bits on Saturdays to go to the movies, it wasn’t the feature he cared about. It was the newsreels showing scenes from the battlefields. Augie sat as far forward as he could, hoping that somehow he’d get lucky and see a picture of John. He never did — but he did see the war, up close, the horrible explosions, the tanks, the fields overrun, the cities bombed, the American soldiers being carried in stretchers, still with their helmets on, but not moving. Not even a Buster Crabbe serial episode could stop him from shaking — and hating the Germans who might be throwing grenades at his brother, for all he knew.

Augie dreaded the next gold star that might come to a window of a house on 107th Street. When he looked at the front windows of his own house, he could almost imagine it there, and he’d turn away — otherwise he might bawl.

So when Mrs. Müller’s gate got knocked down again, Augie didn’t go over. It lay on the ground for weeks. The next broken window on the second floor got a sheet of plywood. The next swastika got washed off, sort of — but you could still see the crooked red lines against the whitewash.

But when June came around and fourth grade was almost done, Augie remembered how the thirteenth of the month was Friedrich’s birthday, and how he used to make such a big deal about being six months older than John. He remembered how they used to give each other gag gifts: a box full of chameleons, a fake baseball card with made-up stats for each other’s favorite players (Joe Gordon for Friedrich, Charlie Keller for John), training wheels for their bikes as if they were babies, stuff like that. He remembered how they laughed when they opened them.

So maybe Augie wasn’t surprised that on June 13th, Friedrich’s birthday, his mother came back to St. Paul’s German Lutheran Church. She came back to where she and Friedrich had always sat: left side, a little more than halfway forward. Augie watched. No one talked to her. No one sat near her. She didn’t seem to care. She sat there, almost as if Friedrich were beside her. Almost as if she could touch him. She sat for the greetings and announcements. She stood for the hymns. She knelt for the prayers. And the whole time, Augie watched. He could not figure out why exactly he wanted to bawl.
And when the time came for holy Communion, Augie got up with his mother and father and stood on the line for the right side. And Mrs. Müller got up and stood on the line for the left side.
But no one got in line behind her.
Everyone on the left side of St. Paul’s moved to the line on the right side.
No one looked at Mrs. Müller — except Augie.

He watched. He watched. She kept her head straight. She did not turn. She walked slowly at the end of her slow line, heading for Father Fischer and the wine he held in the chalice of St. Paul. The chalice that no one wanted to put their lips to after she would have touched it.

The newsreels played in Augie’s head. The bombs, the explosions. The shattered buildings. The wounded men on stretchers. The wounded men who all looked alike. The wounded men who could have been his brother John.
And then he looked at Mrs. Müller, who had come to St. Paul’s German Lutheran Church on the birthday of her son, who had once been carried off a field in France in a stretcher, and didn’t make it.
Augie’s heart clenched — and then released. He didn’t know how to describe it any other way. His heart sort of released.

He got out of line from behind his mother and father and began to cross over to the other side of the church, through the close pews, across the center aisle, toward the left side. His mother saw. His father too. They did not stop him. Perhaps they couldn’t have stopped him. Augie came to the left line. He moved up to the end and stood behind Mrs. Müller, who turned and looked at him. Augie held his hand out, and she took it.
They held each other’s hands all the way to the front of St. Paul’s German Lutheran Church, where they took holy Communion.
Together.

articles.title: Immunity
articles.author: Kim Roberts
articles.description: A speaker describes the T-cells in their body.
articles.intro: Kim Roberts is the author of five books of poetry, most recently The Scientific Method (WordTech Editions, 2017). In this poem, a speaker describes T-cells, important "defender" blood cells that are critical to the body's immune responses.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the speaker portrays T-cells.
articles.permissions: Reprinted from The Scientific Method (WordTech Editions, 2017) by Kim Roberts ( http://www.kimroberts.org ), with permission of the author.

blocks.text: I don’t really understand T-cells
but when my doctor describes them,
I imagine T’s posted at intervals
in my bloodstream, like sentries
at a crenellated wall.
Are they T-shaped?
Or is T merely the first initial
of a long, inscrutable, Latin word?
I see a line of cells holding shields
emblazoned with Ts.
I hear their rhythmic chant,
T - T - T - T - T,
as they fall into formation
for their regular bloodstream drills.

options.label: T-cells are the body's most important defense against danger.
Imagining defensive soldiers in the bloodstream can help the body heal.
It's difficult for humans to comprehend how their body works.
T-cells act as defensive soldiers to protect the body from harm.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following expresses the main idea of the poem?

options.label: "I don't really understand T-cells / but when my doctor describes them" (Line 1-2)
"in my bloodstream, like sentries / at a crenellated wall." (Line 4-5)
"Or is T merely the first initial / of a long, inscrutable, Latin word?" (Line 7-8)
"emblazoned with Ts. / I hear their rhythmic chant" (Line 10-11)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: It prompts the speaker to come up with a mental image of T-cells.
It causes the speaker to feel confusion about what T-cells do.
It makes the speaker interested in learning more about their body.
It makes the speaker feel impressed by what the body can do.

questions.prompt: How does hearing about T-cells from the doctor affect the speaker?
How does the poet's word choice to describe T-cells contribute to the meaning of the poem?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the poem, the speaker compares T-cells to soldiers. How can poetry help us understand confusing scientific topics? If you were going to create a simile or metaphor that describes the importance of skin and muscles to the human body, what would it be?

In the poem, the speaker describes how T-cells can protect the body. Think about other components of the body that are designed to help protect it. What do those components do? What are some habits that you can have to help the body be protected?
How does the speaker portray T-cells confronting things that might threaten the body? Besides allowing your T-cells to do their job, how else can you protect yourself from getting sick?

articles.title: Languages
articles.author: Carl Sandburg
articles.description: Sandburg laments the fleeting nature of language in the context of human history.
articles.intro: Carl Sandburg (1878-1967) was an American poet and writer who won three Pulitzer Prizes in his lifetime. "Languages" was published in Chicago Poems in 1916 alongside a collection of poems that pay tribute to the common man.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Sandburg's use of figurative language and how they contribute to the poem's message.
articles.permissions: Languages by Carl Sandburg is in the public domain.

blocks.text: THERE are no handles upon a language
Whereby men take hold of it
And mark it with signs for its remembrance.
It is a river, this language,
Once in a thousand years
Breaking a new course
Changing its way to the ocean.
It is mountain effluvia
Moving to valleys
And from nation to nation
Crossing borders and mixing.
Languages die like rivers.
Words wrapped round your tongue today
And broken to shape of thought
Between your teeth and lips speaking
Now and today
Shall be faded hieroglyphics
Ten thousand years from now.
Sing—and singing—remember
Your song dies and changes
And is not here to-morrow
Any more than the wind
Blowing ten thousand years ago.

options.label: All languages, like everything, will eventually die out and cease to be.
Preservation of language is key through proper education.
Identity is strongly informed by what language or languages one speaks.
Progress means creating and enforcing one universal language.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following best describes a central theme of the text?

options.label: "Whereby men take hold of it / And mark it with signs for its remembrance." (Lines 2-3)
"It is mountain effluvia / Moving to valleys /And from nation to nation / Crossing borders and mixing." (Lines 8-11)
"It is a river, this language, / Once in a thousand years / Breaking a new course / Changing its way to the ocean." (Lines 4-7)
"Your song dies and changes / And is not here to-morrow / Any more than the wind / Blowing ten thousand years ago." (Lines 20-23)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which of the following quotes best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: The speaker compares language to "mountain effluvia" to depict language as a sort of plague, diffusing into different places and cultures, making them forget their more primal roots.
The speaker compares language to "mountain effluvia" to describe language as in a state of decay, giving off an unpleasant scent.
The speaker compares language to "mountain effluvia" because he dislikes the changing untouchability of language (hence the comparison to an unpleasant odor).
The speaker compares language to "mountain effluvia" to depict language as diffusing and mixing into different places and cultures, molding into different languages all together.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best explains the comparison between language and "mountain effluvia" in Line 8?

options.label: The poem is written as a lyric poem, echoing its comparison of language to singing and thus contributing to its message regarding the beauty of language.
The poem is written in free verse, a modern style, contributing to the poem's message about social progress.
The poem is written in free verse, echoing the poem's message about changing language and structure.
The poem is written in free verse, which flows naturally like the river or the wind; it therefore contributes to its message about preserving nature.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best explains how the poem's form develops its message?
How do the references to time contribute to the overall meaning of the poem?

discussionQuestions.prompt: According to Sandburg, if language, "dies and changes," what is the purpose of language?
Choose two examples of metaphor in which the speaker connects nature to language. Why do you think the speaker uses nature to explain language?
The speaker talks about the "mountain effluvia," and how it mixes as it moves through the nation. How is this like language?
Hieroglyphics were used ancient Egyptians to communicate and record ideas. How does this form of communicating compare to how we communicate today?

How does the theme of death emerge in this poem? In the context of this poem, how do people face death? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

articles.title: The Song of The Shirt
articles.author: Thomas Hood
articles.description: This poem depicts the inhuman working conditions for England's poor.
articles.intro: Thomas Hood (1799-1845) was an English poet who is often remembered for his poem "The Song of the Shirt." In this poem, Hood writes about the inhumane working conditions of England's poor.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the effect of Hood’s word choice and figurative language on the poem's overall message.
articles.permissions: The Song of The Shirt by Thomas Hood is in the public domain.

blocks.text: With fingers weary and worn,
With eyelids heavy and red,
A woman sat in unwomanly rags,
Plying her needle and thread—
Stitch! stitch! stitch!
In poverty, hunger, and dirt,
And still with a voice of dolorous pitch
She sang the “Song of the Shirt.”
“Work! work! work!
While the cock is crowing aloof!
And work—work—work,
Till the stars shine through the roof!
It’s O! to be a slave
Along with the barbarous Turk,
Where woman has never a soul to save,
If this is Christian work!
“Work—work—work,
Till the brain begins to swim;
Work—work—work,
Till the eyes are heavy and dim!
Seam, and gusset, and band,
Band, and gusset, and seam,
Till over the buttons I fall asleep,
And sew them on in a dream!
“O, men, with sisters dear!
O, men, with mothers and wives!
It is not linen you’re wearing out,
But human creatures’ lives!
Stitch—stitch—stitch,
In poverty, hunger and dirt,
Sewing at once, with a double thread,
A Shroud as well as a Shirt.
“But why do I talk of death?
That phantom of grisly bone,
I hardly fear his terrible shape,
It seems so like my own—
It seems so like my own,
Because of the fasts I keep;
Oh, God! that bread should be so dear.
And flesh and blood so cheap!
“Work—work—work!
My labour never flags;
And what are its wages? A bed of straw,
A crust of bread—and rags.
That shattered roof—this naked floor—
A table—a broken chair—
And a wall so blank, my shadow I thank
For sometimes falling there!
“Work—work—work!
From weary chime to chime,
Work—work—work,
As prisoners work for crime!
Band, and gusset, and seam,
Seam, and gusset, and band,
Till the heart is sick, and the brain benumbed,
As well as the weary hand.
“Work—work—work,
In the dull December light,
And work—work—work,
When the weather is warm and bright—
While underneath the eaves
The brooding swallows cling
As if to show me their sunny backs
And twit me with the spring.
“O! but to breathe the breath
Of the cowslip and primrose sweet—
With the sky above my head,
And the grass beneath my feet;
For only one short hour
To feel as I used to feel,
Before I knew the woes of want
And the walk that costs a meal!
“O! but for one short hour!
A respite however brief!
No blessed leisure for Love or hope,
But only time for grief!
A little weeping would ease my heart,
But in their briny bed
My tears must stop, for every drop
Hinders needle and thread!”
With fingers weary and worn,
With eyelids heavy and red,
A woman sat in unwomanly rags,
Plying her needle and thread—
Stitch! stitch! stitch!
In poverty, hunger, and dirt,
And still with a voice of dolorous pitch,—
Would that its tone could reach the Rich!—
She sang this “Song of the Shirt!”

options.label: Death is rampant among the working poor.
The lives and working conditions of the poor are inhumane and should be improved.
Industrial progress improves the lives of thousands, at the cost of a minority.
Fairness and financial equality is an unrealistic ideal.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following best describes a central theme of the text?

options.label: "Work—work—work, / Till the brain begins to swim; / Work—work—work, / Till the eyes are heavy and dim!" (Lines 17-20)

"A woman sat in unwomanly rags, / Plying her needle and thread— / Stitch! stitch! stitch! / In poverty, hunger, and dirt, / And still with a voice of dolorous pitch,— / Would that its tone could reach the Rich!— / She sang this "Song of the Shirt!"" (Lines 83-89)

articles.title: Stories Saved My Life, and That's Why I Write for Young People
articles.author: James Bird
articles.description: James Bird describes his journey growing up and feeling "different."
articles.intro: James Bird is a screenwriter, director, and author. As a Californian native of Ojibwe descent, Bird grew up feeling "different."
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the trajectory of Bird’s life and what impacted the decisions that he made.
articles.permissions: This essay was originally published on the We Need Diverse Books blog here . It has been republished with permission from WNDB and the author.

blocks.text: When we say someone is smart, what does that mean? Does it mean they performed well in school? If it does, then I’m the opposite of smart. I was awful in school. It wasn’t because I didn’t know how to read or write, or that I didn’t understand the concept of math. I just couldn’t learn the way my teachers wanted me to. I was different, and none of my schools had time for different.

What made me different? Was I a product of my environment? Maybe. I lived in poverty and bounced around from school to school after each eviction sent my family on the search for another apartment and my single mother in pursuit of another job. Learning in those circumstances would be difficult for any kid. But did that make me not smart? Sure, I wasn’t an honor roll student, but none of the other kids knew about section 8, HUD housing, food stamps, hotel vouchers, and how to pack everything you own into two black trash bags in under thirty minutes while the sheriff tossed your furniture out. I knew these things. This qualifies as smart, doesn’t it?

But that was life outside of my brain. What was going on inside was the other reason people thought there was something wrong with me. The doctors explained it like this: If everyone has a sack of water around their brain, and lightning strikes, the water absorbs the blow and allows the brain to only receive a small charge of electricity, letting you learn safely and normally. My brain was made without the sack surrounding it. I take the lightning — information coming in — head on. I learn from each strike, but not the way others learn new things or process information. This meant that I dissected what I heard or read, seeing how many other words I could make from various words. I’d then play with the letters, trading vowels with words in the next sentence to make new words.

This made reading very difficult and reading aloud nearly impossible. The teachers didn’t know what to do with me. I’d hand in assignments that had nothing to do with the lesson, but every letter from each question was used to create a story. I’d hand in my story and hope the teacher enjoyed it, but I always failed. I was always placed in the back of the classroom, the place for students the teachers had given up on. I was allowed to hand in my silly stories and I managed to pass with Ds.
But I loved words. I collected them to make stories. Always stories. I had to tell stories.

It got worse in high school (or better, depending on how you look at it). I was failing all of my classes. I was reprimanded constantly for not paying attention, for not focusing, and for fooling around. But I was paying attention, just not the way they expected. I was different.

Being reminded I was different wasn’t new to me. We were the poor family. I was the kid who didn’t have a dad. I was the kid who wore the same pants every day. And we were always the only Native American family anyone knew. And in school, we were taught that Native Americans were the bad guys. The savages. The ungrateful killers who refused the bible and scalped all the innocent white people who just wanted to flee their oppressive homelands and make a better life for their families.

This was my identity I was given. I was a poor Native American boy with no dad and oh, yeah, there was also something wrong with my brain. That’s who I was to people on the outside, but that’s not who I ever was. Not really. I was different, yes, but I didn’t yet know that didn’t mean I was un-teachable.

Throughout high school, the lightning strikes and my grades got worse. I was sent to doctors and put on pills. These pills were intended to calm the storm inside my brain. At this time, I was frequently having seizures. I’d wake up in a hospital completely humiliated, knowing everyone at school would be talking about how I snapped during class, started spitting, and was flopping on the floor like a fish out of water. It was so embarrassing. I even quit playing basketball, where I was my school’s MVP, because of the fear of having another seizure on the court, in front of everyone.

I found myself depressed for the first time in my life. I didn’t want to live anymore if it meant worrying that my next trip to the hospital could happen at any moment. I stopped sports, I stopped hanging out with my friends, and I didn’t have anyone to talk to about this weird condition. All I had left is words. So I kept writing my stories.

I was taught how to tell stories by my mom. Her wildly imaginative tales always made me feel better. And that’s what I needed, to feel better. I plunged headfirst into storytelling. I became obsessed with comics, movies, and books. I didn’t have a dad in real life, but I chose to no longer live in the real world. I took all the life lessons a father bestows onto his son from Lawrence Fishburne in Boyz N da Hood . He was my dad. Tom Sellick in Quigley Down Under taught me to always fight for the oppressed; he was my dad too. Wes Studi in Geronimo taught me to never give up. Johnny Depp in Edward Scissorhands taught me it’s okay to be different. I had the coolest dads. I was happy again, all because of stories. They saved my life.

Around that time, my mom became fed up with doctors and meds, and told me, “Maybe you’re not the one with the problem. Maybe everyone else is.” And I chose to believe her. I kept watching movies and reading books, and finally understood my desire to tell stories. But what was my story?

For that answer, I had to know where I came from; who was in my blood. So, I dropped out of high school and got two jobs in one mall. I worked nonstop for a couple of months, enough to help Mom with rent while I was away, and then I left. With one backpack stuffed full of clothes, I moved to Minnesota. Where my mom grew up. Home to our people, The Ojibwe.

It was there where I reconnected with my blood. And although Minnesota had many more trees, lakes, and friendlier people than California, not everything was different. My relatives were still poor. Life was still hard. But something in the cold air made sense to me. It was there where I realized something that shaped the rest of my life: Being different isn’t just okay, it’s awesome. Finally, I felt free. I didn’t have to learn the ways they wanted me to. I didn’t have to be who they wanted me to be. I could finally just be me.
I realized my Ojibwe people didn’t learn how to live their lives in a classroom. They learned differently. And that way was my way. Through storytelling. It’s in my blood. It’s in my DNA.

I knew what I had to do. My purpose had been staring me in the face the entire time. I went back home to California. I re-enrolled for my senior year in high school, and immediately went to speak to a guidance counselor. And by luck, I landed in Mrs. Wilkin’s office. She was an odd white lady known for keeping bugs as pets in her classrooms.

She looked over my grades and I shared with her the epiphany I‘d had in Minnesota. That day, she told me something I will never forget: “I’ve never said this to a student before, and I doubt I ever will again, but school is not for you. You need to listen to that blood, and move to Hollywood so you can tell the world your stories.”
And that’s what I did.

But for my storytelling plan to work, I needed to be smart. Strategic. Clever. And well prepared. I decided not to master one medium of storytelling but to study all mediums. That way, once I found my favorite form, I’d pursue that, but still know a little about everything.

I thrust myself into acting classes, fashion design school, creative writing workshops, photography, music, and even painting. This was all so that when I got on set, I’d be able to know what I’m talking about to whoever I’m talking to.

Years went by and it was all going according to plan. I traveled the country with photography. I got my AA in Fashion Design. I got small acting roles here and there. I wrote scripts after scripts and I finally began directing. I became my own teacher and student. I let my blood run through me. I no longer felt like I had learning disabilities. I began to feel that my brain, which I once hated and begged to be normal, was in fact extraordinary. And this happiness led me to my wife, Adriana Mather. She was the first person, besides my mom, to love my brain. And together, we set out to embark on a new medium of storytelling; writing books. And here I am now, writing Native American stories and living with my beautiful wife and son, Wolf.

I asked above what it means to say someone is smart. I don’t think it has anything to do with their grades or how quickly they catch on to new lessons or assignments. A smart person knows who they are and knows what they want. And if you don’t know who you are yet and don’t know yet what you want, that doesn’t mean you’re not smart, it just means you’re still learning.

Another word for that is living. We learn every day. For some it’s calculus, for others like me, it’s storytelling. And if you’re someone with a brain that doesn’t quite fit in the way everyone says it’s supposed to, just think about this: If someone with a ‘different’ brain was there when the Constitution was being written, and when it came to the part that “All Men Are Created Equal,” maybe someone would have had fun with the letters and switched out the C for a T. Think of how ‘different’ this country would be today. We need to be treated as equals. We need different brains. We need to tell our stories. We need you.

articles.title: Organized Labor and The Great Upheaval
articles.author: USHistory.org
articles.description: At the turn of the 20th century, the Great Upheaval inspired workers to advocate for their rights.
articles.intro: The American economy boomed during the Gilded Age in the mid to late 19th century. However, workers didn't reap the benefits and suffered from poor working conditions and few rights. During the Great Upheaval, laborers spontaneously started to unite to demand change at the turn of the 20th century.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the Great Upheaval inspired the organized labor movement in the US.
articles.permissions: "Organized Labor and The Great Upheaval" from USHistory.org. Copyright © 2016 by USHistory.org. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: In the mid-19th century, the vast majority of American work was still done on the farm. By the turn of the 20th century, the United States economy revolved around the factory.

Most Americans living in the Gilded Age knew nothing of the millions of Rockefeller, Carnegie and Morgan. They worked 10 hour shifts, 6 days a week, for wages barely enough to survive. Children as young as eight years old worked hours that kept them out of school. Men and women worked until their bodies could stand no more, only to be released from employment without retirement benefits. Medical coverage did not exist. Women who became pregnant were often fired. Compensation for being hurt while on the job was zero.
Come Together

Soon laborers realized that they must unite to demand change. Even though they lacked money, education, or political power, they knew one critical thing. There were simply more workers than there were owners.

Unions did not emerge overnight. Despite their legal rights to exist, bosses often took extreme measures, including intimidation and violence, to prevent a union from taking hold. Workers, too, often chose the sword when peaceful measures failed.

Many Americans believed that a violent revolution would take place in America. How long would so many stand to be poor? Industrial titans including John Rockefeller arranged for mighty castles to be built as fortresses to stand against the upheaval they were sure was coming.

Slowly but surely unions did grow. Efforts to form nationwide organizations faced even greater difficulties. Federal troops were sometimes called to block their efforts. Judges almost always ruled in favor of the bosses.

The workers often could not agree on common goals. Some flirted with extreme ideas like Marxism. Others simply wanted a nickel more per hour. Fights erupted over whether or not to admit women or African Americans. Immigrants were often viewed with hostile eyes. Most did agree on one major issue — the eight-hour day. But even that agreement was often not strong enough glue to hold the group together.

Organized labor has brought tremendous positive change to working Americans. Today, many workers enjoy higher wages, better hours, and safer working conditions. Employers often pay for medical coverage and several weeks vacation. Jobs and lives were lost in the epic struggle for a fair share. The fight sprouted during the Gilded Age, when labor took its first steps toward unity. It began with the Great Upheaval.
The Great Upheaval

It started with a 10% pay cut. When leaders of the Baltimore and Ohio Railroad Company ordered this second reduction in less than eight months, railroad workers in Martinsburg, West Virginia decided they had had enough. On July 16, 1877, workers in that town drove all the engines into the roundhouse and boldly declared that no train would leave until the owners restored their pay. The local townspeople gathered at the railyard to show their support for the strikers. A great showdown was on.

Strikes or other actions seen as disturbances are usually handled at the local level. The mayor of Martinsburg tried in vain to threaten the striking workers, but the crowd merely laughed and booed. The local police were far too insubstantial to match the numbers of the rabble. In desperation, the mayor turned to the governor of West Virginia for support. The governor sent units of the National Guard to Martinsburg to accompany the trains out of town by force of arms. There was little support for the effort among the Guardsmen, however, because a majority of them were railroad workers themselves. After two people were killed in the standoff, the Guard simply lay down their weapons and began chatting with members of the crowd.
Only when federal troops sent by President Hayes arrived did the trains leave the station. Even then they were sabotaged and harassed along their routes. Only one train reached its destination.
The Strike Spreads

The Martinsburg strike might have gone down in history as one of many small local strikes put down by force, but this time the strike spread. Soon other B & O units joined the Martinsburg strike. The movement spread into Pennsylvania, when workers on the Pennsylvania and Reading railroads joined their compatriots. Pittsburgh is the gateway to the Midwest, and so the strike widened to that region.

The police, the National Guard, and the United States Army clashed with angry mobs throughout America. Throughout the land, wealthy individuals feared that the worst had finally come. A violent revolution seemed to be sweeping the nation.

But then it stopped. In some cases the strikes were ended by force. In others, the strikers simply gave up. After all, most workers were not trying to overthrow the government or the social order. They simply wanted higher wages and more time to spend with their families. The Great Upheaval was not the first strike in American History; it was the first mass strike to involve so many different workers separated by so much space.
What Did This Mean for America?

Was it successful? From a distance, it seems to have failed. However, in many cases, workers did have their demands met. There is no telling how many future pay cuts were avoided because of fear of reprisal from the laborers. The Great Upheaval was spontaneous. There was absolutely no advanced planning, showing how many rank and file workers had the same concerns about quality of life, as well as the same anger at those who controlled the wealth. More than 100,000 workers had gone on strike, shutting down nearly half of the nation's rail systems.

When the strike ended in the first week in August, over 100 people were killed and a thousand more were imprisoned. Untold millions of dollars of damage was caused to rail lines, cars, and roundhouses. The fight was over, but America had not seen the last of the mass strike.

options.label: The Great Upheaval was a single strike by railroad workers in West Virginia who refused to continue working until they received their pay.
The Great Upheaval was the largest mass strike of laborers across industries in the U.S. and it empowered laborers to advocate for their rights.
The Great Upheaval changed the power structure of laborers and bosses in America and the powerful leaders lost their monopoly over their workers.
The Great Upheaval was the largest mass strike of laborers across industries in the US, but the movement failed and didn't have an impact on the rights of laborers.

articles.title: The First Time Percy Came Back
articles.author: Mary Oliver
articles.description: A speaker imagines reuniting with a beloved pet who passed away.
articles.intro: Mary Oliver (1935-2019) was an American poet who won the National Book Award in 1992 and the Pulitzer Prize in 1984. Today, she is regarded as one of America's most famous and bestselling modern poets. In this poem, the speaker writes about her dog Percy.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Percy’s dialogue affects the tone of the poem.
articles.permissions: "The First Time Percy Came Back" by Mary Oliver. Reprinted by the permission of The Charlotte Sheedy Literary Agency as agent for the author. Copyright © 2012, 2013 by Mary Oliver with permission of Bill Reichblum.

blocks.text: The first time Percy came back
he was not sailing on a cloud.
He was loping along the sand as though
he had come a great way.
“Percy,” I cried out, and reached to him —
those white curls —
but he was unreachable. As music
is present yet you can’t touch it.
“Yes, it’s all different,” he said.
“You’re going to be very surprised.”
But I wasn’t thinking of that. I only
wanted to hold him. “Listen,” he said,
“I miss that too.
And now you’ll be telling stories
of my coming back
and they won’t be false, and they won’t be true,
but they’ll be real.”
And then, as he used to, he said, “Let’s go!”
And we walked down the beach together.

articles.title: The Story of David and Goliath
articles.author: The Book of Samuel
articles.description: In this classic story about the triumph of an underdog, a young shepherd defeats a giant warrior.
articles.intro: In the Judea-Christian tradition, the famous story of David and Goliath is taken from the first book of Samuel, chapter 17; the following translation has been taken from the New International Version Bible . In the story, the young and small shepherd David takes up the giant enemy warrior Goliath's challenge for battle in a true underdog fashion.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the story’s central ideas and our understanding of where power and strength come from.
articles.permissions: The Story of David and Goliath by The Book of Samuel is in the public domain.

blocks.text: Now the Philistines gathered their forces for war and assembled at Sokoh in Judah. They pitched camp at Ephes Dammim, between Sokoh and Azekah. Saul and the Israelites assembled and camped in the Valley of Elah and drew up their battle line to meet the Philistines. The Philistines occupied one hill and the Israelites another, with the valley between them.

A champion named Goliath, who was from Gath, came out of the Philistine camp. His height was six cubits and a span. He had a bronze helmet on his head and wore a coat of scale armor of bronze weighing five thousand shekels; on his legs he wore bronze greaves, and a bronze javelin was slung on his back. His spear shaft was like a weaver’s rod, and its iron point weighed six hundred shekels. His shield bearer went ahead of him.

Goliath stood and shouted to the ranks of Israel, “Why do you come out and line up for battle? Am I not a Philistine, and are you not the servants of Saul? Choose a man and have him come down to me. If he is able to fight and kill me, we will become your subjects; but if I overcome him and kill him, you will become our subjects and serve us.” Then the Philistine said, “This day I defy the armies of Israel! Give me a man and let us fight each other.” On hearing the Philistine’s words, Saul and all the Israelites were dismayed and terrified.

Now David was the son of an Ephrathite named Jesse, who was from Bethlehem in Judah. Jesse had eight sons, and in Saul’s time he was very old. Jesse’s three oldest sons had followed Saul to the war: The firstborn was Eliab; the second, Abinadab; and the third, Shammah. David was the youngest. The three oldest followed Saul, but David went back and forth from Saul to tend his father’s sheep at Bethlehem.
For forty days the Philistine came forward every morning and evening and took his stand.

Now Jesse said to his son David, “Take this ephah of roasted grain and these ten loaves of bread for your brothers and hurry to their camp. Take along these ten cheeses to the commander of their unit. See how your brothers are and bring back some assurance from them. They are with Saul and all the men of Israel in the Valley of Elah, fighting against the Philistines.”

Early in the morning David left the flock in the care of a shepherd, loaded up and set out, as Jesse had directed. He reached the camp as the army was going out to its battle positions, shouting the war cry. Israel and the Philistines were drawing up their lines facing each other. David left his things with the keeper of supplies, ran to the battle lines and asked his brothers how they were. As he was talking with them, Goliath, the Philistine champion from Gath, stepped out from his lines and shouted his usual defiance, and David heard it. Whenever the Israelites saw the man, they all fled from him in great fear.

Now the Israelites had been saying, “Do you see how this man keeps coming out? He comes out to defy Israel. The king will give great wealth to the man who kills him. He will also give him his daughter in marriage and will exempt his family from taxes in Israel.”

David asked the men standing near him, “What will be done for the man who kills this Philistine and removes this disgrace from Israel? Who is this Philistine that he should defy the armies of the living God?”
They repeated to him what they had been saying and told him, “This is what will be done for the man who kills him.”

When Eliab, David’s oldest brother, heard him speaking with the men, he burned with anger at him and asked, “Why have you come down here? And with whom did you leave those few sheep in the wilderness? I know how conceited you are and how wicked your heart is; you came down only to watch the battle.”

“Now what have I done?” said David. “Can’t I even speak?” He then turned away to someone else and brought up the same matter, and the men answered him as before. What David said was overheard and reported to Saul, and Saul sent for him.
David said to Saul, “Let no one lose heart on account of this Philistine; your servant will go and fight him.”
Saul replied, “You are not able to go out against this Philistine and fight him; you are only a young man, and he has been a warrior from his youth.”

But David said to Saul, “Your servant has been keeping his father’s sheep. When a lion or a bear came and carried off a sheep from the flock, I went after it, struck it and rescued the sheep from its mouth. When it turned on me, I seized it by its hair, struck it and killed it. Your servant has killed both the lion and the bear; this uncircumcised Philistine will be like one of them, because he has defied the armies of the living God. The Lord who rescued me from the paw of the lion and the paw of the bear will rescue me from the hand of this Philistine.”
Saul said to David, “Go, and the Lord be with you.”

Then Saul dressed David in his own tunic. He put a coat of armor on him and a bronze helmet on his head. David fastened on his sword over the tunic and tried walking around, because he was not used to them.

“I cannot go in these,” he said to Saul, “because I am not used to them.” So he took them off. Then he took his staff in his hand, chose five smooth stones from the stream, put them in the pouch of his shepherd’s bag and, with his sling in his hand, approached the Philistine.

Meanwhile, the Philistine, with his shield bearer in front of him, kept coming closer to David. He looked David over and saw that he was little more than a boy, glowing with health and handsome, and he despised him. He said to David, “Am I a dog, that you come at me with sticks?” And the Philistine cursed David by his gods. “Come here,” he said, “and I’ll give your flesh to the birds and the wild animals!”

David said to the Philistine, “You come against me with sword and spear and javelin, but I come against you in the name of the Lord Almighty, the God of the armies of Israel, whom you have defied. This day the Lord will deliver you into my hands, and I’ll strike you down and cut off your head. This very day I will give the carcasses of the Philistine army to the birds and the wild animals, and the whole world will know that there is a God in Israel. All those gathered here will know that it is not by sword or spear that the Lord saves; for the battle is the Lord’s, and he will give all of you into our hands.”

As the Philistine moved closer to attack him, David ran quickly toward the battle line to meet him. Reaching into his bag and taking out a stone, he slung it and struck the Philistine on the forehead. The stone sank into his forehead, and he fell facedown on the ground.
So David triumphed over the Philistine with a sling and a stone; without a sword in his hand he struck down the Philistine and killed him.
David ran and stood over him. He took hold of the Philistine’s sword and drew it from the sheath. After he killed him, he cut off his head with the sword.

When the Philistines saw that their hero was dead, they turned and ran. Then the men of Israel and Judah surged forward with a shout and pursued the Philistines to the entrance of Gath and to the gates of Ekron. Their dead were strewn along the Shaaraim road to Gath and Ekron. When the Israelites returned from chasing the Philistines, they plundered their camp.
David took the Philistine’s head and brought it to Jerusalem; he put the Philistine’s weapons in his own tent.
As Saul watched David going out to meet the Philistine, he said to Abner, commander of the army, “Abner, find out whose son this young man is.”
As soon as David returned from killing the Philistine, Abner took him and brought him before Saul, with David still holding the Philistine’s head.
“Whose son are you, young man?” Saul asked him.
David said, “I am the son of your servant Jesse of Bethlehem.”

articles.title: Marshlands
articles.author: E. Pauline Johnson
articles.description: Indigenous poet E. Pauline Johnson describes how dusk serenely settles on the marshland.
articles.intro: Emily Pauline Johnson was an important Indigenous and Canadian poet, author, and performer whose work was popular in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. In this poem, the speaker describes a marshland — an area of land where water covers the ground for long periods of time.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the marshland is described.
articles.permissions: "Marshlands" originally appeared in The White Wampum (Copp Clark Co., 1895).

blocks.text: A thin wet sky, that yellows at the rim,
And meets with sun-lost lip the marsh’s brim.
The pools low lying, dank with moss and mould,
Glint through their mildews like large cups of gold.
Among the wild rice in the still lagoon,
In monotone the lizard shrills his tune.
The wild goose, homing, seeks a sheltering,
Where rushes grow, and oozing lichens cling.
Late cranes with heavy wing, and lazy flight,
Sail up the silence with the nearing night.
And like a spirit, swathed in some soft veil,
Steals twilight and its shadows o’er the swale.
Hushed lie the sedges, and the vapours creep,
Thick, grey and humid, while the marshes sleep.
