articles.title: Gyroscopes
articles.author: Susan Muaddi Darraj
articles.description: An Arab American teen deals with painful feelings when she confronts racism in an unexpected place.
articles.intro: Susan Muaddi Darraj won the American Book Award for her short story collection, A Curious Land . Her chapter book series, Farah Rocks , is the first to feature a Palestinian American protagonist. She lives and teaches in Baltimore. In this story, an Arab-American teenager deals with painful feelings when she confronts racism in an unexpected place.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Layla’s thoughts about her interactions with other characters in the story.
articles.permissions: "Gyroscopes" by Susan Darraj. Copyright © 2022 by Susan Darraj. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-ND 4.0 license.

blocks.text: The cheering and clapping in the gymatorium is so loud that the wooden bleachers feel fragile, like the whole upper class is going to tumble to the floor in a heap. I’m in the middle of the row, so there is no way out for me without climbing over dozens of knees and ankles.
I stay put, quieting my heart.
It’s the fall pep rally, and it will be over in thirty minutes. Besides, the school play will be announced at the end and no way I’m missing that.
So yeah, I can survive thirty minutes.
Probably.

My friend, Lauren, cranes her neck and tells me Samantha is on the gym floor, leading a new cheer. That’s when everyone starts stomping their feet on the bleachers, joining in. I check my phone to distract myself and see an email from Dr. Bledsoe, my AP Physics and second-favorite teacher.
“Layla, I made a plan for the gyroscope,” he says. “Come by this week.”
“Will do,” I tap back, just as a roar echoes across the whole gymatorium.

The basketball team has trotted out onto the floor. They’re doing some fancy dribbling, and the varsity cheerleaders follow with a routine that is so haram it almost makes me blush. Even Principal McKenzie looks nervous. Samantha is dancing in a pathetic way, her pom-poms on her thighs, an intense expression on her face. Her hips move wildly like the gyroscope Bledsoe wants me to help him build. I mean, there’s stability, but it looks like it’s going to spin off its axis at any minute.
“I guess she’s trying to twerk,” Lauren scoffs.

We both crack up laughing but get quiet when McKenz stands up. He makes a few announcements, thanks all the coaches, and then adjusts his glasses on his oily nose – I can seriously see the shine from here – before he introduces Mr. Davison.

Lauren and I clap and root for him. Three things to know about D – 1) he looks like Andre the Giant, but with a more expressive face; 2) he’s our Drama Club advisor; and 3) he’s my #1 favorite teacher.

D glides theatrically to the podium, right under the basketball hoop. He’s wearing a long blue cape pulled tightly around his body. It even covers his face. He’s so big he could probably just pick up McKenz and drop him right into the net.

I’m wondering what D is up to now when the sound system booms with a loud clash of cymbals. A familiar song starts up -- Dat-dat-dat WAH WAH . Dat-dat-dat WAH WAH -- and everyone recognizes it all at once.
“Oh no,” I mumble. Cheers erupt around me.

As if to confirm it, D does a dramatic twirl and flips open the cape, and everyone screams. His face is painted blue. But of course, because it’s D, there’s more. He reaches under the cape and pulls out a white and gold turban and sticks it on his head.

“You know it! You know it!” he shouts jovially into the microphone. “Hold on to your sandals! We’ll be putting on a magical production of Aladdin !” Samantha shakes her pom-poms wildly. McKenz and his shiny nose start swaying awkwardly from side to side. Later, I’ll realize that the man was actually trying to dance.
My heart thuds out of control.

After school, Lauren and I go to McDonald’s and each buy a $1 soda and a $1 hamburger. Mama would kill me to know I come here and buy crap food, when she cooks mountains of food at home, but all we really want is the table. Now, we can sit for an hour at least before the manager tells us to leave.
As we eat, Lauren says, “Disney has a new Aladdin movie now. It’s not racist.” She sounds annoyed.

I know what she’s talking about. In the live-action film, there’s even a cute Arab guy playing Aladdin. Will Smith is the genie, so who can be mad at that? And Disney took out the lyrics about Arabs being “barbaric” and all.

I don’t like this. Lauren and I don’t disagree about anything. So it’s hard to explain how Jasmine makes me cringe. She’s the only Arab girl I ever see in movies and she wears haram clothes and talks to a tiger. And when people hear I’m Arab, that’s who they think about, a princess whose father locks her up in a castle.

Meanwhile, my baba is a mechanic who took me to his garage when I was younger. Mama never liked having me home in the kitchen, because that was her space, and anyway, Baba realized I could figure things out easily. “Layla, come here and try to take this out,” he’d said one afternoon, in the summer. I was fourteen. He popped the hood on a Buick, and we both leaned over, studying the engine bay. He was pointing to the alternator. He didn’t tell me how, just watched me. When I finally did it, he said, “Now put it back.” So I did. Another time, he handed me pliers and told me to take the seats out of a Malibu. Some tasks took me longer than others, but I always figured them out. And he always knew I could.
I finish eating and fold my hamburger wrapper into tiny squares, smaller and smaller until it’s the size of my thumbnail.
“I’m not trying to step on everyone here,” I say. “I know everyone’s excited.”
“Have you even seen the new movie?” Lauren asks me, guzzling the last of her Coke. As if that proves something. When I shake my head, she shrugs. “Well, you shouldn’t complain then.”

D talks about the play at the first Drama Guild meeting after school the following week. He has pizza for us and cheeseballs and cans of Sprite. “We’re asking for four thousand from the PTA,” he explains, “but they think we will actually make a lot of money on ticket sales.” He’s up on stage, sitting cross-legged, on a long, oak table, which is what we use to assemble and sew costumes during the year. Spreading his hands out, he says, “It’s Aladdin . So yeah — everyone wants to see it.”
There are two things he wants to do, he says, to blow people’s minds: the genie has to appear and disappear somehow, using the fog machines, and the magic carpet has to be … magical.
“Can we build something?” He’s looking right at me. “You know, to make it actually fly somehow? They do that in the Broadway version.”
I’m sitting below him in the orchestra pit, with Lauren and Rajesh. Samantha is there too. Last year, she was Dorothy in The Wizard of Oz .
“Maybe...” I say, then add, “...actually, I was wondering...you know.”
“I don’t. Tell me.”
“Well...how final is the decision to use Aladdin ?” I ask. My heart starts racing. In a second, my voice will tremble.
“Pretty final.” His tone is curious, and he tilts his head as he looks down at me.
“It’s just that, it’s a movie that has a lot of stereotypes about Arab Americans and — ”
“Well, it was written in a time when there was no such thing as America,” he says laughing, and everyone else laughs too because now it’s awkward.

He listens patiently, while I stumble through the little speech I practiced this morning. I talk about the lyrics to the original opening song and how they painted Arabs in a bad way, and how it fed into the stereotypes that already exist about us.
Lauren is across from me, her eyes on the ceiling.

D is gazing down at me, his face attentive. But you know how you can tell when an adult is humoring you? Like, they’re going to let you say your thing, and then they’ll pretend to think about it? He’s definitely doing that to me.

When I’m done, he goes, “I appreciate you bringing this up.” Looks all serious. He even hushes Samantha, who seems alarmed that the play is under threat. “In fact, I’m very sensitive to those issues and I’ve already considered them. I made sure that our script has no mention of those troubling lines at all.”
“What were the lines?” Samantha asks.
“The song said Arabia is a land ‘where they cut off your ear if they don’t like your face,’” I say quietly.
“But they were changed?” Samantha asks D, and he nods. “OK. So...that’s good, right?”
D throws out his hands, like he’s just done a magic trick. “Absolutely. And that’s how it should always work.”
“It’s not just the lines...” I start to say.
But he ignores me and starts going on about being inclusive and how we have to listen to everyone’s voice...except he’s just ignored mine.
Dr. Bledsoe is like a caricature of a crazy scientist — his brown hair is never combed, and he’s so skinny because he forgets to eat. I always think about how my mother would love to feed him.

I’m the only one in the whole AP Physics class that he asked to help him with the gyroscope. “Is it for a grade?” I reply. I’m taking four AP classes this year, including AP Calculus and AP Literature, but they have nothing on the Physics class. I’m nervous about taking on something that will hurt my grade.
“No grade, just for fun,” he says. “Are you in?”
“Heck yeah.”

Even when I was a freshman and didn’t qualify yet for his classes, I would wander in and he would let me hang out in his lab. I’d clean the equipment and wipe the tables after school several days a week when I wasn’t in Drama Guild, and so I became kind of an assistant lab tech.

“Why acting?” he would ask me sometimes, studying a paper or peering at his computer screen through his tiny, rectangular wireless glasses. They looked funny sometimes, like two blank microscope slides, butterflying his nose.
“I like acting.”
“Are you an actor?”

“No. I’m too nervous to be on stage. I stay behind the scenes. But I like being part of something big like that.” Sometimes, I think that is why I like science too. Science doesn’t mind when you make a mistake. Science looks forward to mistakes.

Before we start planning the gyroscope, Bledsoe shows me a video. “It’s almost like an optical illusion,” he says. I see a disk mounted on a frame, and it starts spinning wildly – but not really, I notice. There is stability provided by the frame, a force that always keeps it upright no matter how out-of-control it looks.
“It looks like it should be falling over, but it’s not,” I say.

“Like it’s defying gravity, right?” He nods excitedly. “The torque applied to the disk causes a precession — so what happens is the axis is caught between gravity and its own angular momentum vector, and it’s forced to find a happy middle ground to remain stable.”
We watch the video quietly, like awed worshippers at an altar.
“It’s beautiful.”
“I could buy one,” he says. “I have money in the science budget.”
“Nah,” I reply. “Let’s build it.”

One day at lunch, I hear Rajesh announce he’s auditioning for the part of Aladdin, and three guys, who are all in Drama Guild, roll their eyes. Because they know it’s over. Rajesh is a great actor – he played the Scarecrow to Samantha’s Dorothy, and he basically stole the whole show. He could have played the Wicked Witch and done it well. He just has a lot of charisma and a smile that could knock you off a chair.
“I might try out for the genie,” Lauren says.
“Perfect,” says Rajesh.
“That’s ok, right? D didn’t say it has to be a guy.”
“The genie can be gender inclusive,” he says with a smile. “This is a new era.” He looks at me pointedly.
“I’m allowed to have an opinion,” I say, starting to feel nervous.

“You are,” Rajesh says. “But, like, if the show is offensive, I’d be offended. I’m Indian.” He says it, like that explains it. And now the whole table starts talking like they’re historians, about how the stories originated in China and India and they’re not even Arab in origin. I start to feel nervous. I hate when people bring up rebuttals that I don’t know about, because I can’t tell if they’re legit or if they’re blowing smoke.
But they’re all intent on making sure I know I’m wrong.
And that is something.
Who am I, anyway? I’m just the nerd who builds scenery. And I’ve always been on the fringe of the group, anyway. It would suck to get pushed out even further.
I cave.

“I’ve been sketching ideas for a mechanism to make the carpet fly.” I say quietly. They all pay attention. I am a team player , I say with my eyes as I explain my idea. I tell them how I researched the way it was done on Broadway. “It won’t be anything like that...it probably won’t carry anyone. But I think I can make it hover above the stage.” I don’t mention how, last week, I sawed some 2x4s and hammered them together in a cross shape. I strung some heavy-duty wire to bind them and sat there, staring at it for a while.
I thought, This might work .
I could make this work.
I’m just not sure I want it to.

“I never took you to see the movie because I hated it when I was your age,” Mama tells me. We’re walking to the Enoch Pratt Library, which takes up almost a whole block on Cathedral Street. The front door looks like a massive fortress gate, and inside, the hallways are lined with statues and paintings. The best room is the Poe Room – Edgar Allan Poe died in this city, so they claimed him by naming the football team after his poem, “The Raven.”

Mama is taking classes at the community college, to finish up a degree in psychology that she stopped when I was born. She comes to the Pratt to study and I come with her. Our apartment is too loud, because Anthony the electrician upstairs lifts weights and drops them all afternoon, and next door, Mrs. Sanjay has four kids who are running around and screeching all the time. We find a table by the teen section, and we put our papers and books down. “Mama, tell me...Why is Aladdin bad?” I ask her. “I’m trying to explain it to my Drama Guild at school but I get confused when they say stuff.”
“It portrays Arabs as barbaric people.”
“But Jasmine and Aladdin are characters everyone likes. They’re not bad. And they’re Arabs.”
“They’re also the ‘least’ Arab of all the characters… Jafar is very stereotypically Arab...” she continues.
“Yeah, but...what’s the reason?”
“Habibti,” she says with a sigh, and I suddenly feel sad. If she can’t even find the right words, what can I do?

“Look,” she says, “sometimes people just have to take your word for it. It’s like someone stepping on your toes and not moving off. Do you have to really explain how the pressure is causing you pain?”
“Right.”

“Sometimes, they have to imagine the pain to get it. You don’t have to prove it.” She glances at me over her glasses. “Or agonize over how to explain it.” She turns back to her laptop. “Let’s get to work.”

I talk to D during our Drama Club. Someone has told him about my plans for the lift, so he wants to see my sketch. “This is how we can make it hover, maybe at a point when Aladdin summons it,” I explain. While he reviews my sketch, I ask him, “Did you know there was a school that canceled Aladdin last year?”
“In Catonsville? Yeah, I know about that. It wasn’t Aladdin ...it was Aladdin Jr. ”
“Same thing, though, right?”

“Basically. But they didn’t even consult the parents. Very disrespectful to the drama parents who, as you know, do so much for the club.” He puts out his hands, looking like some wise Buddha.“ The thing is,” he adds solemnly, “everybody has a right to investigate other cultures and add their own artistic flair. Can you imagine if someone told me, because I’m white, that I’m not allowed to listen to or enjoy rap music?”
“Nobody is saying you can’t read the Arabian Nights. But the—”
D smiled, like he is being patient with me. “You know,” he says, “this cultural appropriation stuff wears me out.”
Samantha and two other girls are trying out for Jasmine. Nobody else but Rajesh dares to audition for Aladdin, so he acts in the scene with each of them.

I’m still working on the pulley mechanism. It’s always been hard to be the person D depends on for stage equipment and engineering, as well as to play minor roles. I watch Rajesh with Samantha, who’s wearing a black wig over her blond hair, and she’s dressed in a teal blouse and loose pants. She’s really working for this one, I think. When she turns around, I see the black eyeliner dramatically sweeping across her eyelids.

She’s not too bad. Ever since sophomore year, she’s always had the lead roles in the plays. I only ever had one role, as a Munchkin, but that’s because Allison got sick and I am the same size. They gave Allison’s lines to someone else, and I was only on stage for a total of twelve minutes.
Rajesh steps off stage for a bit and smiles at me. “You should try out for Jasmine,” he says. “Your voice is terrific.”
“Thanks. But I don’t think so.”
“So,” he says, pausing for a beat, “this sounds weird, but you look authentic. If that makes sense.”
“It doesn’t make sense, because she’s a cartoon,” I say and go back to work.
The gyroscope works.

Bledsoe is freaking like carbon dioxide gas bubbling out of a beaker. “I knew it,” he says, slamming his fist down on the table. Except he does it weird, like he gets a strong windup going, but right before impact, he slows down so that his fist just gently taps the surface. It’s hilarious.

“It’s amazing,” I say, watching it closely. Honestly, it looks like a bike wheel inside of a globe, but it spins magically, automatically righting itself whenever it leans too far forward or back. It just knows when to correct itself and pops back into alignment every time. I stop it and start it again with a flick of my wrist, over and over, taking a video for Baba. It never stumbles. It re-centers and finds the middle, no matter how close it gets to falling.
It looks like a goddess being born, growing taller and taking shape, right there on the workbench, the round orb like a halo around her brilliant head.
“You know,” he tells me, his eyes still fixed on the gyroscope. “I think you have a natural talent for science.”
“I have a father who’s a mechanic,” I say, “and I’m used to figuring things out.”

Baba never finished high school because of the war. It’s one reason why he pushes Mama to finish her degree at the college. “We had dreams too,” he said to her once, when she worried about the cost. “Let’s start here and see what happens.” I remember that moment, because they hugged for a long time.
I glance at the time on my phone and stand up. “I need to get to D’s room.”
“They’re still putting that play on?”

“Yeah.” I pause. “I’m trying to tell them that I have a problem with it, but...they want me to explain why , and they want a reason, and D doesn’t really want to talk about it. The whole thing just sucks.”
He shrugs. “It does suck.” He reaches out and sets the gyroscope into motion again. “The monkey was the most interesting character.”
When I get to the classroom, there is a huddle of people in front of D’s door, where a paper is taped. Samantha is there, being hugged by her friends, and there are tears in her eyes.
But she doesn’t look sad at all.
Not at all.
I get closer and see, “Aladdin/ Rajesh. Princess Jasmine – Samantha.”
Lauren is there too, and she slides her arm around me. “I guess she’s the star again.”
I knew she would be.

So why do I want to break something, as I watch Samantha fake-cry with her friends? Drama is not like science, where there’s a right and a wrong. The test shows positive or negative. The experiment works or it fails. Either the thing spins or it doesn’t. This is different and murky and I’m not feeling very good.
I go to my work room and lock the door.
My heart is thudding in my chest, but I ignore it this time. It’s not going to stop me.

The genie lift is on the bench. I can hear Dr. Bledsoe’s voice, saying, “It does suck,” and I continue to play his words in my mind as I use the brick hammer to smash the whole thing into tiny, jagged shards.

options.label: When you are confused, you can always rely on the adults around you to help you figure it out.
If everyone else around you sees things differently than you do, it is probably because you are wrong.
It is generally better to compromise and keep the peace than to push too hard to have your voice heard.
The fact that others do not understand why something is hurtful does not mean that you should accept it.

questions.prompt: What is the theme of the story?

options.label: Dr. Bledsoe listens to Layla and takes her thoughts seriously, while Mr. Davison mostly cares about putting on a popular play.
Mr. Davison wants Layla to take Drama Guild more seriously, but Dr. Bledsoe worries that her grades might suffer if she does.
Dr. Bledsoe thinks that Layla should focus on her AP classes and her grades, but Mr. Davison thinks it is important to also have fun.
Mr. Davison listens carefully to Layla's goals related to her future in drama, while Dr. Bledsoe mostly cares about working on the gyroscope.

questions.prompt: In the story, how are Dr. Bledsoe's and Mr. Davison's points of view different?

options.label: Like the gyroscope, Layla is incapable of losing her balance because she has so much faith in herself.
Layla decides that the opinion of her favorite teacher is more important to her than how she feels inside.
Layla regains her balance after moments of doubt and determines the right decision by being true to herself.
Unlike the gyroscope, Layla does not know how to balance all the parts of her life in a way that makes her happy.

questions.prompt: Re-read Layla's description of the gyroscope in paragraph 104. Which of the following statements best describes how the gyroscope symbolizes Layla's character development?

options.label: "'Pretty final.' His tone is curious, and he tilts his head as he looks down at me." (Paragraph 34)
"When I'm done, he goes, "I appreciate you bringing this up." Looks all serious." (Paragraph 40)
"'In fact, I'm very sensitive to those issues and I've already considered them. I made sure that our script has no mention of those troubling lines at all.'" (Paragraph 40)
"But he ignores me and starts going on about being inclusive and how we have to listen to everyone's voice...except he's just ignored mine." (Paragraph 46)

questions.prompt: Which detail supports the idea that Mr. Davison's actions are hypocritical, or different from what he claims to believe?

options.label: It illustrates for the reader that Layla and her mother see the situation differently because of generational differences.
It reminds the reader that Layla's mother is too busy with her own studies to worry about Layla's hurt feelings over a school play.
It alerts the reader to the fact that not understanding why the play is upsetting to Layla is the fault of Mr. Davison and Layla's peers, and not Layla's responsibility to correct.
It shows the reader that Layla's mother understands her feelings, but thinks that she needs to be a bit more patient with her teachers and classmates to help them better understand.

questions.prompt: For what purpose does the author include paragraph 87 in the story?

options.label: They show that Layla's feelings of anger have taken over, and she is no longer able to take a joke.
They show that Layla feels misunderstood and stereotyped both as an Arab person and as a young woman.
They show that Layla feels misunderstood by her white classmates, but is able to take comfort in other students of color.
They show that Layla feels comfortable with her classmates and knows that any disagreements that they have will eventually pass.

questions.prompt: How do paragraphs 97-100 contribute to an understanding of Layla's feelings?
How does Layla grow more confident in expressing herself by the end of the story?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Cultural appropriation is using all or part of a culture that is not your own inappropriately or without giving credit for where it came from. This can include treating parts of a culture that is not your own like a costume or a fashion trend. In the story, Mr. Davison says that "'this cultural appropriation stuff really wears me out'" (Paragraph 93). Do you see any examples of cultural appropriation in the text? If so, what do you think the character(s) could have done differently to appreciate the culture rather than appropriate it? Have you either witnessed or experienced cultural appropriation in your own life? What happened? How did it feel? If you have ever unknowingly appropriated someone else's culture in the past, what can you do to make sure you avoid the same mistakes in the future?

Stereotypes are overly simplified and frequently negative images or ideas about people from a particular group, often used in a harmful way. In the story, Layla explains that the way that Jasmine is portrayed in Aladdin is upsetting because it is nothing like who she is as a real-life Arab girl. Explain in your own words why stereotypes can be both personally hurtful and harmful to minority groups. Have you ever experienced someone making judgments about you based on a stereotype? What was the stereotype? How did it make you feel? Do you think some stereotypes are more harmful than others? Explain.

The story alludes to issues of racism in some beloved Disney movies and cartoons. Have you ever gone back to watch a cartoon, movie, or TV show that you once loved and been surprised to find that it contained racial or gender-based stereotypes? What are some examples? Is it right to still love old favorites if we discover that they are problematic, or do we have to give them up completely? Is there a middle ground between the two? Explain and discuss your answer.

At the beginning of the story, Layla says that Mr. Davison is her favorite teacher and Dr. Bledsoe is her second-favorite teacher, and that she loves both drama and science. Why do you think that Mr. Davison was Layla's favorite teacher in the beginning? How would you describe the difference in the way that each of these two teachers treats Layla? Have your feelings about an adult in your life ever changed based on how they treated you during an important moment? What happened? How did it feel?

articles.title: Transfer Rules Could Change College Athletics
articles.author: Fred Bowen
articles.description: A new student-athlete transfer rule will have a major impact on college sports.
articles.intro: Student-athletes are full-time or part-time students of a university or college who also take part in an organized and competitive athletic program offered by the school. In this text, Fred Brown explores a new rule for student-athletes.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the arguments presented that are for and against the new transfer rule.
articles.permissions: "Transfer Rules Could Change College Athletics" by Fred Bowen. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: There is a word you hear a lot around college sports these days… transfers.
Transferring is when a student-athlete chooses to leave one school where they are playing a sport to go to another school to play on the team there.

In the past, when an athlete playing one of the five major sports — football, men’s basketball, women’s basketball, baseball, or hockey — transferred, they had to sit out a year before playing at the new school.

In 2021, the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA), the organization that governs college sports, changed the rule. Now, athletes in the five major sports can change schools and play right away one time.
As a result, lots of athletes are changing schools and teams. Let’s look at the numbers.

According to ESPN, 3,284 players from Football Bowl Subdivision (FBS) schools — the schools with the largest and highest-ranked football programs — entered the transfer portal to seek a transfer between August 1, 2022, and May 1, 2023. That is more than 20% of the FBS players, or almost twenty-five players per team.

It’s not just football. More than 1,500 players in men’s college basketball entered the transfer portal. That’s about five players for each team (remember, college basketball teams only have about fifteen players).
And it’s not just men’s sports. More than a thousand players in women’s college basketball sought transfers.
Transfers are making a big difference for a lot of teams. For example, fourteen of the basketball players in the 2023 men’s Final Four had transferred from other teams.

Angel Reese, who led Louisiana State University (LSU) over Iowa in the 2023 NCAA women’s basketball championship game, was a transfer. Reese had played her first two seasons at the University of Maryland.
It’s too early to tell how teams could be affected by the new transfer rules. There are lots of reasons for student-athletes to switch schools.
Some players who are not getting significant playing time at top programs may transfer to get more time on the field or court. This could even out the talent among the different teams.

However, top programs may use the transfer portal to recruit older, more proven college players they may have overlooked in the original recruiting process for high school athletes. When the rule took effect, head football coach John Grass of Jacksonville State, a smaller Football Championship Subdivision (FCS) school, told Sports Illustrated , “Mid-majors are fixing to feel the pain… The top 25 (teams) are going to [take players from] the mid-majors and the mid-majors are going to try to [take players from] the FCS guys.”

Still, the new rule seems fairer. As Tom Mars, an attorney who argued for the rule change, told CBS News, “It represents a giant step in treating college athletes equally and fairly… college athletes deserve the same freedom of movement as head coaches who are paid millions of dollars a year and freely move from one job to another.”
Whether the rule is more fair or regardless of whichever teams are helped, some fear the new transfer rules may teach players a bad lesson.

Long-time college basketball coach Tubby Smith voiced concerns to ESPN about the new transfer rules. “We're teaching [players] how to quit. That’s what we’re doing. Things are not going well, let’s quit.”

Smith, who was one of 17 children, remembered calling his father when he wanted to transfer from his college in 1969. After Smith’s father asked him several questions to make sure he was being treated all right at High Point, he said: “Well, you can’t come home. Your bed’s been taken... But you can join the Army.”
Smith said his father’s tough love was a good lesson. He stayed at High Point, graduated, and went into coaching. In 1998, his team at the University of Kentucky won the NCAA national championship.
For better or for worse, we know one thing — transfers are changing college sports.

options.label: The new student-athlete transfer rules are supported by all coaches and players in the top divisions.
The new student-athlete transfer rules will have little effect on teams that are already highly successful.
The new student-athlete transfer rules guarantee that top athletes will get more playing time if they switch teams.
The new student-athlete transfer rules allow more freedom for student- athletes to control their college athletic careers.

questions.prompt: Which sentence best states the central idea of the article?

options.label: All teams will encounter problems due to the new transfer rules because it will create a culture of taking players from other teams.
Student-athletes will benefit from the transfer rule because they will be able to go to teams where their skills will be fully utilized.
The new transfer rule allows all of the top college teams to work together to trade players and form the best teams possible.
Mid-major teams will benefit from the new transfer rules because they will gain players from the top 25 teams.

questions.prompt: Which idea about how the new transfer rules' effects is emphasized in paragraph 12?

options.label: mid-level teams are most likely to suffer from the transfer rules.
mid-level teams are going to start to beat upper-level teams.
student-athletes are more at risk of injuries if they transfer.
student-athletes rarely benefit from transferring.

questions.prompt: The phrase "'feel the pain'" in paragraph 13 shows that —

options.label: It introduces the author's opinion that he supports in the rest of the article.
It introduces a new main idea that is explored in the rest of the article.
It describes the causes for the events in the first half of the article.
It summarizes the main ideas of the first half of the article.

questions.prompt: Read this sentence from paragraph 15: "Whether the rule is more fair or regardless of whichever teams are helped, some fear the new transfer rules may teach players a bad lesson." How does this sentence contribute to the structure of the article?
Why does the author of the article include arguments that are both for and against the new student-athlete transfer rules?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Consider the reasons and evidence provided by the author. Do you think student- athletes should be afforded the "freedom of movement" that the new transfer rules provide, or are the new rules teaching players "how to quit"? Use evidence from the text and your own experiences to explain your answer.

Do you think it is necessary for long-standing rules, such as the student transfer rule, to be reviewed and modified over time? What are some rules in your life that you think are unfair that should be reviewed and changed? How would you suggest they be changed?

As stated in the article, Tubby Smith's father would not let him transfer from his college to a new one in 1969. Do you agree with his father's decision? Do you think students should be allowed to quit activities, change schools, or switch teams if they are unhappy? What lesson might a student learn if they are forced to stay even if they do not want to? What lesson might they learn if they are allowed to leave?
What reasons, besides the ones described in the text, do you think a student-athlete might consider when looking to transfer? Explain your thinking.

articles.title: Chemo Side Effects: Memory
articles.author: Elise Partridge
articles.description: A speaker describes their struggles with memory.
articles.intro: Elise Patridge is a poet and editor. In this poem titled "Chemo Side Effects: Memory," a speaker describes their difficulties with memory. Chemo, short for chemotherapy, is a common treatment for cancer that often has painful side effects.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the figurative language the speaker uses to describe their experiences with memory.
articles.permissions: Elise Partridge, "Chemo Side Effects: Memory" from Chameleon Hours. Copyright © 2008 by Elise Partridge. Reprinted by permission of The University of Chicago Press. Source: Chameleon Hours (University of Chicago Press, 2008)

blocks.text: Where is the word I want?
Groping
in the thicket,
about to pinch the
dangling
berry, my fingerpads
close on
air.
I can hear it
scrabbling like a squirrel
on the oak's far side.
Word, please send over this black stretch of ocean
your singular flare,
blaze
your topaz in the mind's blank.
I could always pull the gift
from the lucky-dip barrel,
scoop the right jewel
from my dragon's trove...
Now I flail,
the wrong item creaks up
on the mental dumbwaiter.
No use —
it's turning
out of sight,
a bicycle down a
Venetian alley —
I clatter after, only to find
gondolas bobbing in sunny silence,
a pigeon mumbling something
I just can't catch.

options.label: The most frightening part of chemo is the inability to remember.
It can be comforting to get lost in good memories while going through chemo.
The positive outcomes of chemo are worth the negative side effect of memory loss.
Chemo can make it so that words and memories are just out of reach.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which statement best expresses the theme of the poem?

options.label: "about to pinch the / dangling / berry" (Lines 4-6)
"I could always pull the gift / from the lucky-dip barrel" (Lines 16-17)
"No use — / it's turning / out of sight" (Lines 23-25)
"gondolas bobbing in sunny silence, / a pigeon mumbling something" (Lines 29-30)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the poem best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: They show how the only memories that the speaker can remember are bad ones.
They suggest how close the speaker is to giving up on trying to remember anything at all.
They show how it's only becoming more difficult for the speaker to retrieve memories each time.
They suggest that the speaker feels close to remembering, but the memory escapes them.

questions.prompt: PART A: How do the metaphors and similes used to describe memory retrieval contribute to the meaning of the poem?

options.label: "about to pinch the / dangling / berry, my fingerpads / close on / air." (Lines 4-8)
"Word, please send over this black stretch of ocean / your singular flare" (Lines 12-13)
"I could always pull the gift / from the lucky-dip barrel, / scoop the right jewel" (Lines 16-18)
"a bicycle down a / Venetian alley — / I clatter after" (Lines 26-28)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which quote from the poem best supports the answer to Part A?
How does the final stanza of the poem contribute to its overall theme? Use details from the poem to support your answer.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the poem, the speaker describes how they struggle to remember certain things. How important do you think your memories are to who you are?

In the poem, the speaker describes memory loss as one of the side effects of chemo, which is used to treat cancer. What do you think it takes for a person to overcome something as difficult as cancer and the side effects of its treatment? Do you think it requires more physical or emotional strength? Why?

articles.title: Explainer: How scientists know Earth is warming
articles.subtitle: With satellites and ice cores, scientists can calculate the planet's temperature
articles.author: Sharon Oosthoek
articles.description: This text explains how scientists can document global warming by measuring the Earth's temperature.
articles.intro: Scientists have been saying for a long time that the Earth is getting warmer, but how do they know for sure? In this text from Science News for Kids, Sharon Oosthoek discusses how scientists measure the Earth's temperature.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how human activity has contributed to the change in Earth’s temperature.
articles.permissions: From Science News for Students , 2015. © Society for Science & the Public. Reprinted with permission. This article is intended only for single-classroom use by teachers. For rights to republish Science News for Students articles in assessments, course packs, or textbooks, visit: https://www.societyforscience.org/permission-republish .

blocks.text: Temperatures on Earth can soar above 40° Celsius (104° Fahrenheit) and drop well below 0 °C (32 °F). Despite that variability, scientists can calculate a global average. Countries around the world have had reliable weather monitoring stations on land and sea since about 1880. In the 1960s, researchers also began taking the Earth's temperature with the help of satellites. Satellites don't measure temperature directly. Instead they measure radiation given off by oxygen that's in Earth's atmosphere. The intensity of this radiation is linked to air temperatures. Computer programs take all of these data and turn them into a global average.
Scientists use temperature readings from both satellites and climate monitoring stations to show that 2014 was officially the warmest year on record — at least through 2014.
The temperatures have not stopped rising, however. By the close of 2015, temperatures were yet again about to hit a new record high.

People have been keeping detailed temperature readings only as far as 1880. So scientists have to use other methods to calculate what earlier temperatures would have been. With these methods, they now can see that global temperatures have at times in the distant past been even higher than they are today.

But the historical record also shows that over the past 100 years or so, temperatures have been rising exceptionally fast, notes Allegra LeGrande. She is a climate scientist at the NASA Goddard Institute for Space Studies and the Center for Climate Systems Research, in New York City.

Isotopes are different forms of the same element that vary in their weight. LeGrande finds clues about past and present climates in oxygen and hydrogen isotopes. Warmer temperatures and drier conditions in the atmosphere lead to the water in rain and snow having more heavy isotopes of both oxygen and hydrogen. Cooler, wetter conditions lead to greater numbers of the lighter isotopes of oxygen and hydrogen.

Glaciers and ice sheets trap samples of oxygen and hydrogen isotopes from Earth’s atmosphere going back about one million years. As more snow piles up, the snow on the bottom gets compacted into ice. A new layer accumulates each year.

Scientists drill and remove ice cores — long tubes of ice — from glaciers. Each layer of the ice core contains hydrogen and oxygen going back further in Earth's history. The amount of oxygen and hydrogen isotopes in each layer can tell us how warm or cold the climate was back then.

Those ice cores show current warming is happening faster now than in the past. In the last million years or so, the planet has gone through a series of ice ages. The Earth got cooler, then warmed up. Research by the National Aeronautics and Space Administration shows that during the periods of warming, global temperatures rose 4 °C to 7 °C (7.2 °F to 12.6 °F) over about 5,000 years. But in the past century, the temperature has climbed roughly 10 times faster!

Temperatures are rising quickly now because of greenhouse gases, such as carbon dioxide and methane. When the planet's ground and water are warmed by the sun, they radiate heat back out into the atmosphere. Greenhouse gases trap that heat close to the planet’s surface. That's good, up to a point. Life on Earth needs that warmth to survive. But pollution is adding additional greenhouse gases to the atmosphere. This happens every time people burn fossil fuels such as coal, oil or gas.

During the Industrial Revolution, people started to burn large amounts of fossil fuels. We are burning even more today. Since about 1750, carbon dioxide levels have increased nearly 38 percent (at least through 2009), according to data from the National Aeronautics and Space Administration. Methane concentrations have increased 148 percent. And levels of both greenhouse gases continue to rise.
That has been trapping a lot of heat. As a result, Earth is warming.

options.label: While scientists know that the Earth has been warming since the 1880s, they don't have reliable weather information from before then.
By studying isotopes trapped in glaciers, scientists can confirm the different types of pollutants that are contributing to global warming.
Scientists have confirmed the Earth's rapid warming by monitoring weather globally for over a century and studying clues trapped in glaciers from even farther back.
Even though the Earth is warming, confirmed by both satellite data and ice cores, it's happening at a slow enough rate that scientists are not yet concerned.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which statement best expresses the central idea of the text?

options.label: "Scientists use temperature readings from both satellites and climate monitoring stations to show that 2014 was officially the warmest year on record — at least through 2014." (Paragraph 2)
"With these methods, they now can see that global temperatures have at times in the distant past been even higher than they are today." (Paragraph 4)
"Warmer temperatures and drier conditions in the atmosphere lead to the water in rain and snow having more heavy isotopes of both oxygen and hydrogen." (Paragraph 6)

"Those ice cores show current warming is happening faster now than in the past. In the last million years or so, the planet has gone through a series of ice ages." (Paragraph 9) E. "Greenhouse gases trap that heat close to the planet's surface. That's good, up to a point. Life on Earth needs that warmth to survive." (Paragraph 10) F. "During the Industrial Revolution, people started to burn large amounts of fossil fuels. We are burning even more today." (Paragraph 11)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which TWO details from the text best support the answer to Part A?

options.label: to explain that some people are still skeptical about global warming because the Earth has warmed in the past
to show how scientists are in the dark about Earth's temperatures farther back in history
to provide readers with potential solutions for how humans can bring down the Earth's global temperature
to explain the methods scientists have used to learn that the Earth is warming at an alarming rate

questions.prompt: What is the author's main purpose in the text?

options.label: It reassures readers that sudden increases and decreases in global temperatures is normal for the Earth.
It emphasizes that while the Earth has gone through periods of warming in the past, it has never been at such a rapid rate.
It shows how the information that the ice cores can provide scientists about global warming is limited.
It suggests that glaciers and ice sheets are the most seriously affected by changes in global temperatures.

questions.prompt: How does paragraph 9 contribute to the development of ideas in the text?
What is the relationship between human activity and the warming temperatures on Earth? Use details from the text to support your response.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the text, the author discusses how the burning of fossil fuels has led to the Earth warming. How have fossil fuels contributed to some of the advances humans have made? Do you think the advantages of fossil fuels outweigh what they cost the environment? Why or why not? What other sources of fuel are available to us?

In the text, the author discusses how the Earth goes through natural phases of warming and cooling. How do we know that this period of warming is caused by humans, not the natural course of the environment?

articles.title: Explainer: How CRISPR Works
articles.subtitle: This technique lets scientists edit DNA in plants and animals
articles.author: Tina Hesman Saey
articles.description: Tina Hesman Saey describes a gene editing tool: CRISPR.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Tina Hesman Saey discusses how scientists are able to edit organisms' genes.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, takes notes on how CRISPR changes DNA.
articles.permissions: From Science News for Students , 2017. © Society for Science & the Public. Reprinted with permission. This article is intended only for single-classroom use by teachers. For rights to republish Science News for Students articles in assessments, course packs, or textbooks, visit: https://www.societyforscience.org/permission-republish .

blocks.text: Scientists usually shy away from using the word miracle. Unless they’re talking about the gene-editing tool called CRISPR, that is. “You can do anything with CRISPR,” some say. Others just call it amazing.

CRISPR stands for “clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats.” Those repeats are found in bacteria’s DNA. They are actually copies of small pieces of viruses. Bacteria use them like collections of mug shots to identify bad viruses. Cas9 is an enzyme that can cut apart DNA. Bacteria fight off viruses by sending the Cas9 enzyme to chop up viruses that have a mug shot in the collection. Scientists recently figured out how bacteria do this. Now, in the lab, researchers use a similar approach to turn the microbe’s virus-fighting system into the hottest new lab tool.
This CRISPR/Cas9 tool was first described in 2012 and 2013. Science labs around the world soon started using it to alter an organism’s genome — the entire set of its DNA instructions.

This tool can quickly and efficiently tweak almost any gene in any plant or animal. Researchers already have used it to fix genetic diseases in animals, to combat viruses and to sterilize mosquitoes. They have also used it to prepare pig organs for human transplants and to beef up the muscles in beagles.

So far CRISPR’s biggest impact has been felt in basic biology labs. This low-cost gene editor is easy to use. That has made it possible for researchers to delve into the basic mysteries of life. And they can do it in ways that used to be difficult if not impossible.

Robert Reed is a developmental biologist at Cornell University in Ithaca, N.Y. He likens CRISPR to a computer mouse. “You can just point it at a place in the genome and you can do anything you want at that spot.”

At first, that meant anything that involved cutting DNA. CRISPR/Cas9 in its original form is a homing device (the CRISPR part) that guides molecular scissors (the Cas9 enzyme) to a target section of DNA. Together, they work as a genetic-engineering cruise missile that disables or repairs a gene, or inserts something new where the Cas9 scissors has made some cuts. Newer versions of CRISPR are called “base editors.” These can edit genetic material one base at a time, without cutting. They’re more like a pencil than like scissors.
Here’s how it works

Scientists start with RNA. That’s a molecule that can read the genetic information in DNA. The RNA finds the spot in the nucleus of a cell where some editing activity should take place. (The nucleus is a compartment in a cell where most of the genetic material is stored.) This guide RNA shepherds Cas9 to the precise spot on DNA where a cut is called for. Cas9 then locks onto the double-stranded DNA and unzips it.

This allows the guide RNA to pair up with some region of the DNA it has targeted. Cas9 snips the DNA at this spot. This creates a break in both strands of the DNA molecule. The cell, sensing a problem, repairs the break.
Fixing the break might disable a gene (the easiest thing to do). Alternatively, this repair might fix a mistake or even insert a new gene (a much more difficult process).

Cells usually repair a break in their DNA by gluing the loose ends back together. That’s a sloppy process. It often results in a mistake that disables some gene. That may not sound useful — but sometimes it is.

Scientists cut DNA with CRISPR/Cas9 to make gene changes, or mutations. By comparing cells with and without the mutation, scientists can sometimes figure out what a protein’s normal role is. Or a new mutation may help them understand genetic diseases. CRISPR/Cas9 also can be useful in human cells by disabling certain genes — ones, for instance, that play a role in inherited diseases.

“The original Cas9 is like a Swiss army knife with only one application: It’s a knife,” says Gene Yeo. He is an RNA biologist at the University of California, San Diego. But Yeo and others have bolted other proteins and chemicals to the dulled blades. That has transformed that knife into a multifunctional tool.

CRISPR/Cas9 and related tools can now be used in new ways, such as changing a single nucleotide base — a single letter in the genetic code — or adding a fluorescent protein to tag a spot in the DNA that scientists want to track. Scientists also can use this genetic cut-and-paste technology to turn genes on or off.

This explosion of new ways to use CRISPR hasn’t ended. Feng Zhang is a molecular biologist at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in Cambridge. He was one of the first scientists to wield the Cas9 scissors. “The field is advancing so rapidly,” he says. “Just looking at how far we have come... I think what we’ll see coming in the next few years will just be amazing.”

options.label: CRISPR can only be used on organisms with genetic diseases.
CRISPR has developed to alter DNA in impactful ways.
CRISPR is at risk of being used unethically.
CRISPR is dangerous to use on humans.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which statement identifies the central idea of the text?

options.label: "Bacteria fight off viruses by sending the Cas9 enzyme to chop up viruses that have a mug shot in the collection. Scientists recently figured out how bacteria do this." (Paragraph 2)
"CRISPR/Cas9 in its original form is a homing device (the CRISPR part) that guides molecular scissors" (Paragraph 7)
"Cells usually repair a break in their DNA by gluing the loose ends back together. That's a sloppy process." (Paragraph 11)
"CRISPR/Cas9 also can be useful in human cells by disabling certain genes — ones, for instance, that play a role in inherited diseases." (Paragraph 12)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: She describes her own experiences using the tool.

questions.prompt: How to does paragraph 4 contribute to the development of ideas in the text?

options.label: to build
to predict
to examine
to improve

questions.prompt: What is the meaning of "delve" in paragraph 5?

options.label: It is a small tool.
It is old technology.
It is easy to navigate.

questions.prompt: Which statement is the likely reason Robert Reed "likens CRISPR to a computer mouse" in paragraph 6?

options.label: Base editors only erase DNA.
Base editors cannot cut DNA.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the text, the author discusses the benefits of genetically editing animals' genes. How might gene editing improve food production and crop yields? Do you think there could be a downside to genetically editing plants or animals? Why or why not?
In the text, the author discusses editing human DNA to protect them from inherited genes. Do you think that humans' genes should be modified? Why or why not?

articles.title: On the Cultivation of Virtue, Woman's Work, and Politeness
articles.author: Zhao Ban
articles.description: China's first female historian shares some of the principles for women to serve their husbands.
articles.intro: Zhao Ban (c. 45 – c. 116 CE) was China's first female historian and author of influential books about how women should behave. These texts were so influential that, although they were written during the Han dynasty (206 BC-220 BCE), they were still read during the late Ming (1369-1644) and Qing (1644-1912) dynasties. Because this piece was written hundreds of years ago in another language, the sentence structure may be difficult to understand at first. Read the text carefully, paying attention to the punctuation and rereading lines when necessary.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the characteristics that Ban believes make up the “perfect” woman.
articles.permissions: On the Cultivation of Virtue, Woman's Work, and Politeness by Zhao Ban is in the public domain.

blocks.text: Chapter I: On the Cultivation of Virtue
All girls, everywhere,
First should learn to cultivate virtue.
Of cultivating virtue’s methods,
The most important is
To be pure and upright in morals;
If pure, you are clean inside and outside;
Chastity is your body’s glory;
Having it, all your acts shine.
When walking, look straight, turn not your head;
Talking, restrain your voice within your teeth;
Sitting, don’t shake your knees – common fault with men;
Standing, keep quiet your skirts;
When pleased, laugh not aloud;
If angry, still make no noise;
Inner and outer rooms’ duties
Fully understand.
Boys and girls must not together be.
With outside business you have no concern;
Therefore, go not beyond the court.
If necessary outside to go,
Exhibit not your form,
But screen your face with fan or veil.
To men who are not with you related you may not speak.
With women and girls of not careful conduct you may not associate.
Following virtue, decorum, and uprightness, you so accomplish the end of your being.
Chapter II: Woman’s Work – Weaving Silk, etc.
All girls, everywhere,
Should learn woman’s work.
In weaving cloth,
Distinguishing between the coarse and fine;
When sitting at the loom work carefully;
When boiling the silk cocoons,
Collecting for them the mulberry and chia leaves,
In all be very diligent.
Protect the worms from wind and rain.
If cold, warm them by the fire;
Keep them in a clean place;
As the young ones grow,
Transfer them to baskets, but crowd them not;
Provide them leaves, not too many nor too few.
Making silk, be careful of the straight and cross threads, so you will make a perfect piece.
Chapter III: On Politeness
All girls, everywhere,
Should learn woman’s work.
When women guests are expected,
You should the chairs arrange in order.
Let your own dress be neat and suitable.
Slowly and lightly walk;
Move not your hands about;
And let your voice be gentle and low.
With such deportment
Invite your guests to enter:
Present your salutations,
Inquiring after their welfare since last you met.
In conversation with them,
Talk not at random.
When they questions ask, or answer,
Give most polite attention.
In asking of their welfare.
Or talking of yourself, in a low voice speak;
The tea and refreshments carefully prepare.

options.label: Women should be virtuous, polite, diligent, and modest, and avoid associating with others who do not adhere to these standards.
Women should have self-confidence and pride when they are able to do things perfectly and carefully, and courage when they are faced with accomplishing a challenge.
Women should all have space and time to themselves, allowing them privacy, individuality, and independence.
Women and men should both strive to be morally upright, pious, and hard-working

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following best identifies a central idea of this text?

articles.title: Molecular scissors fix disease-causing flaw in human embryos
articles.subtitle: Scientists learned to use this gene-editing tool better to repair a gene that causes heart failure
articles.author: Tina Hesman Saey
articles.description: Tina Hesman Saey discusses research on gene editing in human embryos.
articles.intro: Not long ago, the idea of being able to edit our genes was the stuff of science fiction; however, this is no longer the case. In this informational text, Tina Hesman Saey discusses gene editing research conducted on human embryos.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how gene editing could impact genetic diseases.
articles.permissions: From Science News for Students , 2017. © Society for Science & the Public. Reprinted with permission. This article is intended only for single-classroom use by teachers. For rights to republish Science News for Students articles in assessments, course packs, or textbooks, visit: https://www.societyforscience.org/permission-republish .

blocks.text: Researchers used “molecular scissors” to mend a faulty gene in human embryos. These tests are the first of their kind in the United States. Scientists used an enzyme known as CRISPR/Cas9 as their scissors. It snipped out the faulty gene, which can lead to heart failure. With this tool, they fixed the problem in nearly three out of every four treated embryos (about 72 percent).

Such tinkering with an embryo’s DNA is known as germline editing. And it is controversial. Many people worry that it could be used to create so-called designer babies. Parents of these still-fictional designer babies might try to choose their child’s eye and hair color. Or they might have doctors change certain genes to make their child into a super-smart athlete.

In fact, the new study suggests such designer babies might be very hard to make. They would require injecting bits of new DNA to supply the desired traits. And in the latest tests, treated embryos rejected pieces of DNA that the researchers had added (in this case, to help fix the faulty gene).

The new work “is not announcing the dawn of the designer baby era,” says R. Alta Charo. She is a lawyer at the University of Wisconsin Law School in Madison. She also is a bioethicist, someone who studies the wisdom, morality or social value of certain decisions or policies. The researchers have not attempted to add any new genes or change traits, she notes. Here they sought only to repair one version of a disease-causing gene.
This research offers lessons in how to more accurately wield the CRISPR/Cas9 scissors. They reduced the share of embryos that were only partly edited.

That’s important if researchers want to use these gene scissors to cure genetic diseases, says Shoukhrat Mitalipov. He is a co-author of the new study. A reproductive biologist, he works at Oregon Health & Science University in Portland. If even one cell in an early embryo is unedited, he explains, “that’s going to screw up the whole process.” His team did its work in Korea, China and two U.S. states (Oregon and California).
They described their new achievements on August 2 in Nature .
Snip . . . then repair!
Mutations are changes that occur in a gene. Some mutations lead to disease. Others seem to cause no harm. In their new work, researchers wanted to fix a mutation in the gene known as MYBPC3.

Nearly every cell of the body has two copies of each gene. People who inherit just one mutant copy of MYBPC3 can develop a life-threatening heart problem. It’s called hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HY-per-TROW-fik KAR-dee-oh-my-AH-puh-thee). It can cause sudden death from heart failure. The heart problem affects about one in every 500 people worldwide. Mutations in the MYBPC3 gene are to blame for about 40 percent of cases. Doctors can treat symptoms of the condition, but there is no cure.
In the new study, researchers used sperm from a man who carries a flaw in the MYBPC3 gene. They injected that sperm into eggs from women with healthy copies of the gene.

The researchers also injected the gene editor into the egg. The editor has several pieces. One is the scissors, a DNA-cutting enzyme called Cas9. They also need a piece of RNA that directs the enzyme to the exact place in the gene where a cut should be made. The researchers injected another piece of DNA into the egg, too. The researchers had designed that hunk of DNA to serve as directions for repairing the breach that would be made by Cas9. But the cells ignored it. Embryos instead used the mother’s healthy copy of the gene to mend the cut.

That result surprised the researchers. Gene editing in other types of cells usually requires an added piece of DNA as a pattern to guide a repair, Mitalipov says. If all embryos reject added DNA repair patterns, researchers may struggle to fix mutations in embryos. It would be hardest when neither parent has a healthy copy of the gene. The finding also means that embryos may reject attempts to add new traits. That might mean designer babies would be hard to achieve.
Careful timing

Timing is important in these experiments. At first, the researchers added the scissors one day after fertilizing the eggs. Those experiments often produced patchwork embryos — ones having some unedited cells. Thirteen out of 54 treated embryos had some unrepaired cells. Affected eggs that were fertilized had probably copied the DNA before researchers had added Cas9, Mitalipov now suspects.

When researchers injected the cutting enzyme and sperm at the same time, this didn’t occur. And none of the resulting embryos showed any signs that Cas9 had snipped a cut in the wrong place. (One concern about gene editing is that “off-target” cutting could create errors in other parts of a cell’s DNA.) When an egg was treated at fertilization, this way, only one patchwork embryo occurred. It had repaired the MYBPC3 mutation in all of its cells. Some cells had used the mother’s DNA for repair. Other cells used the DNA template supplied by the researchers.

The new study is a step toward using gene editing to prevent genetic diseases, says Janet Rossant. She is a developmental biologist in Canada at the Hospital for Sick Children and the University of Toronto. Still, she warns, researchers have a lot more work to do before they can do gene editing in people. Doctors will need to be sure the technique will work well and every time, she says.

Researchers have edited human embryos before. But they were merely probing the earliest stages of human development. They also wanted to learn things about basic human biology. Mitalipov and his colleagues instead wanted to probe the use of gene editing in medicine. They want to improve the technique so that it would be safe and reliable enough to use in people. Such studies might one day involve editing embryos in the lab, then implanting these into a woman to establish a pregnancy.

In the United States, such human trials are not allowed. The Food and Drug Administration will not allow research meant to introduce changes in embryos that could be passed down to someone’s future children and grandchildren.

Researchers used improved gene editing techniques to repair a mutated gene that caused a genetic disease.

articles.title: Francisco Menéndez: An Unsung Hero
articles.author: Jane Landers
articles.description: Learn about the life and accomplishments of Francisco Menéndez.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Jane Landers discuss the life and accomplishments of Francisco Menéndez, who escaped slavery and never stopped fighting for his freedom.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Menéndez challenged English colonists in America.
articles.permissions: "Francisco Menéndez: An Unsung Hero" by Jane Landers, Footsteps , © by Carus Publishing Company. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Carus Publishing Company, d/b/a Cricket Media, and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: While many of the African American colonial heroes are little known today, records do tell of a Mandingo man from West Africa by the name of Francisco Menéndez. In 1715, Menéndez and other enslaved Africans joined the Yamassee Indian War against English colonists in South Carolina. When the war turned in favor of the English, the Yamassees and their African allies fled south to Spanish Florida. Why did Menéndez choose Spanish Florida for their new home? Spanish colonists held slaves, just as the English colonists to the north did, but Spanish law recognized slaves as human beings as well as property. Under "Untitled" by unknown is used with permission. the Spanish, slaves could buy their freedom or win it by brave deeds, such as military service. Spain’s state religion, Roman Catholicism, also encouraged non-Christians to convert to the “true faith.” This is why, in 1687, a group of slaves stole a canoe and escaped from Carolina to Florida. Once in St. Augustine, they requested Catholic baptism. Other slaves soon followed, and Florida’s governor asked Spain what to do. In 1693, King Philip II issued a decree “giving liberty to all... the men as well as the women... so that by their example and by my liberality others will do the same.” This decree changed Spanish policy and shaped the politics of the Southeast and the Caribbean for years to come. This decree gave Menéndez and his followers hope that they, too, could be free in Spanish Florida. Even before Menéndez was officially freed, the Spaniards named him captain of the 1. to change someone’s religious faith or beliefs 2. people from Spain Black Militia. Menéndez led his men in several battles against English forces that repeatedly attacked Spanish Florida. He also led expeditions back to Carolina to rescue other Africans from slavery. In 1738, Florida’s governor freed Menéndez and authorized him to establish a town named Gracia Real de Santa Teresa de Mose.

Menéndez and the new homesteaders of Mose wrote to the king of Spain to express their gratitude and vowed to be “the most cruel enemies of the English.” They also promised to risk their lives and spill their “last drop of blood in defense of the Great Crown of Spain and the Holy Faith.” Shortly after, England declared war on Spain, and, in 1740, English troops invaded Florida and occupied Mose. Menéndez’s troops helped retake Mose and the governor recognized their bravery in reports to the king. Menéndez, who was literate in Spanish (and probably in Arabic) wrote the king two letters recounting his military services. Hoping to travel to Spain to seek his reward in person, Menéndez became a corsair — a state-sponsored pirate. On one voyage, Englishmen captured him and sold him into slavery in the Bahamas. But by 1759, the unconquerable Menéndez had regained his freedom and the leadership of Mose. After Spain ceded Florida to England in the peace treaty of 1762, Menéndez and the people of Mose, along with other Floridians, relocated to Cuba. Once in Cuba, the Spanish gave each refugee land, food, tools, and an African slave. Menéndez and other black Floridians then helped establish a new town called San Agustín de la Nueva Florida (Spanish for: St. Augustine of New Florida). Harsh conditions on the Cuban frontier eventually drove Menéndez and his family back to the city of Havana, where he ended his days. Menéndez spent more than 50 years fighting for freedom and more than 40 years serving the king of Spain who had freed him. As more historians begin to track black history through Spanish documents, the stories of more unsung colonial heroes with stories similar to Francisco Menéndez’s will be uncovered. articles.title: Red Brocade
articles.author: Naomi Shihab Nye
articles.description: In this poem, the speaker discusses an Arab custom of welcoming strangers.
articles.intro: Naomi Shihab Nye is an American poet, editor, songwriter, and novelist. She was born to an American mother and Palestinian father. In this poem, the speaker discusses an Arab custom of welcoming strangers.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, make note of the details that support how the speaker treats a stranger.
articles.permissions: Poetry selection from Everything Comes Next by Shihab Nye - Read By: Naomi Shihab Nye, James Patrick Cronin Used by permission of HarperCollins Publishers.

blocks.text: The Arabs used to say,
When a stranger appears at your door,
feed him for three days
before asking who he is,
where he’s come from,
where he’s headed.
That way, he’ll have strength
enough to answer.
Or, by then you’ll be
such good friends
you don’t care.
Let’s go back to that.
Rice? Pine nuts?
Here, take the red brocade pillow.
My child will serve water
to your horse.
No, I was not busy when you came!
I was not preparing to be busy.
That’s the armor everyone put on
to pretend they had a purpose
in the world.
I refuse to be claimed.
Your plate is waiting.
We will snip fresh mint
into your tea.

options.label: People should welcome strangers.
People should offer everything they have to strangers.
Doing something different can lead to a great friendship.
Doing something for someone else will always lead to a reward.

articles.title: American Bards: a Brief History of the Poet Laureate of the United States
articles.author: Thomas Pool
articles.description: This text discusses the role of Poet Laureate in the United States and how it can connect Americans.
articles.intro: The United States Poet Laureate is the official poet of the country. During their term, the Poet Laureate uses their own unique style to raise interest in and appreciation for poetry in the U.S. This article discusses the role of Poet Laureate in the United States and how it can connect Americans to all the voices that make up our unique chorus.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the position of Poet Laureate developed throughout history.
articles.permissions: "American Bards: A Brief History of the Poet Laureate of the United States" by Thomas Pool. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: The Poet Laureate is an honor that is given to poets around the world. In fact, it might be more accurate to describe it as an honorific — a title given as a mark of respect with few or no duties. There are national Poets Laureate and state laureates and, in some places, even local Poets Laureate. The role of a Poet Laureate will differ from place to place, but it is always a title that is considered prestigious and esteemed.
The History of the Poet Laureate

The role of the Poet Laureate can be traced back to ancient Greece. The ancient Greeks held poets, playwrights, and philosophers in high regard and treated them as heroes. As such, they would be crowned with laurel wreaths, a symbol of divinity and honor. These heroes were known as laureates. The term was revived during the Renaissance-era in Europe, as the kings and queens of Europe looked to their ancient ancestors for inspiration. The first “modern” poet laureate was John Dryden, the first Poet Laureate of England, in 1668. These early poet laureates were chosen by their monarchs and were expected to write tributes to celebrate their kings and queens, and their realms. As democracy blossomed, so too did the poet laureates’ freedom of expression.

In the United States, the national Poet Laureate is appointed by the Library of Congress. The position was first established in 1937 when it was simply known as the Consultant in Poetry to the Library of Congress. The more prestigious title of “Poet Laureate of the United States” was established by Congress in 1985, after it was championed by the amateur poet and Hawaiian Senator, Spark Matsunaga. Although they are appointed by the government, the Poet Laureate is not paid by the government. Instead, the laureate receives a small stipend provided by a philanthropic endowment.

Many American poets scoffed at the idea of a Poet Laureate at first. They thought the role would be similar to the poet laureates of the past, and many disliked the idea of having to write odes to the President. Fortunately, this fear was entirely misguided. The Poet Laureate’s task is to simply raise the literary and poetic consciousness of the American people, and not to laud the government or the country’s leaders, as their Renaissance-era counterparts would have.

The position is a post of honor rather than one of responsibility. The Laureate has very few required duties, and the Library of Congress allows the poet to pursue their own projects and writing while they are in office. However, that does not mean that the Poets Laureate are — pun intended — resting on their laurels. Each Laureate has used their position in their own way to enhance America’s poetry in unique and engaging ways. Gwendolyn Brooks visited schools to teach children how to write poetry. Maxine Kumin established poetry workshops for women. Rita Dove brought together other writers and poets to explore the African diaspora. She also championed the poetry of children, and even recited a poem at the first state dinner of President Clinton.
The Poet Laureate today

Today, the national Poet Laureate is Joy Harjo. Harjo was appointed Poet Laureate of the United States in 2019 by the Library of Congress. She is the first Native American Poet Laureate. The signature project of her time in the position is “Living Nations, Living Words,” which seeks to connect the American public with the rich language of contemporary Native American poets. Harjo’s work follows in the footsteps of previous Poets Laureate, many of whom also sought to connect Americans with a diverse array of poetry and poets.

Since 1937, there have been many “first” Poets Laureate, each of whom used their talent and poetry to inspire and enlighten America. The first woman to serve in the role as Consultant was Louise Bogan, from 1945 to 1946, and the first African American Consultant was Robert Hayden. After 1985, when the role was formalized into Poet Laureate, the first woman to hold the role was Mona Van Dun, the first African American was Rita Dove, and the first Latino was Juan Felipe Herrera.

In 2016, the position of National Youth Poet Laureate was established. The program seeks to connect young people with poetry, in all its forms, whether written or spoken, sonnet or song. America’s first National Youth Poet Laureate was Amanda Gorman, who was only 19 at the time. Just four years later, Amanda Gorman would ascend the steps of the Capitol Building to recite her poem, “The Hill We Climb”, at the inauguration of President Biden. The poem went viral, it was read in living rooms and classrooms throughout the country. Amanda Gorman’s message of bravery in the face of adversity, of community over disunity, was a recent example of the power of poetry, delivered by a former Poet Laureate.
