articles.title: The Human Gift: an Introduction to the History of Poetry
articles.author: Thomas Pool
articles.description: This article chronicles the history of poetry and its uses from early human history to the present.
articles.intro: Poetry began in its oral form before written language was developed, making it one of the oldest human artforms. This article provides a brief look at some of the major Western poetic traditions and connects the "digital fire" of today's online life to the bonfires around which humans' first stories were told.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what historical events were important to each poetic movement.
articles.permissions: "The Human Gift: a Brief Introduction to the History of Poetry" by Thomas Pool. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: Poetry is thought to be the oldest form of human artistic expression. When asked to describe poetry, the writer Jorge Luis Borges once said, “Poetry is something that cannot be defined without oversimplifying it. It would be like attempting to define the color yellow, love, the fall of leaves in the autumn.”

Perhaps, in an oversimplified way, it can be best described as a deliberate arrangement of language in a way that compels someone to think and reflect. Poetry pre-dates the written word and recorded information, so we cannot really say when it began. It could be as old as mankind. The earliest forms of poetry were rhymes that were used as a mnemonic device, which means they helped assist people in remembering things. In a time before writing, a lot of knowledge was passed down to children through word of mouth, things like people’s family tree, how to hunt, or how to build shelters. This is known as the oral tradition. The first recorded poem, the one we have a written record of, is The Epic of Gilgamesh , the ancient Sumerian tale of creation and heroism. More familiar perhaps are Homer’s The Iliad and The Odyssey , the ancient Greek tales of the Trojan War and its heroes and villains. Poetry, like humanity, has evolved as societies developed and shaped themselves. Once written language was invented, poetry flourished as an artistic expression.
From Homer to Harlem and Beyond

The Greek word poetes means “maker.” The ancient Greeks were fond of poetry, and the most Western poetic traditions can trace their roots back to ancient Greece. The Greeks used poetry to recall their own ancient histories and to catalog their creation myths, the pillars of their religion. Homer was not the original author of The Iliad or The Odyssey , but they are attributed to him since he was the first to write them down. Poets were revered in ancient Greece, and as a symbol of their importance they would be crowned with laurel wreaths, which is where the term “Poet Laureate” comes from, a title that is still given to poets around the world today as a mark of respect. The Romans adopted Greece’s love of poetry, but after the fall of the Roman Empire, poetry in Europe largely declined in favor of religious texts. Meanwhile, poetry flourished in the Middle East and North Africa as well as in India, China, and Japan.

In 16th century England, William Shakespeare began to write his first sonnets. The Bard, as he was popularly known, revived the tradition of poetry in the English language. His sonnets, now known as Shakespearean Sonnets, contained 14 lines comprising three quatrains (four-line stanzas) with one rhyming couplet (two lines of verse). The sonnets were written in iambic pentameter, a type of rhythm, with ten syllables in each line, with the emphasis on the second syllable of each couplet. Shakespeare’s poetry is still read and recited today. His revival of English-language poetry marked a stark departure from the religiously influenced poetry of the Middle Ages, and started a trend that moved towards expressions of love, philosophy, and nature.

After Shakespeare came the Romantics. The Romantics mainly wrote during the late 1700s and the early 1800s. Among them were Lord Byron, Percy Shelley, John Keats, William Wordsworth, and Samuel Coleridge. Romantic poetry was revolutionary for its time. It focused on feeling and emotion, and how the poet saw themselves and their worlds. Its use of flowery, superfluous language is sometimes regarded as uptight and stuffy today, but was a remarkable embrace of human spirit at its height.

American poets redefined the artform throughout the 19th century. The Transcendentalist movement is the name given to a time when poets began to discuss the inherent goodness in human beings in their work. They rejected the old puritanical idealism of the individual in favor of the spirit of community. The themes in their work sought to capture peace and divinity in the everyday, as well as the beauty of nature. This was at a time when the United States was relentlessly pushing West through a series of bloody wars and forced resettlement. Henry David Thoreau, one of the most prominent Transcendentalists, was imprisoned over his refusal to pay taxes that supported the Mexican-American War. Transcendentalism became a defining American poetic tradition, with renowned writers like Emily Dickinson, Ralph Waldo Emerson, Walt Whitman, Louisa May Alcott, Nathaniel Hawthorne, and many others among their ranks.

After the First World War and the Great Migration, creativity and intellectualism flourished in the predominantly-Black neighborhood of Harlem, in New York City. Art, music, philosophy, literature, and poetry exploded during this era, which is now known today as the Harlem Renaissance. Alain Locke, the first African-American Rhodes Scholar and a contemporary of the Harlem Renaissance, called it the “spiritual coming of age” for African-Americans. Genre-defining poets like Langston Hughes, Claude McKay and Georgia Douglas Johnson are closely associated with the Harlem Renaissance. Their work often featured themes of Black pride, community, beauty and resilience among African-Americans. This was all at a time when many African-Americans were only a generation or two removed from slavery. Jean Toomer’s Cane , an experimental book that was part drama, part poetry and part fiction, is considered one of the most important works to emerge from this period.

The destruction of World War Two and the invention of atomic weaponry soured the mood of the new post-war generation of American poets known as “The Beat Generation.” This period is loosely described as lasting from the end of World War Two to the Vietnam War era. The Beats, or “Beatniks,” cloistered themselves mostly in New York City and San Francisco. While they were a small group, their work became some of the most influential poetry in the English language. Influenced by the freeform-style of jazz, the Beats refused the traditional rhyming schemes of English-language poetry. They experimented with words and language, and focused on meaning and understanding over things like rhyming and alliteration. Poets such as Lawrence Ferlinghetti, Jack Kerouac, and Diane Di Prima focused on free expression and a less conventional attitude redefined what it meant to be a poet in America. However, the most prominent Beat poet was Allen Ginsburg. Ginsburg’s 1955 collection of poetry, Howl , was critical of the consumer and hyper-militant culture of post-war America. Ginsburg and his fellow Beats changed the thinking and attitudes of millions of young Americans who felt equally dissatisfied with the state of the world and their country.
The Digital Fire

Poetry in the 21st century has absorbed and learned from the poetic traditions of thousands of years. Slam poetry, for instance, embraces the rhyming tradition that was foundational to poetry, and uses it to convey messages of importance, just as our ancestors did. Poets like Naomi Shahib Nye, Maggie Nelson, Ocean Vuong, Joy Harjo, Ada Limón, and Claudia Rankine defy the traditional structured forms as the Beats did, but like the Transcendentalists and the Harlem Renaissance writers, they also embrace the spirit of resistance, optimism, and bravery. Their work, focusing on injustice, community, and the failure of the American Dream has spoken to the experiences of millions.

Poetry has never been more accessible than it is today. Many amateur poets have found a voice on social media, and the more established figures have their poems widely accessible online thanks to organizations like The Poetry Foundation. The light of the digital fire burns bright — what stories with ancient lineages will you learn while huddled around it? Whatever you learn, remember it, keep it guarded and sacred – poetry is the most human of gifts.

options.label: Poetry became the most popular form of art once written language was developed around the world.
Poetry of the Shakespearean and Romantic eras was of a higher quality than the poetry written today.
Poetry was important in the past, but people have moved on to more modern artforms in the digital age.
Poetry has a long history and its role in society changes over time.

discussionQuestions.prompt: The Romantic and Transcendentalist movements focused heavily on an appreciation for the natural world and the feelings that it evokes. What is the most beautiful place that you have ever seen in nature? How did it make you feel? How do you think poetry and nature relate?
Do you think music is poetry? Why, or why not? Can you think of an example of a song that feels "poetic" to you? What are the qualities of the song that remind you of poetry?

In the digital age, poetry is more diverse in content and style — and more accessible to most people — than it has ever been at any point in human history. How does this impact which stories are told, how the stories are told, and how they are delivered to audiences? Does it matter who tells a story? Why, or why not?

The author compares our modern online experience to a "digital fire" that we gather round to share stories and learn histories. Think about your own online presence and the different communities in which you participate. Do you think the author's comparison is a valid one? Why, or why not?

articles.title: The End of Sleepovers
articles.author: Pamela Huber
articles.description: A Jewish girl feels uncomfortable when her Christian friend wants her to attend her church.
articles.intro: In this story by Pamela Huber, a Jewish girl named Ruth loves to have sleepovers with her Christian friend Kaylee.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Kaylee’s actions affect Ruthie.
articles.permissions: "The End of Sleepovers" by Pamela Huber. Copyright © 2023 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: It started with sleepovers.

Kaylee and I would have ballet class or piano lessons, and afterwards one of us would ask our mother to let us have a sleepover. Our moms, who were friends ever since they first met in a Mommy and Me class, always said yes. At age five, sleepovers meant playing with our dolls or brewing dirt and pebble potions in the yard until it grew dark. Then we’d huddle on the floor in our sleeping bags and exchange ghost stories until the moon rose to hush us.

Our moms traded hosting duties each month. Sometimes we slept on my living room floor, and sometimes we slept in Kaylee’s bedroom. Kaylee lived with just her mom – her parents separated when she was four, and her mom moved around a lot for work, so Kaylee didn’t see her dad often. I loved when they lived in an old farmhouse best. Kaylee shared the attic with her big sister. It was magical, with sloped ceilings and a staircase instead of a door; it reminded me of the rooms at the top of towers where princesses were hidden away. After my first sleepover at the farmhouse, I asked my parents why I couldn’t share a room with my big sister too. They rolled their eyes while my sister asked for a door lock to be installed.

By age eight, we had graduated from dirt and pebble potions to acting out our favorite scenes from musicals. We came up with our own dance routines and bonded over our shared love of the stage. Our talents were unequally distributed: Kaylee was a better singer and dancer and piano player than me because she got all that from her mom, but I was the superior actress. We conspired to go to Julliard together: she was going to sing; I was going to act.

Then one day during a sleepover at my house, I reached for the top shelf of my bookcase, grasping at a school acting award I had wanted to show off to Kaylee, when I tipped the whole case over. Kaylee managed to pull me from the wreckage. Kaylee’s mother insisted, after seeing my bruises, that all sleepovers thereafter had to be at her house. Mom resented that, but she couldn’t tear Kaylee and me apart if she tried. We were inseparable, friends since diapers, and we were convinced nothing would ever change that.

Shortly after we moved all of our sleepovers to Kaylee’s, her family moved out of the old farmhouse and into a practical, ranch-style home. Our after-school Friday night sleepovers moved to Saturdays. Fridays wouldn’t work anymore, Kaylee’s mom said, though she never explained why.

Switching to Saturday sleepovers meant we couldn’t sleep in on Sunday mornings, because Kaylee and her family went to church every week. That first Saturday night, they invited me to join them. Church made me uneasy, but that felt rude to say. I asked if they could drop me at home on the way there.
Sorry Ruth, but there’s no time , her mom said. But you can come to church with us if your parents want to sleep in .
And suddenly, I became the only Jew attending Kaylee’s Sunday School.

Kaylee’s family knew we were Jewish. So when they invited me to church each Sunday I slept over, I didn’t know how to feel. They told me not to worry because their church was a welcoming place for everyone. I didn’t need to be Christian to attend.

Mom asked me why I wanted to go. I explained it was the only way to spend time with Kaylee. She pursed her lips at that but didn’t want to press it with Dad right there, smiling at the idea that his side of their cold war over religion might win out. I figured if she cared more about me going, she’d get up early and pick me up. But all she said was, If Kaylee means that much to you, go to church so you can see her. What do I care?

Mom did all the stuff Jewish kids did in the ‘60s on Long Island. She had dinner with her parents every Friday night, she ate Ashkenazi dishes of liverwurst and gefilte fish, she respected her bubbe, she went to summer camp in the Catskills. She still knows all the Hebrew prayers and will chant them in a thick New York accent whenever she overhears them.

Except for respecting our grandparents, my sister and I did none of this. For one, we didn’t live near Mom’s family in New York; we lived further South, in Delaware, near Dad’s family. There weren’t many Jews where we grew up. But also, I think Mom didn’t want to fight with Dad over how to raise us religiously. She was a secular Jew who didn’t believe in God. My dad was a Christian who quietly did. It was easiest just to leave religion up to my sister and me to figure out on our own at a later date. Neither of us had ever set foot in a synagogue or church.

No matter what we believed about God, Mom made it very clear from a young age that we were Jewish. She tried explaining that while Judaism is the religion of the Jews, the Jews are also a people. This is your ethnicity, she said. We were born Jewish and we would die Jewish.
This confused me a lot growing up since we didn’t live near or know many Jews. My school never taught us what ethnicity meant either.
Sometimes Mom put it more bluntly: Hitler would’ve killed you no matter what you believed .

Church seemed cool because they had a rock band that Kaylee’s mom sang in. They even had a big tub they’d wheel out on stage for adults getting baptized for the first time. It reminded me of a dunk tank. I didn’t get to see much of the services because kids spent most of the time there in Sunday school. We sang songs and read stories. To make sure we were paying attention, the Sunday school teachers had us answer questions about the stories to receive angel bucks, play money that we could spend at the end-of-year Christmas market.

Our community had a one-track mind each December. Our Brownies Girl Scout troop took us caroling on Christmas Eve. I sang Christmas carols all through school choir and played Christmas songs that Kaylee’s mom taught me on the piano. In 4th grade, our teacher had Kaylee and I solve math problems to figure out where to glue certain pieces of Santa’s beard on a piece of paper. No one at school knew I was Jewish, and no one ever thought to ask. The kids who celebrated Christmas thought everyone celebrated Christmas.

I didn’t want to collect angel bucks that first Sunday because I wouldn’t be at the Christmas market. Kaylee and I only had sleepovers once a month. Even if I answered two questions right each time I went to church with Kaylee, at most I was looking at six angel bucks by December. I tried explaining this to Kaylee as I passed her the first angel buck I earned, but she told me to keep it. She said she thought I’d come more often than once a month if I wanted to be in the Christmas pageant.

Given my dreams of Broadway, a role in a pageant certainly caught my attention. A Christmas pageant was an unexpected opportunity, but I figured if I sang Christmas carols in the school choir anyway, what’s the difference? What harm could come from a few additional Sunday school sessions if it not only meant more time with Kaylee, but also that I got to be an actress, a phenomenon, a star?

Three months of rehearsals flipped by like photos in a stack: wanting to be Mary but getting passed over for Kaylee who’d put in her time years prior to earn the coveted role; begging not to be put in a fake beard playing a wise man; telling my mother that she had to make — not buy, make — me an angel costume.

While technically Jews do believe in angels, Mom was not happy, because first of all, she didn’t believe in angels, and second, she liked to joke I was no angel, and third, and most especially, because all the church moms were shocked she didn’t know how to sew.

I convinced myself that this was just another acting role. Singing and acting at the same time would be great practice for Broadway and Julliard. I told myself that playing the part of something I didn’t believe in was especially good acting.

Rehearsals for Kaylee’s Christmas pageant always came after Bible study, where we read stories about Noah and the Ark or Joseph and his jealous brothers from what Kaylee called the “Old Testament,” and which my people just call the Torah. I hated that they called the Torah “old.” In Sunday School, the teacher said the Old Testament was called that because it was meant to be replaced by the New Testament of Jesus.
Hearing that sent a chill through me. My people weren’t half-baked. We weren’t waiting to be finished — we were whole already.

The longer I spent in Sunday school, the more I identified with the Jews in the Torah stories. I wasn’t there for Jesus’s stories. I learned who he was. I learned how he flipped the money-lenders’ tables in the Temple, and I still didn’t care. He wasn’t for me.

Our sleepovers were soon taken over by Christmas pageant rehearsals and Sunday school. Some weekends, we didn’t even have a sleepover; Kaylee’s mom would just pick me up from my house and take me to church. Mom had politely declined invitations to church in the past when she first met Kaylee’s family. But now that I was going every weekend and the pageant was coming up, the invites picked up again. Mom took to honking her horn when she picked me up so she wouldn’t have to get out and deal with other adults telling her she should join the congregation.

One time, my big sister got roped into coming to church after a sleepover with Kaylee’s sister. She didn’t go to the little kid Bible study, but a different one for older kids. She never told me what happened, but she announced when we got home that she was never going back.

For years, our two families were linked: two mothers the same age, two sets of daughters the same age. Our friendships were tight and convenient until they weren’t. Mom and my sister began spending more time befriending the Rosens, a Jewish mother and daughter they met through my sister’s Girl Scouts troop. By the sleepover before dress rehearsals in December — when Kaylee brought up the Easter pageant — I was the only one of us three holding onto my friendship with Kaylee’s family at all.
The night before the pageant, as Kaylee and I played chopsticks on her piano, she started talking about the spring.

We can be in the Easter pageant together then, too. It’s for all the kids from Sunday school taking First Communion. Since you’ve come to all the classes, you can take communion with us, but first, you have to get baptized. You can’t take communion if you’re not baptized.

My hands froze mid-tune. I reminded Kaylee that I wasn’t Christian. I told her, Jews don’t get baptized . Mom fought tooth and nail with Dad’s parents to keep my sister and me from being baptized. No way would I undo her will for the sake of another acting credit. But even worse, Kaylee didn’t seem to realize the magnitude of what she was asking me to do. Communion wasn’t earning angel bucks. It was real religion. Hadn’t the whole point of this pageant been that we could spend more time together, and I could act, even though I didn’t practice her religion?

Kaylee insisted I had to take communion to be in the Easter pageant with her, that it was the culmination of a year of Bible Study, a graduation of sorts from the little kid Sunday school. I stood up and stepped away from the piano. I don’t want to be in your stupid pageant .
I called my mom to pick me up.
There would be no more sleepovers. Not when they left me feeling chipped away inside.

Once I blew up at Kaylee, Mom finally decided I was ready to hear what she thought of the whole church thing. While I had been focused on spending time with Kaylee and acting, Mom had been worried something like asking for a baptism would be at stake. She hadn’t wanted to insult Dad by digging up their baptism debate, so she had been keeping all her worries bottled up.

I asked Mom if I could keep my friend and my Jewishness. Kaylee had been my oldest friend, my swing buddy at recess, my first sleepover. But it felt like she only wanted to spend time with me doing church stuff, even when she knew I was Jewish.
Mom’s answer surprised me.

“Honey, I’ve been told I need Christ in my life by more people than I can remember. Even my best friend growing up, Nina, got on her hands and knees and begged me to convert so we could go to heaven together when we die.” When I asked if that bothered her, she said, “Of course, it bothered me. But I also knew that her sister, who wasn’t religious, had just died. And Nina was mourning and she thought she wouldn't get to see her sister in heaven. And we’ve been friends since we were kids, and she thinks of me like a sister, and in her grief, she was just trying to grasp onto something she could do to make it feel like she had a little control. And I couldn’t be mad at her for wanting to hold onto me when she felt she couldn’t hold onto her sister.”
“But you don’t believe in her heaven,” I said.

“I know. But I did believe in our friendship. So I told her we’d talk about it the next day after she calmed down, and we did. And I told her that what she’d said made me feel she didn’t respect me or my choices. I told her we couldn’t be friends if she did it again. And she was so embarrassed when I told her how she made me feel. She apologized, and she never did it again.”
“And she’s still your best friend?”
“And she’s still my best friend.”
“What if I tell Kaylee and it doesn’t fix anything?”
“Well Ruthie, at least you’ll have tried. If you lose a friend either way, wouldn’t you at least want to say you tried?”
When I told Kaylee I didn’t want to go to church with her anymore, she asked if that meant no more sleepovers.

I tried to explain I still wanted sleepovers and nights spent whispering ghost stories and days in the backyard acting out our favorite scenes from musicals. I wanted to hold onto it all for as long as we could. I didn’t want this whole church thing to come between us.
I chickened out from telling her how bad it made me feel to think she only wanted to be my friend if she could change me. So instead I asked if she could stay at my house on Fridays again.
She said she’d ask her mom. I don’t know if she ever did. But she stopped asking about sleepovers from then on. For a long time, I thought that was the end of our story.

By the end of the year, I quit ballet classes, switched piano teachers, and graduated out of our shared Brownies Girl Scout troop. By the time we moved on to different middle schools, Kaylee and I hardly kept in touch. She invited me to her birthday once, and I tried bonding with her new friends. But I didn’t fit into her life anymore. So we drifted apart.

I made new friends at my middle school. At the start of 6th grade, a popular girl invited me to a group sleepover. She was a dancer, with a smile so bright it hurt. It was early in the year, before we knew each other very well.

At the sleepover, we played with her bunnies and baked cookies and lip-synced to Britney Spears. We all went to bed early, or so I thought. But when they woke me in the night giggling, some deep part of me that was tired, and another deep part of me that was hurt to be left out of their laughter lashed out, yelling at them for waking me. The popular girls looked at me with saucer-wide doe eyes when I spat the word selfish at them. I’ll never know why I felt so self-righteous in that moment. Maybe I was just out of practice having sleepovers. Probably, I was really yelling at Kaylee.
I apologized the next day, blaming it on a nightmare they’d woken me from, but those girls never invited me to sleep over or sit with them at lunch ever again.
The last time Kaylee and I spoke was in high school when her father died suddenly of a heart attack.

There was never a question over whether my family would go to the funeral service. Mom wasn’t religious, but her Jewish values were the bedrock of our family, and in Jewish circles, no matter what falling out you’d had with someone, you showed up when it mattered most.

The service was in a church close by. Mom drove. It was my first time seeing an open casket. I approached Kaylee as she looked down on her father. He seemed to be sleeping. I didn’t recognize him; he’d been so absent from Kaylee’s life that I only knew him from pictures when she was a baby.
Kaylee didn’t look at me but I knew she knew I was there and that she trusted me because she started whispering, “He doesn’t even look like him. I just want to see the real him one last time.”

I didn’t know what to say. She’d spent so many years mad at her father, and now she just wanted one more moment with him. He’d been absent in her life; now he was absent in his death. Somehow I knew that despite all her faith in heaven, she didn’t want to hear how happy and at peace her father might be without her. So I reached out my arm and placed it around her. She leaned her head on my shoulder.
I’m sorry I let us lose touch , her weight seemed to sigh into my shoulder.
I’m sorry I couldn’t find the words to say how I really felt , my arms seemed to say as they supported her.

We were two kids trying to grow up, unsure how to enter this new world where relationships changed and people left our lives before we could accept their atonement. But what I realized at that moment was that friendship, like energy, is never created nor destroyed. It just changes form over time.

So we stood there, in silence, facing her father’s open casket. Seeing the difference between the losses we allow because they can be recovered and the ones that are final, which we can never forgive.

options.label: Change is an unavoidable part of relationships and growing up.
It is better not to discuss things that make us different with our friends.
Religion brings people together even if they practice it in different ways.
It is easy to find someone who will embrace you for who you are as you get older.

discussionQuestions.prompt: How have the friendships in your life changed over time? Have you ever had any friendships end? How did it make you feel? If you have not had any friendships end, do you think your feelings about their end may be more similar or different from Ruth's? Why?
What do you think Kaylee's family's motives were for inviting Ruth to church? Do you think Kaylee was a genuine friend to Ruth? Why or why not?
What are some challenges of being friends across lines of difference like race, gender, religion, or class? What can people do to be good friends to each other despite meaningful differences?
How do we change as we grow up? How does our understanding of who we are change as we grow up? How does this affect our friendships and other relationships?

articles.title: Harvey Milk Lives!
articles.author: Thomas Pool
articles.description: This text introduces the life and legacy of gay rights activist and politician Harvey Milk.
articles.intro: Harvey Milk (1930-1978) was the first openly gay elected official in California. An activist, community organizer, and politician, Milk fought for what he believed and encouraged others to do the same.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Harvey Milk dealt with challenges that he faced throughout his life.
articles.permissions: "Harvey Milk Lives!" by Thomas Pool. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0

blocks.text: Harvey Milk was a civil and human rights leader. He made history as the first openly gay man to become an elected official in the United States when, in 1977, he won a seat on San Francisco’s city council. Milk was an outspoken and unapologetic advocate for LGBTQ rights at a time when, in many places throughout the country, same-sex relationships were illegal. He inspired thousands and enraged thousands more who hated him for simply being gay. Milk was on the council for just eleven months when, in November 1978, he was brutally gunned down in an office at City Hall. Milk was just 48 years old when he died. If the assassin’s intention was to silence Milk, he failed. In his death, Harvey Milk continues to live on as an inspiration to others. Today, he is seen as a martyr in the gay community but also as a hero and visionary to all. His life has been the subject of countless biographies and documentaries, a movie has been produced and even an opera. There are schools and streets named after him. In 2009, President Obama posthumously awarded Milk the Presidential Medal of Freedom for his contributions to the gay rights movement.
The Beginning

Harvey Milk’s story begins in 1930, the year he was born into a Lithuanian Jewish family in Woodmere, New York, just outside of New York City. The Milks were a well-respected and well-known family in their community. Harvey and his older brother, Robert, worked in “Milks,” their family’s department store. As a youth, Harvey attended Bayshore High School, where he was known to play the class clown, and was often teased for having big ears and a big nose. But he also developed a wide range of interests in school, from playing football to singing in the school’s opera. By this time, Harvey knew he was gay.

He attended the New York State College for Teachers in Albany, now SUNY Albany, where he majored in math. After graduating from college, Milk enlisted in the U.S. Navy as a diving officer during the Korean War in 1951. Both of Milk’s parents had also served in the Navy during World War I.

After the war, in 1955, Milk was stationed at a naval base in San Diego where he trained other sailors as a lieutenant diving instructor. Back then, it was the official policy that gay men were forbidden from serving in the military. Suspicions began to arise about Milk’s sexuality; he was officially questioned over the matter. He resigned from the Navy and made his way back to New York City.

For the remainder of the ‘50s through the early ‘70s, Milk worked several different jobs. The 1960s and ‘70s were a time of great cultural upheaval and change. The ever-worsening Vietnam War, as well as the counter-cultural and sexual-liberation movements, changed everyday, American life. Harvey Milk became openly political during this time –– he protested against the Vietnam War and advocated for gay rights. It was during this pivotal period, when Milk was in his 40s, that he came out as gay. In 1972, Milk packed his bags and left New York for “The City by the Bay.”
Mayor of Castro Street

Milk moved to Castro Street, the heart of an emerging LGBTQ neighborhood in San Francisco. He opened a camera shop and lived above it. Milk’s outgoing and friendly personality quickly made his camera shop a hub of social activity on Castro Street. Many of the people who lived in and around The Castro neighborhood were diverse and open minded, but there were others who expressed hate or prejudice toward gay people. When a number of local businessmen tried to prevent a gay couple from opening a store on Castro Street, Milk and several other outraged business owners joined together to form the Castro Village Association. It was the first organization in the U.S. formed specifically to protect the interests of LGBTQ businesses. Recognizing his growing popularity and support, Milk ran in his first election for a seat on the Board of Supervisors, San Francisco’s city council, in 1972. Even though he lost, he earned a reputation as an outspoken supporter of his community and neighborhood.

In 1974, Milk and the new Castro Village Association organized the Castro Street Fair to attract more customers to LGBTQ businesses on Castro Street. The fair was a success, and Milk began to realize that his mobilization of the Castro Street community had turned the LGBTQ community into a political force that City Hall would have to recognize. Milk ran for the Board of Supervisors a second time in 1975. He narrowly lost again but found a friend in San Francisco’s mayor, George Moscone, who appointed Milk to the city’s Board of Permit Appeals, making him the first openly LGBTQ city official in the United States.

In 1977, Milk ran for the Board of Supervisors a third time and won handily. He was inaugurated as the first openly gay elected official in the U.S. on January 9th, 1978. His election made headlines across the country and around the world. But not everyone welcomed such progress.

California State Senator John Briggs, encouraged by widespread homophobia and hate, spearheaded a ballot initiative, California Proposition 6, that would fire every openly LGBTQ public-school teacher in the state. Using his newfound political capital, Milk was able to successfully push back against Briggs and defeat the initiative, at a time when homophobic laws were gaining ground elsewhere in the country.

In a passionate speech defending the American ideals of liberty and equality, Milk said: “Gay people, we will not win our rights by staying quietly in our closets... We are coming out to fight the lies, the myths, the distortions. We are coming out to tell the truths about gays, for I am tired of the conspiracy of silence, so I’m going to talk about it. And I want you to talk about it. You must come out.”
If a Bullet Should Enter My Brain

Milk was a skillful politician who excelled at building coalitions. During his brief tenure as a Supervisor, he brought together women’s groups, the Latino community, the Asian community, and the disabled community.

Milk’s election inspired hope for marginalized communities. Because of it, thousands of Americans, young and old, decided to come out to their loved ones. But for some, Milk’s election inspired anger.

Milk received countless death threats and always knew he might be killed for who he was and his beliefs. In his will, he wrote: “If a bullet should enter my brain, let that bullet destroy every closet door.”

On November 27th, 1978, Dan White — a former city supervisor, who was resentful after failing to get his job back — snuck into City Hall armed with a handgun. He made his way to Mayor Mosocone’s office and killed him. He then went down the hall to find Milk and assassinated him, too. Milk’s friend and colleague, then Supervisor Diane Feinstein, found Milk on the floor of his office. “I remember it, actually, as if it was yesterday,” Feinstein said of finding Milk. “And it was one of the hardest moments, if not the hardest moment, of my life. It was a devastating moment. For San Francisco, it was a day of infamy.”
A Grave Injustice

White, a former police officer, turned himself in at his old police station. He confessed to the murders of both men. At trial, White’s lawyer claimed that White could not be held responsible for his actions because he had consumed too much junk food earlier that day and it had clouded his judgement. This became known as the “Twinkie defense.” The jury consisted of white, middle-class San Franciscans who where mostly Catholic; gay people and ethnic minorities were excused from the jury pool. As the jury listened to White’s tearful recorded confession, some cried. At the end of the confession, interrogators thanked White for his honesty. White was convicted of manslaughter and not double murder. He received a sentence of only eight years in prison, of which he served just two.
The city erupted in anger over the ruling, in what became known as the “White Night Riots.” Police retaliated by raiding Castro Street, vandalizing gay businesses, and beating people in the street.

Shortly after Milk’s death, his nephew, Stuart Milk, whom Milk was very close with, came out as gay. Stuart was one of thousands of people who came out after Milk’s assassination, no longer willing to conceal their true and full selves. Protesters marched through Washington, D.C., demanding gay rights. As they marched, they chanted “Harvey Milk lives!”

According to a 2021 census report from the LGBTQ Victory Institute, there are nearly 1,000 elected LGBTQ officials nationwide — a 17% increase from the previous year. And that statistic only reflects the number of elected officials that have decided to come out publicly. There are likely many more LGBTQ people serving as elected officials who do not feel the need to come out publicly. Inside San Francisco’s City Hall sits a bust of Milk with a wide smile, the first non-mayor to have his likeness installed in the civic building. Milk paved the way, with his life, for the chance for people to serve their communities no matter whom they love. For that, he is remembered across LGBTQ, Jewish, marginalized, and activist communities as an American icon.

articles.title: Why are bees vanishing?
articles.subtitle: Scientists find a combination of threats that may explain declining honeybee populations
articles.author: Alison Pearce Stevens
articles.description: Scientists find a combination of threats that may explain declining honeybee populations.
articles.intro: Honeybees are an important part of Earth's ecosystem—they pollinate plants, which produce food. Over the past ten years, researchers have been trying to figure out why so many honeybee colonies are collapsing.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, find evidence to answer the following questions: Why are bees vanishing? How could this affect us?
articles.permissions: "Why are bees vanishing? " from societyforscience.org , © 2014, Society for Science. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.This article is intended only for single-classroom use by teachers. For rights to republish Science News for Students articles in assessments, course packs, or textbooks, visit: https://www.societyforscience.org/permission-republish .

blocks.text: The post office is buzzing as package after package of honeybees await delivery to their new homes. The tiny hooked feet of some worker bees cling to the screens on the sides of each wooden case. Other worker bees huddle around a small central cage containing their queen.

Sorting and delivering packages of live honeybees isn’t the favorite task of postal workers. Still, it is a job they have to handle more and more often. That’s because beekeepers in the United States and Europe have been losing bees to a mysterious condition known as colony collapse disorder, or CCD. Each mail-order package contains the seed of a new honeybee colony to replace one that has vanished.
“The bees appear fine in the fall,” says Michael Breed, a honeybee researcher at the University of Colorado at Boulder. “Then by mid-spring they’re simply gone.”

Breed has been working with these insects for 35 years. He has always ordered a few new bee colonies each spring. But since CCD began affecting the bees, he has had to order more and more each year. Before 2005, he never had a colony of bees simply disappear. Lately, it seems to happen all of the time. And when his colonies collapse, so do those maintained by neighboring beekeepers. The Northern Colorado Beekeepers Association now trucks in hundreds of packages of bees each spring to replace those that have vanished. Across the United States, up to one-third of the colonies kept by commercial beekeepers collapse each year, according to government surveys.

Exactly what’s causing CCD remains a mystery. Among the early suspects: parasites that infiltrate the hives, especially the bloodsucking Varroa (Vuh ROW uh) mite. Later, some scientists found evidence that assigned the blame to certain pesticides. Other biologists have linked the problem to infections, including some caused by viruses.

Scientists now suspect all three — parasites, pesticides and infections — combine to deliver a triple whammy. Pesticides first may weaken the bees. That leaves the insects too weak to survive diseases and pests that otherwise would not kill them. Earth’s changing climate worsens things, Breed notes. A changing climate can bring droughts or flooding that affect the availability of flowers on which bees depend. This makes bees more vulnerable than ever.

Even these threats may not capture the whole picture. Worker bees do many jobs in the hive: Nurse bees tend larvae. Forager bees gather food. A small number of guard bees protect the hive entrance from honey thieves. And some bees patrol the hive, scouting for sick and dying bees. These “undertaker” bees cart off the dead, dropping their bodies outside the hive. If the insects were just becoming deathly ill, beekeepers should find the evidence near the hive. The bees wouldn’t just vanish.
But they have been.
Too much ‘noise’

Another explanation for the collapse of so many colonies is that the bees are getting lost. Christopher Connolly thinks they may be forgetting their way home. A neuroscientist at the University of Dundee in Scotland, Connolly studies bee brains.

Connolly is especially interested in how pesticides affect those wee brains. Honeybees can encounter pesticides in different places. People treat hives where bees live with chemicals to kill Varroa mites. Farmers and gardeners treat the crops and flowering plants that bees feed on with chemicals to kill insects and other pests. Even the sugary corn syrup many beekeepers feed their bees over winter can contain traces of the pesticides that farmers had applied to growing corn.

In most cases, bees contact only tiny amounts of these poisons. Normally these exposures would be too small to kill them. Still, even tiny amounts will move throughout a bee’s body. About one-third will reach its brain. And that may be enough to confuse the bee, Connolly says.

The part of the bee brain responsible for learning and memory is called the mushroom body (named for its mushroom-like shape). When cells here receive information — about the location or scent of a flower, for instance — they “talk” to other cells. It’s through these chemical conversations in its brain that a bee learns a flowery scent means nectar is available. Or it may learn that a certain landmark means home is close by. The bee responds by zooming in on its target.

Of course, the brain chatters using not sounds but chemical signals. Chemical messengers shuttle back and forth to relay these signals. Scientists refer to these messenger chemicals as neurotransmitters. They’re the “language” by which one nerve cell in the brain talks to a neighbor. Once a message has been received, an enzyme between the nerve cells gobbles up the neurotransmitter. That way the cells won’t have to “listen” to an old message.
Connolly set out to discover how pesticides affect those conversations between brain cells.
Where the message gets lost

He started the study by selecting three common pesticides: one used to kill Varroa mites, and two known as neonicotinoids (Nee oh NICK uh tin oydz). Farmers and gardeners often use these last two, called neonics for short. One reason: They are less toxic to people than many other pesticides are.

Connolly then removed the brains from honeybees and bumblebees and put those brains in a watery bath. He inserted a tiny, needlelike probe into a cell in the mushroom body of each brain. This probe recorded electrical signals.

Electrical pulses emerge every time a nerve cell receives a message from its neighbor. The cell then prepares to relay that information to the next cell. (It’s a bit like the game of “telephone,” where children pass along a message with a whisper. Only in this case, the nerve cells share their message by releasing a messenger chemical.) Each electrical pulse Connolly detected indicated that the probed cell was chatting with a neighbor.
He then tested each of the three pesticides individually, adding a small amount to a bee brain’s bath.

With the neonics, he exposed each bee’s brain cells to about as much as the insect might encounter while foraging on plants treated with the pesticide. And the tests showed that even very low levels of neonics caused the brain cells to become overly chatty.

It’s as if all the cells in the brain decided to talk at once, Connolly explains. Just as you might miss information directed at you in the midst of a noisy crowd, the bee’s brain cells might miss an important message about the location of food or a landmark.

The pesticide used in beehives to kill mites only made the problem worse. It stopped the enzymes from doing their job. So not only did mushroom-body cells find themselves in the midst of endless crosstalk, but the enzymes did nothing to hush the old messages. That made the bee’s brain even noisier.

Amidst that racket, a bee could miss important information, Connolly thinks. Similar to the way a distracted driver may miss a turn, these bees may miss landmarks pointing the way home. And this, the scientist says, could explain the mysterious disappearance of entire colonies of honeybees. One by one, bees get forever lost. And every lost bee is one more that fails to bring food home to its colony.
Disappearing scent trail

As if pesticides, parasites and infections weren’t enough, honeybees face another serious threat. Experts from the University of Southampton, England, discovered that air pollution from cars and trucks can erase the scent that bees follow to find food. Foraging honeybees locate most flowers by smell. In fact, that’s why flowers smell good — not for our enjoyment, but to help lure pollinators. Each flower’s scent is a complex mix of released chemicals.

Honeybees use the whole mix of odors to find a preferred type of flower. When some share of the chemicals disappear, bees no longer recognize what is left of the starting scent. It’s like trying to recognize the smell of a pepperoni pizza just from its dough. As a result, the trail that bees had been following to locate food vanishes.

Pollution from cars and trucks can partially erase a flower’s scent, Robbie Girling and his team now show. They traced the problem to diesel exhaust. Their new findings appeared Oct. 3 in the journal Scientific Reports . With bees no longer able to recognize a flower’s scent, they may miss food. This can leave a colony hungry, they conclude — even if the nectar foragers do make it home.
More than just honey

Losing honeybees means more than just a world without honey. These insects play a major role in producing all kinds of foods, including berries, apples, almonds, melons, kiwis, cashews and cucumbers. That’s because honeybees move pollen between flowers. This fertilizes plants. Without this pollination, many plants won’t produce fruit. Bees also pollinate crops used to feed livestock. Fewer bees could therefore mean less of many different foods at the grocery store, including meat and dairy.

Pollination is so important that many farmers rent bees. Once crops start blooming, beekeepers truck in commercial hives to let the bees do their work. In agricultural states such as California, vanishing honeybee colonies may pose a serious threat to crop fertilization and the food supply.

However, research by Rachael Winfree suggests that disappearing honeybees might not hurt all farmers equally. An ecologist, she works at Rutgers University in New Brunswick, N.J. In her state, farmland is often located near habitats that support other, wild pollinators.

Fruit plants visited by a diverse mix of pollinators produce more fruit than those visited by just a few species, Winfree has found. Particularly important are wild bees. These are the natives that beekeepers can’t control. Some wild bees will even pollinate flowers that honeybees can’t. A bumblebee’s vibrating belly, for example, does a better job than honeybees of pollinating cherry tomatoes.
Nor are bees the only pollinators. Some moths, bats and other critters help move pollen as well.
Other bees not safe from pollution

The world is home to more than 20,000 species of bees. North America alone boasts about 4,000. Those species of native bees all pollinate plants. However, none of the world’s seven honeybee species come from North America. Those now found there originally came from Europe: Settlers brought them in the 1600s to guarantee a source of wax and honey.

Of course, native bees face pesticides, diseases and other pressures too. The fate of these wild bees remains largely unknown. Certainly, many native bees encounter widely used pesticides, including neonicotinoids. If bumblebees reflect the risks faced by North America’s other native bees, then “many species might be declining,” Winfree says.

In June, for instance, bumblebees rained out of flowering trees at a parking lot in Wilsonville, Ore. Rich Hatfield investigated. He’s a biologist with the Xerces (ZER sees) Society. His group is dedicated to protecting bees and their relatives. What Hatfield found shocked him. “I walked into a parking lot littered with dead bodies,” he recalls.

The trees had been sprayed with a neonicotinoid pesticide, he learned. Hatfield estimates that more than 50,000 bumblebees died in just this one incident. That’s as many bees as live in about 300 colonies, he says.

Bumblebees are even more susceptible to neonics than honeybees, Connolly has found. That likely explains why only bumblebees died in the Wilsonville incident. Still, all bee brains have mushroom bodies with cells that can be overwhelmed by the noise induced by neonics.
These pesticides represent just a small share of the many types sprayed on crops, flowers and other plants.

Even chemicals not intended for plant use can harm bees if flowering plants are located nearby. In September, for example, several honeybee colonies died in Minneapolis, Minn., after being exposed to the pesticide fipronil. Experts at the University of Minnesota believe the chemical was applied to the foundation of a building. The chemical appears to have tainted nearby plants that had been blooming.
How such chemicals affect bumblebees and other native bees remains unknown, says Connolly. How harmful other chemicals might be to their brains may vary widely, he says.

The vast majority of native bees are solitary. That means they don’t live in colonies. That makes them harder to study. Yet scientists know that even solitary bees need to navigate. They need to remember where the best food is. And females need to find their nests so that they can supply their young with food. Lost or confused native bees may mean fewer and fewer bees over time. That would mean less diversity in the animals available to pollinate crops. And as Winfree’s work suggests, that too might diminish our food supply.
Recommendations
While scientists search for pesticides that are safe for wildlife, people and bees, the rest of us can support bees at home — even in the middle of a city.

All four researchers suggest planting native flowers and leaving untended areas in our yards and gardens. Native bees readily nest in such areas. That helps ensure more pollinators will be around the next year. The experts all recommend avoiding the use of pesticides around our homes. The best way to do this is by using integrated pest management. This approach can be effective and good for the environment.

Pesticides won’t go away completely. They ensure that pests won’t destroy the crops on which people depend for food. But, “killing bees and other insects is not justified just to have pretty flowers,” Connolly argues. Allowing insects to eat our garden plants can provide them with a lifeline. And that lifeline might also extend to us, if it helps protect the pollinators on which our food supply depends.

articles.title: Giant Volcanoes Lurk Beneath Antarctic Ice
articles.subtitle: The expanse of buried volcanoes raises questions about the future of the ice sheet
articles.author: Beth Geiger
articles.description: Beth Geiger discusses 91 volcanoes discovered below the Antarctic ice sheet.
articles.intro: The Antarctic ice sheet is the single largest mass of ice on Earth, and below it 91 new volcanoes have just been discovered. In this informational text, Beth Geiger explores how the volcanoes and the ice sheet above them could interact in the future.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how these volcanoes could contribute to rising sea levels.
articles.permissions: From Science News for Students , January 5, 2018. © Society for Science & the Public. Reprinted with permission. This article is intended only for single-classroom use by teachers. For rights to republish Science News for Students articles in assessments, course packs or textbooks, visit: https://societyforscience.org/permission-republish

blocks.text: Lurking beneath Antarctica’s ice are 91 volcanoes that until now no one knew existed. This may be one of the most extensive volcanic regions on Earth. The discovery isn’t, however, just a fun factoid about the planet’s southernmost continent. It’s got scientists wondering just how active these volcanoes are. For instance, their volcanic heat might speed the shrinking of Antarctica’s already endangered ice.

Max Van Wyk de Vries is an undergraduate geology student at the University of Edinburgh in Scotland. He was curious about what Antarctica looked like under all its ice. He found data on the internet that described the underlying land. “I wasn’t really looking for anything in particular when I first started,” he recalls. “I was just interested in seeing what the land looked like under the ice.”

But then, he says, he started seeing familiar-looking cone shapes. Lots of them. Cone shapes, he knew, are typical of volcanoes. He looked more closely. Then he showed them to Andrew Hein and Robert Bingham. Both are geologists at his school.
Together, they confirmed what Van Wyk de Vries thought he saw. These were 91 new volcanoes hiding beneath ice as much as 3 kilometers (1.9 miles) thick.

Some peaks were large — up to 1,000 meters (3,280 feet) high and tens of kilometers (at least a dozen miles) across, says Van Wyk de Vries. “The fact that there was a large number of undiscovered volcanoes in Antarctica that had escaped attention was honestly surprising to all of us, especially given that many of them are huge,” he notes. Small bumps on the ice mark the site of some buried volcanoes, he says. No surface clues, however, reveal the existence of most of them.
The team described its findings last year in a Geological Society of London Special Publication.
Volcano hunters

Previous scientific studies in the area had focused on the ice. But Van Wyk de Vries and his colleagues looked instead at the land surface beneath the ice. They used an online data set called Bedmap2. Created by the British Antarctic Survey, it combines different types of data about Earth. One example is ice-penetrating radar, which can “see” through the ice to reveal the shape of the land below.

The geologists then cross-checked the cone shapes they had spotted with Bedmap2 against other types of data. They used several methods that can help confirm the presence of a volcano. For example, they studied data showing the density and magnetic properties of the rocks. These can give scientists clues to their type and origins. The researchers also looked at images of the area taken by satellites. In all, 138 cones matched all of the criteria for a volcano. Of those, 47 had been identified earlier as buried volcanoes. That left 91 as brand new to science.

Christine Siddoway works at Colorado College in Colorado Springs. Although she studies Antarctic geology, she did not take part in this project. The new study is a great example of how online data and images can help people make discoveries in inaccessible places, Siddoway now says.

These volcanoes are hidden beneath the vast, slowly moving West Antarctic Ice Sheet. Most lie in a region called Marie Byrd Land. Together, they form one of the planet’s largest volcanic provinces, or regions. This newfound province stretches across a span as large as the distance from Canada to Mexico — some 3,600 kilometers (2,250 miles).

This mega-volcanic province is likely associated with the West Antarctic Rift zone, explains Bingham, an author of the study. A rift zone forms where some of the tectonic plates of Earth’s crust are spreading or splitting apart. That allows molten magma to rise toward Earth’s surface. That in turn can feed volcanic activity. Many rifts around the world — such as the East African Rift zone — have been linked with active volcanoes.

Lots of molten magma marks a region that could produce plenty of heat. Just how much, though, is not yet known. “The West Antarctic Rift is by far the least known of all of Earth’s geologic rift systems,” notes Bingham. The reason: Like the volcanoes, it’s buried beneath thick ice. In fact, no one is even sure how active the rift and its volcanoes are. But it is surrounded by at least one gurgling, active volcano sticking above the ice: Mount Erebus.

Van Wyk de Vries suspects the hidden volcanoes are pretty active. One clue is that they are still cone-shaped. The West Antarctic Ice Sheet is slowly sliding toward the sea. Moving ice can erode underlying landscapes. So if the volcanoes were dormant or dead, the moving ice would have erased or deformed that characteristic cone shape. Active volcanoes, in contrast, constantly re-build their cones.
Volcanoes + ice = ??
If this region hosts a lot of live volcanoes, what might happen if they interact with the ice above them? The scientists don’t yet know. But they describe three possibilities in their study.
Perhaps the most obvious one: Any eruptions could melt the ice sitting above. With Earth’s climate warming, melting Antarctic ice is already a huge concern.

Melting ice raises sea levels around the globe. The West Antarctic Ice Sheet is already crumbling around its edges, where it floats on the sea. In July 2017, for example, a chunk of ice the size of Delaware broke off and drifted away. (That ice didn’t raise sea levels, because it was sitting on top of water. But its loss makes it easier for ice on land to flow into the sea where it would raise sea levels.) If the whole West Antarctic sheet melted, sea level would rise at least 3.6 meters (12 feet) worldwide. That’s enough to flood most coastal communities.
Individual eruptions, though, probably wouldn’t have much effect on the whole ice sheet, says Van Wyk de Vries. Why? Each would be just one small point of heat under all that ice.

If the whole volcanic province is active, however, that would create a different story. High temperatures over a large region would melt more of the base of the ice. If the melt rate was high enough, it would carve channels along the bottom of the ice sheet. Flowing water in those channels would then act as a powerful lubricant to speed the ice sheet’s motion. Faster sliding would send it out to sea sooner, where it would melt even faster.
Measuring temperatures at the base of an ice sheet is quite hard, notes Van Wyk de Vries. So it’s hard to tell how warm the volcanic province is, beneath all that ice.

A second possible impact of all of those volcanoes is that they might actually slow the flow of ice. Why? Those volcanic cones make the land surface under the ice bumpier. Like speed bumps in a road, those cones might slow the ice, or tend to “pin” it in place.

A third option: Ice thinning due to climate change might work to trigger more eruptions and ice melting. Ice is heavy, Bingham notes, which serves to weigh down Earth’s rocky crust below. As an ice sheet thins, that pressure on the crust would diminish. This reduced pressure might then “uncap” magma inside the volcanoes. And that could trigger more volcanic activity.

This has, in fact, been seen on Iceland. And there is evidence it could happen in Antarctica too, Bingham adds. It looks like exposed volcanoes like Mount Erebus erupted more often after the last ice age, when the ice thinned. Van Wyk de Vries thinks we can expect a repeat. “This almost certainly will happen as the ice melts,” he says.

But exactly what will happen, and where, is complicated, he adds. Buried volcanoes may behave differently in different parts of the ice sheet. Researchers may find all three effects — melting, pinning and erupting — at different spots. That will make predicting the overall impacts especially tough. But at least now scientists know where to look.

options.label: It is common for volcanoes to hide underneath ice sheets, and they usually remain inactive due to the weight of the ice.
If the volcanoes under the West Antarctic Ice Sheet erupted, it would be because humans thinned the ice sheet.
While the volcanoes under the West Antarctic Ice Sheet appear to be active, they are buried too deep to have an effect if they erupt.
The activity of the volcanoes underneath the West Antarctic Ice Sheet will affect the ice sheet as a whole, but the exact impacts are unknown.

to encourage readers to think about the environment and the greater ramifications for the Earth's warming

to show how scientists learn about places that they can't reach through collecting data and new technology

articles.title: Cool Jobs: Reaching Out to E.T. Is a Numbers Game
articles.subtitle: Scientists are using math to find aliens — and talk to them
articles.author: Ilima Loomis
articles.description: Ilima Loomis discusses how scientists use math to look for alien life.
articles.intro: Scientists in the search for extraterrestrial intelligence, also known as SETI, are using math to aid in their search. In this informational text, Ilima Loomis discusses the different ways that scientists use math.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how scientists use math to help them construct and send messages into space.
articles.permissions: From Science News for Students , 2017. © Society for Science & the Public. Reprinted with permission. This article is intended only for single-classroom use by teachers. For rights to republish Science News for Students articles in assessments, course packs or textbooks, visit: https://societyforscience.org/permission-republish

blocks.text: On November 16, 1974, astronomers at the Arecibo radio telescope in Puerto Rico broadcast a powerful signal into outer space. Aiming their transmitter at a star cluster on the edge of our galaxy, they sent out a series of pings — 1,679 of them, to be exact. Why that number?

They knew 1,679 was unusual. It is the result of multiplying 23 by 73. Each is a prime number, a type divisible only by one and itself. The product of this equation would be unlikely to occur in nature. So the scientists hoped that if any aliens intercepted their broadcast, the number would show them that the pings were meant to be an intended signal. It might then help them decode the hidden message that those pings had contained (including pictures of DNA, the solar system and a stick figure).

Searching for aliens may sound like science fiction. Yet for many scientists, it has become serious business. Here we meet three who are using math in their quest to find other living beings in our universe. One is calculating the likelihood of finding life on other planets. Another is trying to figure out where best to beam a “hello” to E.T. The third is looking for a common language with extraterrestrials — and it will likely be numbers.
If we could talk to the aliens

Douglas Vakoch has spent a lot of time thinking about what he’d like to say to E.T. He is president of METI International in San Francisco, Calif. (METI stands for Messaging Extraterrestrial Intelligence.) His group is focused on broadcasting signals to outer space in the hope of contacting a civilization on some other world. Vakoch wants to use bright lights, such as lasers or perhaps a powerful radio telescope like the one at Arecibo (Air-eh-SEE-boh). But the big question: How could he write a message that aliens would understand?
“We don’t expect the extraterrestrials to be speaking English or German,” Vakoch explains. “So we look to mathematics as a universal language.”
The idea is simple. You need to understand math to build things. Any world advanced enough to have the technology to pick up our signals should also know how to work with numbers.

It’s not a new idea. Back in the 1820s, when astronomers still thought there might be little green men living on the moon, they suggested using geometry — the math of shapes — to communicate with them.

One scientist suggested planting trees or using mirrors to draw an enormous triangle in Siberia, a part of Russia. Another proposed digging a giant trench in the shape of a circle and filling it with kerosene. Then someone would light it on fire at night so that it would be visible from space. For these scientists, math was a way to show the aliens not only that we were here, but also that we were intelligent.

Vakoch’s plan is a little closer to what the Arecibo scientists tried in 1974. Back then, they used a binary system: two signals at slightly different frequencies. By sending out the signals in a series of bursts that form a pattern, scientists could create a kind of code, or draw pictures. The Arecibo team used its code to send a dense message. It included pictures.
Vakoch would start with something simpler: counting.

His first message would be seven signals at the same frequency: “ping-ping-ping-ping-ping-ping-ping.” Next, he’d send seven signals again but using two frequencies, like this: “ping-pong-pong-pong-pong-pong-ping.” He’d repeat that sequence four more times, then finish with seven “pings” again. If you draw that pattern on a piece of paper, you’ll see what the aliens will see if they decode his message: a box.

Next, Vakoch would add a third frequency to the code. By dropping in the third frequency at different places in the box, he could count numbers up to 25. By using a binary system — a way of representing numbers by combining zeros and ones — he could count into the millions. (The binary system is commonly used here on Earth. It can be found encoding the data in every computer.)

Once he introduced the code, Vakoch could then use it to send information. For instance, he might try to transmit the periodic table of the elements. It would list chemicals by their atomic numbers. This would show the aliens that we understand what the universe is made of. Another message might contain the Fibonacci sequence . This is a series of numbers that increase, with each successive number being the sum of the two before it. It’s a pattern that commonly appears both in nature and human art.

Even though he’s speaking in numbers, Vakoch wants to do more than count at the aliens. For him, math is just a tool to establish more meaningful communication. In the end, he says, “I want to know something about their culture, their society, their value system and what they see as beautiful.”
Doug Vakoch of the group METI International proposes creating a code using three frequencies of radio signals to transmit messages including the periodic table of elements.
Stay on target
So you want to talk to an alien. Just point your transmitter at the nearest star system and press “send,” right?

Wrong, says Philip Lubin. He’s a physicist at the University of California, Santa Barbara who works on directed-energy systems. These are powerful lasers that could be used to flash signals at other stars. While radio signals spread out as they travel across space, lasers are tightly focused. That means it’s important to aim them precisely. Being off by just a few degrees to either side could cause the signal to miss its target.

Big as a star is, hitting it with a laser is not easy. For one thing, when you look at a star in the sky, you’re seeing light that has been traveling through space for years — maybe thousands of years. “What you see is where the star was,” Lubin says. But its light was traveling to Earth, the star has moved. So you have to project your message into the direction where you think that star will be when your message is due to arrive.

And don’t forget, it will take years for the light from Lubin’s lasers to travel through space in the other direction. And that star is still moving. “It’s like taking a flashlight and trying to shine it at a spacecraft flying by,” he says. “If you want to shine your flashlight at it and have it hit it, you have to know something about the trajectory of the spacecraft.”

Astronomers use math to determine proper motion — a measurement of how objects in outer space change their apparent position in our sky. To do this, the scientists calculate the object’s angle relative to Earth. Next, they figure out how fast it’s moving and in what direction.

Many astronomical objects are so distant that those angles are measured in arcseconds or even smaller milli-arcseconds. Each are tiny amounts describing angles that are less than one degree in size. By calculating proper motion, Lubin can figure out where a star system will be when his signal arrives. “You have to figure out not only where the star is now, but where it will be in the future,” he emphasizes.
Is anybody out there?

For many scientists, trying to communicate with aliens is jumping the gun. They are asking more basic questions: Are we alone in the universe? What are the odds that life exists anywhere else? These scientists use math to figure out whether Earth is likely to be a lonely outpost in space, or one of many inhabited worlds in a universe teeming with life.

More than 50 years ago, astronomer Frank Drake devised an equation to estimate the number of extraterrestrial civilizations whose signals we might pick up from Earth. To get this number, he multiplied many factors. These included the rate at which new stars form, the number of stars with planets that host life and the number of life-bearing planets where that life would be intelligent. Just one problem: Almost all of the variables in this now-famous “Drake Equation” are still unknown.

“It’s not an equation that you can make predictions with,” says Avi Loeb. “It’s an equation that summarizes what we don’t know.” Loeb is a physicist at Harvard University in Cambridge, Mass. He decided to look at the search for extraterrestrial life from a different perspective. Instead of asking how much life exists in the universe, he wanted to know when in the history of the universe life would be most likely to develop.

For this, Loeb developed an equation of his own. It looks at different types of stars, the rate at which they form and how long they live. When he crunched the numbers, Loeb came up with a surprising conclusion: In the scale of cosmic time, the glory days when the universe is full of life might still be far ahead of us.

Many scientists had assumed that life most likely would occur in star systems similar to our own. After all, we know our sun can support life. If life exists elsewhere in the universe, sun-like stars are probably where we would find it, right?

Those sun-like stars usually burn out after some 6 billion years, Loeb knew. Yet there are stars that live longer. Some very small ones can survive for around 10 trillion years! And many of these small stars have planets. Might these planets also support life?

“If the answer is yes, then we know we [on Earth] are premature,” he says. Stars like our sun burn out quickly. So when our sun and its kin are gone, “the life that will remain is life around low-mass stars,” he argues. These are those tiny stars.

Unlike the Drake Equation, Loeb’s math contains only one unknown variable: whether low-mass stars can host life. He hopes other scientists will investigate that question in the decades ahead. “Once we know that, it can be folded into my equation,” he says.

Scientists in the search for extraterrestrial intelligence, or SETI, know they are unlikely to meet a Vulcan or Klingon in their lifetime. Still, they are excited to explore our universe for signs of life. Whether they’re figuring out the odds that we’re alone, or writing messages to aliens and beaming them out to other worlds, they couldn’t carry out this search without turning to numbers.

options.label: Math plays a vital role in scientists' search for alien life as it helps them determine where and when life might exist and communicate with it.
Math is a language that everyone understands on Earth, and it is likely that math is the foundation of all alien languages as well.
While some scientists rely on math to contact aliens, it plays a larger role in general space exploration by helping astronomers get to space.
While searching for alien life with math is exciting, scientists should be focusing on finding any form of alien life, not just intelligent ones.

"'It's not an equation that you can make predictions with,' says Avi Loeb. 'It's an equation that summarizes what we don't know.'…he wanted to know when in the history of the universe life would be most likely to develop?" (Paragraph 24)

"Whether they're figuring out the odds that we're alone, or writing messages to aliens and beaming them out to other worlds, they couldn't carry out this search without turning to numbers." (Paragraph 30)

to show the importance of numbers in exploring space and potential alien life
to reveal why scientists have failed to find alien life using math
to prove that math is the foundation of our world and other potential worlds

questions.prompt: What is the author's main purpose in the text?

options.label: It shows how Loeb's equation depends on the use of the Drake Equation to determine both time and place for alien life.
It emphasizes the importance of identifying the likelihood of alien life before attempting to contact it.
It reveals how math guides scientists to get closer to finding the possibility of alien life, one variable at a time.
It shows how scientists' contact with alien life relies on Loeb's equation as it determines when aliens will exist.
