articles.title: The Stone Boy
articles.author: Gina Berriault
articles.description: A boy handles tragedy in an unexpected way after accidentally killing his brother.
articles.intro: Gina Berriault was an American short story writer. She is known for her use of main characters who are unable to express themselves or act in crisis situations. In this short story, a young boy chooses to cope with a family tragedy in a very unexpected way.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how other people perceive Arnold throughout the story.
articles.permissions: Gina Berriault, "Stone Boy" from Women in Their Beds: Collected Stories. Copyright © 1996, 2017 by Gina Berriault. Used with the permission of The Permissions Company, LLC on behalf of Counterpoint Press, counterpointpress.com

blocks.text: Arnold drew his overalls and raveling gray sweater over his naked body. In the other narrow bed his brother Eugene went on sleeping, undisturbed by the alarm clock’s rusty ring. Arnold, watching his brother sleeping, felt a peculiar dismay; he was nine, six years younger than Eugie and in their waking hours it was he who was subordinate. To dispel emphatically his uneasy advantage over his sleeping brother, he threw himself on the hump of Eugie’s body.
“Get up! Get up!” he cried.

Arnold felt his brother twist away and saw the blankets lifted in a great wing, and, all in an instant, he was lying on his back under the covers with only his face showing, like a baby, and Eugie was sprawled on top of him.
“Whassa matter with you?” asked Eugie in sleepy anger, his face hanging close.
“Get up,” Arnold repeated. “You said you’d pick peas with me.”

Stupidly, Eugie gazed around the room as if to see if morning had come into it yet. Arnold began to laugh deliriously making soft, snorting noises, and was thrown off the bed. He got up from the floor and went down the stairs, the laughter continuing, like hiccups, against his will. But when he opened the staircase door and entered the parlor, he hunched up his shoulders and was quiet because his parents slept in the bedroom downstairs.

Arnold lifted his .22 caliber rifle from the rack on the kitchen wall. It was an old lever-action Winchester that his father had given him because nobody else used it any more. On their way down to the garden he and Eugie would go by the lake, and if there were any ducks on it he’d take a shot at them. Standing on the stool before the cupboard, he searched on the top shelf in the confusion of medicines and ointments for man and beast and found a small yellow box of .22 cartridges. Then he sat down on the stool and began to load his gun.

It was cold in the kitchen so early, but later in the day, when his mother canned the peas, the heat from the wood stove would be almost unbearable. Yesterday she had finished preserving the huckleberries that the family had picked along the mountain, and before that she had canned all the cherries his father had brought from the warehouse in Corinth. Sometimes, on these summer days, Arnold would deliberately come out from the shade where he was playing and make himself as uncomfortable as his mother was in the kitchen by standing in the sun until the sweat ran down his body.

Eugie came clomping down the stairs and into the kitchen, his head drooping with sleepiness. From his perch on the stool Arnold watched Eugie slip on his green knit cap. Eugie didn’t really need a cap; he hadn’t had a haircut in a long time and his brown curls grew thick and matted, close around his ears and down his neck, tapering there to a whorl. Eugie passed his left hand through his hair before he set his cap down with his right. The very way he slipped his cap on was an announcement of his status; almost everything he did was a reminder that he was eldest – first he, then Nora, then Arnold – and called attention to how tall he was (almost as tall as his father), how long his legs were, how small he was in the hips, and what a neat dip above his buttocks his thick-soled logger’s boots gave him. Arnold never tired of watching Eugie offer silent praise unto himself. He wondered, as he sat enthralled, if when he got to be Eugie’s age he would still be undersized and his hair still straight.
Eugie eyed the gun. “Don’t you know this ain’t duck season?” he asked gruffly, as if he were the sheriff.
“No, I don’t know,” Arnold said with a snigger.

Eugie picked up the tin washtub for the peas, unbolted the door with his free hand and kicked it open. Then, lifting the tub to his head, he went clomping down the back steps. Arnold followed, closing the door behind him.

The sky was faintly gray, almost white. The mountains behind the farm made the sun climb a long way to show itself. Several miles to the south, where the range opened up, hung an orange mist, but the valley in which the farm lay was still cold and colorless.

Eugie opened the gate to the yard and the boys passed between the barn and the row of chicken houses, their feet stirring up the carpet of brown feathers dropped by the molting chickens. They paused before going down the slope to the lake. A fluky morning wind ran among the shocks of wheat that covered the slope. It sent a shimmer northward across the lake, gently moving the rushes that formed an island in the center. Killdeer, their white markings flashing, skimmed the water, crying their shrill, sweet cry. And there at the south end of the lake were four wild ducks, swimming out from the willows into open water.

Arnold followed Eugie down the slope, stealing as his brother did, from one shock of wheat to another. Eugie paused before climbing through the wire fence that divided the wheatfield from the marshy pasture around the lake. They were screened from the ducks by the willows along the lake’s edge.
“If you hit your duck, you want me to go in after it?” Eugie said.
“If you want,” Arnold said.
Eugie lowered his eyelids, leaving silts of mocking blue. “You’d drown ‘fore you got to it, them legs of yours are so puny,” he said.
He shoved the tub under the fence and, pressing down the center wire, climbed through into the pasture.

Arnold pressed down the bottom wire, thrust a leg through and leaned forward to bring the other leg after. His rifle caught on the wire and he jerked at it. The air was rocked by the sound of the shot. Feeling foolish; he lifted his face, baring it to an expected shower of derision from his brother. But Eugie did not turn around. Instead, from his crouching position, he fell to his knees and then pitched forward onto his face. The ducks rose up crying from the lake, cleared the mountain background and beat away northward across the pale sky.
Arnold squatted beside his brother. Eugie seemed to be climbing the earth, as if the earth ran up and down, and when he found he couldn’t scale it he lay still.
“Eugie?”
Then Arnold saw it, under the tendril of hair at the nape of the neck – a slow rising of bright blood. It had an obnoxious movement, like that of a parasite.
“Hey, Eugie,” he said again. He was feeling the same discomfort he had felt when he had watched Eugie sleeping; his brother didn’t know that he was lying face down in the pasture.
Again he said, “Hey, Eugie,” an anxious nudge in his voice. But Eugie was as still as the morning about them.

Arnold set his rifle on the ground and stood up. He picked up the tub and, dragging it behind him, walked along by the willows to the garden fence and climbed through. He went down on his knees among the tangled vines. The pods were cold with the night, but his hands were strange to him, and not until some time had passed did he realize that the pods were numbing his fingers. He picked from the top of the vine first, then lifted the vine to look underneath for pods and then moved on to the next.

It was a warmth on his back, like a large hand laid firmly there, that made him raise his head. Way up the slope the gray farmhouse was struck by the sun. While his head had been bent the land had grown bright around him.
When he got up his legs were so stiff that he had to go down on his knees again to ease the pain. Then, walking sideways, he dragged the tub, half full of peas, up the slope.

The kitchen was warm now; a fire was roaring in the stove with a closed-up, rushing sound. His mother was spooning eggs from a pot of boiling water and putting them into a bowl. Her short brown hair was uncombed and fell forward across her eyes as she bent her head. Nora was lifting a frying pan full of trout from the stove, holding the handle with a dish towel. His father had just come in from bringing the cows from the north pasture to the barn, and was sitting on the stool, unbuttoning his red plaid Mackinaw.
“Did you boys fill the tub?” his mother asked.
“They ought of by now,” his father said. “They went out of the house an hour ago. Eugie woke me up comin’ downstairs. I heard you shootin’– did you get a duck?”
“No,” Arnold said. They would want to know why Eugie wasn’t coming in for breakfast he thought. “Eugie’s dead,” he told them.
They stared at him. The pitch cracked in the stove.
“You kids playin’ a joke?” his father asked.

“Where’s Eugene?” his mother asked scoldingly. She wanted, Arnold knew, to see his eyes, and when he had glanced at her she put the bowl and spoon down on the stove and walked past him. His father stood up and went out the door after her. Nora followed them with little skipping steps, as if afraid to be left alone.

Arnold went into the barn, down along the foddering passage past the cows waiting to be milked, and climbed into the loft. After a few minutes he heard a terrifying sound coming toward the house. His parents and Nora were returning from the willows, and sounds sharp as knives were rising from his mother’s breast and carrying over the sloping fields. In a short while he heard his father go down the back steps, slam the car door and drive away.

Arnold lay still as a fugitive, listening to the cows eating close by. If his parents never called him, he thought, he would stay up in the loft forever, out of the way. In the night he would sneak down for a drink of water from the faucet over the trough and for whatever food they left for him by the barn.

The rattle of his father’s car as it turned down the lane recalled him to the present. He heard voices of his Uncle Andy and Aunt Alice as they and his father went past the barn to the lake. He could feel the morning growing heavier with sun. Someone, probably Nora, had let the chickens out of their coops and they were cackling in the yard.

After a while another car turned down the road off the highway. The car drew to a stop and he heard the voices of strange men. The men also went past the barn and down to the lake. The undertakers, whom his father must have phoned from Uncle Andy’s house, had arrived from Corinth. Then he heard everybody come back and heard the car turn around and leave.
“Arnold!” It was his father calling from the yard.
He climbed down the ladder and went out into the sun, picking wisps of hay from his overalls.

Corinth, nine miles away, was the county seat. Arnold sat in the front seat of the old Ford between his father, who was driving, and Uncle Andy; no one spoke. Uncle Andy was his mother’s brother, and he had been fond of Eugie because Eugie had resembled him. Andy had taken Eugie hunting and had given him a knife and a lot of things, and now Andy, his eyes narrowed, sat tall and stiff beside Arnold.

Arnold’s father parked the car before the courthouse. It was a two-story brick building with a lamp on each side of the bottom step. They went up the wide stone steps, Arnold and his father going first, and entered the darkly paneled hallway. The shirt-sleeved man in the sheriff’s office said that the sheriff was at Carlson’s Parlor, examining the Curwing boy.

Andy went off to get the sheriff while Arnold and his father waited on a bench in the corridor. Arnold felt his father watching him, and he lifted his eyes with painful casualness to the announcement on the opposite wall, of the Corinth County Annual Rodeo, and then to the clock with its loudly clucking pendulum. After he had come down from the loft his father and Uncle Andy had stood in the yard with him and asked him to tell them everything, and he had explained to them how the gun had caught on the wire. But when they had asked him why he hadn’t run back to the house to tell his parents, he had had no answer – all he could say was that he had gone down into the garden to pick the peas. His father had stared at him in a pale, puzzled way, and it was then that he had felt his father and the others set their cold, turbulent silence against him. Arnold shifted on the bench, his only feeling a small one of compunction imposed by his father’s eyes.

At a quarter past nine Andy and the sheriff came in. They all went into the sheriff’s private office, and Arnold was sent forward to sit in the chair by the sheriff’s desk; his father and Andy sat down on the bench against the wall.

The sheriff lumped down into his swivel chair and swung toward Arnold. He was an old man with white hair like wheat stubble. His restless green eyes made him seem not to be in his office but to be hurrying and bobbing around somewhere else.
“What did you say your name was?” the sheriff asked.
“Arnold,” he replied; but he could not remember telling the sheriff his name before.
“Curwing?”
“Yes.”
“What were you doing with a .22, Arnold?”
“It’s mine,” he said.
“Okay. What were you going to shoot?”
“Some ducks,” he replied.
“Out of season?”
He nodded.
“That’s bad,” said the sheriff. “Were you and your brother good friends?”

What did he mean – good friends? Eugie was his brother. That was different from a friend, Arnold thought. A best friend was your own age, but Eugie was almost a man. Eugie had had a way of looking at him, slyly and mockingly and yet confidentially, that had summed up how they both felt about being brothers. Arnold had wanted to be with Eugie more than with anybody else but he couldn’t say they had been good friends.
“Did they ever quarrel?” the sheriff asked his father.
“Not that I know,” his father replied. “It seemed to me that Arnold cared a lot for Eugie.”
“Did you?” the sheriff asked Arnold.
If it seemed so to his father, then it was so. Arnold nodded.
“Were you mad at him this morning?”
“No.”
“How did you happen to shoot him?”
“We was crawlin’ through the fence.”
“Yes?”
“An’ the gun got caught on the wire.”
“Seems the hammer must of caught,” his father put in.

“All right, that’s what happened,” said the sheriff. “But what I want you to tell me is this. Why didn’t you go back to the house and tell your father right away? Why did you go and pick peas for an hour?”

Arnold gazed over his shoulder at his father, expecting his father to have an answer for this also. But his father’s eyes, larger and even lighter blue than usual, were fixed upon him curiously. Arnold picked at a callus in his right palm. It seemed odd now that he had not run back to the house and wakened his father, but he could not remember why he had not. They were all waiting for him to answer.
“I come down to pick peas,” he said.
“Didn’t you think,” asked the sheriff, stepping carefully from word to word, “that it was more important for you to go tell your parents what had happened?”
“The sun was gonna come up,” Arnold said.
“What’s that got to do with it?”
“It’s better to pick peas while they’re cool.”

The sheriff swung away from him, laid both hands flat on his desk. “Well, all I can say is,” he said across to Arnold's father and Uncle Andy, “he’s either a moron or he’s so reasonable that he’s way ahead of us.” He gave a challenging snort. “It’s come to my notice that the most reasonable guys are mean ones. They don’t feel nothing.”
For a moment the three men sat still. Then the sheriff lifted his hand like a man taking an oath. “Take him home,” he said.
Andy uncrossed his legs. “You don’t want him?”
“Not now,” replied the sheriff. “Maybe in a few years.”
Arnold’s father stood up. He held his hat against his chest. “The gun ain’t his no more,” he smiled wanly.

Arnold went first through the hallway, hearing behind him the heels of his father and Uncle Andy striking the floor boards. He went down the steps ahead of them and climbed into the back seat of the car. Andy paused as he was getting into the front seat and gazed back at Arnold, and Arnold saw that his uncle’s eyes had absorbed the knowingness from the sheriff’s eyes. Andy and his father and the sheriff had discovered what made him go down into the garden. It was because he was cruel, the sheriff had said, and didn’t care about his brother. Was that the reason? Arnold lowered his eyelids meekly against his uncle’s stare.

The rest of the day he did his tasks around the farm keeping apart from the family. At evening when he saw his father stomp tiredly into the house, Arnold did not put down his hammer and leave the chicken coop he was repairing. He was afraid that they did not want him to eat supper with them. But in a few minutes another fear that they would go to the trouble of calling him and that he would be made conspicuous by his tardiness made him follow his father into the house. As he went through the kitchen he saw the jars of peas standing in rows on the workbench, a reproach to him.

No one spoke at supper, and his mother, who sat next to him, leaned her head in her hand all through the meal, curving her fingers over her eyes so as not to see him. They were finishing their small, silent supper when the visitors began to arrive, knocking hard on the back door. The men were coming from their farms now that it was growing dark and they could not work any more.

Old Man Matthews, gray and stocky, came first, with his two sons, Orion, the elder, and Clint, who was Eugie’s age. As the callers entered the parlor, where the family ate, Arnold sat down in a rocking chair. Even as he had been undecided before supper whether to remain outside or take his place at the table, he now thought that he should go upstairs, and yet he stayed to avoid being conspicuous by his absence. If he stayed, he thought, as he always stayed and listened when visitors came, they would see that he was only Arnold and not the person the sheriff thought he was. He sat with his arms crossed and his hands tucked into his armpits and did not lift his eyes.

The Matthews men had hardly settled down around the table, after Arnold’s mother and Nora had cleared away the dishes, when another car rattled down the road and someone else rapped on the back door. This time it was Sullivan, a spare and sandy man, so nimble of gesture and expression that Arnold had never been able to catch more than a few of his meanings. Sullivan, in dusty jeans, sat down in the other rocker, shot out his skinny legs and began to talk in his fast way, recalling everything that Eugene had ever said to him. The other men interrupted to tell of occasions they remembered, and after a time Clint’s young voice, hoarse like Eugene’s had been, broke in to tell about the time Eugene had beat him in a wrestling match.

Out in the kitchen the voices of Orion’s wife and of Mrs. Sullivan mingled with Nora’s voice but not, Arnold noticed, his mother’s. Then dry little Mr. Cram came, leaving large Mrs. Cram in the kitchen, and there was no chair left for Mr. Cram to sit in. No one asked Arnold to get up and he was unable to rise. He knew that the story had got around to them during the day about how he had gone and picked peas after he had shot his brother, and he knew that although they were talking only about Eugie they were thinking about him and if he got up, if he moved even his foot, they would all be alerted. Then Uncle Andy arrived and leaned his tall, lanky body against the doorjamb and there were two men standing.
Presently Arnold was aware that the talk had stopped. He knew without looking up that the men were watching him.
“Not a tear in his eye,” said Andy, and Arnold knew that it was his uncle who had gestured the men to attention.
“He don’t give a hoot, is that how it goes?” asked Sullivan, trippingly.

“He’s a reasonable fellow,” Andy explained. “That’s what the sheriff said. It’s us who ain’t reasonable. If we’d of shot our brother, we’d of come runnin’ back to the house, like a baby. Well, we’d of been unreasonable. What would of been the use of actin’ like that? If your brother is shot dead, he’s shot dead. What’s the use of gettin’ emotional about it? The thing to do is go down to the garden and pick peas. Am I right?”
The men around the room shifted their heavy, satisfying weight of unreasonableness.
Matthews’ son Orion said: “If I’d of done what he done, Pa would’ve hung my pelt by the side of that big coyote in the barn.”

Arnold sat in the rocker until the last man had filed out. While his family was out in the kitchen bidding the callers good night and the cars were driving away down the lane to the highway, he picked up one of the kerosene lamps and slipped quickly up the stairs. In his room he undressed by lamplight, although he and Eugie had always undressed in the dark, and not until he was lying in his bed did he blow out the flame. He felt nothing, not any grief. There was only the same immense silence and crawling inside of him; it was the way the house and fields felt under a merciless sun.

He awoke suddenly. He knew that his father was out the yard, closing the doors of the chicken houses so that the chickens could not roam out too early and fall prey to the coyotes that came down from the mountains at daybreak. The sound that had wakened him was the step of his father as he got up from the rocker and went down the back steps. And he knew that his mother was awake in her bed.
Throwing off the covers, he rose swiftly, went down the stairs and across the dark parlor to his parents’ room. He rapped on the door.
“Mother?”
From the closed room her voice rose to him a seeking and retreating voice.
“Yes?”

“Mother?” he asked insistently. He had expected her to realize that he wanted to go down on his knees by her bed and tell her that Eugie was dead. She did not know it yet, nobody knew it, and yet she was sitting up in bed, waiting to be told, waiting for him to confirm her dread. He had expected her to tell him to come in, to allow him to dig his head into her blankets and tell her about the terror he had felt when he had knelt beside Eugie. He had come to clasp her in his arms and, in his terror, to pommel her breasts with his head. He put his hand upon the knob.
“Go back to bed, Arnold,” she called sharply.
But he waited.
“Go back! Is night when you get afraid?”

At first he did not understand. Then, silently, he left the door and for a stricken moment stood by the rocker. Outside everything was still. The fences, the shocks of wheat seen through the window before him were so still it was as if they moved and breathed in the daytime and had fallen silent with the lateness of the hour. It was a silence that seemed to observe his father, a figure moving alone around the yard, his lantern casting a circle of light by his feet. In a few minutes his father would enter the dark house, the lantern still lighting his way.

Arnold was suddenly aware that he was naked. He had thrown off his blankets and come down the stairs to tell his mother how he felt about Eugie, but she had refused to listen to him and his nakedness had become unpardonable. At once he went back up the stairs, fleeing from his father’s lantern.

At breakfast he kept his eyelids lowered as if to deny the humiliating night. Nora, sitting at his left, did not pass the pitcher of milk to him and he did not ask for it. He would never again, he vowed, ask them for anything, and he ate his fried eggs and potatoes only because everybody ate meals – the cattle ate, and the cats; it was customary for everybody to eat.
“Nora, you gonna keep that pitcher for yourself?” his father asked.
Nora lowered her head unsurely.
“Pass it on to Arnold,” his father said.
Nora put her hands in her lap.
His father picked up the metal pitcher and set it down at Arnold’s plate.

Arnold, pretending to be deaf to the discord, did not glance up but relief rained over his shoulders at the thought that his parents recognized him again. They must have lain awake after his father had come in from the yard: had they realized together why he had come down the stairs and knocked at their door?

“Bessie’s missin’ this morning,” his father called out to his mother, who had gone into the kitchen. “She went up the mountain last night and had her calf, most likely. Somebody’s got to go up and find her ‘fore the coyotes get the calf.”

That had been Eugie’s job, Arnold thought. Eugie would climb the cattle trails in search of a newborn calf and come down the mountain carrying the calf across his back, with the cow running down along behind him, mooing in alarm.

Arnold ate the few more forkfuls of his breakfast, put hands on the edge of the table and pushed back his chair. If he went for the calf he’d be away from the farm all morning. He could switch the cow down the mountain and the calf would run along at its mother’s side.
When he passed through the kitchen his mother was setting a kettle of water on the stove. “Where you going? she asked awkwardly.
“Up to get the calf,” he replied, averting his face.
“Arnold?”
At the door he paused reluctantly, his back to her knowing that she was seeking him out, as his father was doing and he called upon his pride to protect him from them.
“Was you knocking at my door last night?”
He looked over his shoulder at her, his eyes narrow and dry.
“What’d you want?” she asked humbly.
“I didn’t want nothing,” he said flatly.
Then he went out the door and down the back steps, his legs trembling from the fright his answer gave him.

How does Arnold's awareness of what people think about him contribute to the story's theme?

discussionQuestions.prompt: What does it mean to feel alone? How do we know that Arnold feels alone in the story? Why doesn't Arnold try harder to find comfort? Have you ever felt alone because of something that you did or something you experienced?
How do people face death? Why do you think Arnold waited to tell his family about Eugie's death? What would you have done in Arnold's position?
In the context of the text, how do we find forgiveness? Do you think that Arnold will ever truly find peace? Will he be able to move beyond what happened with Eugie? Why or why not?

articles.title: Mousing Teeth
articles.author: Avalon A. Manly
articles.description: In this short story, Nova tricks their cousin, Ros, into joining them on a dangerous adventure.
articles.intro: Avalon A. Manly teaches secondary English in Colorado. She is currently studying for her doctorate, which focuses on Gothic literature and queer theory. In "Mousing Teeth" Nova manipulates their cousin, Ros, into joining them on a dangerous adventure to prove a paranormal creature is real.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how Nova’s character develops throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "Mousing Teeth" by Avalon A. Manly. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: ROS

Ros yelped as a tree branch whipped back and smacked her face. “Nova,” she hissed, rubbing at the small welt on her cheek. Half an inch higher and she could have lost an eye. Nova’s footsteps trudged along ahead, heedless of Ros falling back or the dying light. It was already almost too dark to see clearly. The forest loomed around them, the trees forming bleary outlines in the dusky light. She tried again, louder this time, “Nova!”
“Come on!” came the reply.

By the time Ros caught up with them beyond the next line of trees, the sunlight was almost gone. The valley spread out below them, bisected by an old dirt road and dotted with the grassy mounds — overgrown, abandoned WWII munitions bunkers — that gave the TNT area its name. It was a hangout spot for fishers who didn’t mind the contamination and for older teenagers, though Ros and her cousin had been here a couple of times before, back when they were still in middle school. Half-buried bottles, wrinkled plastic wrappers, and charred coals in small stone circles were dispersed along the forest floor. The occasional twig figurine with leafy wings strung on a sapling branch as an homage to their town’s main tourist attraction. Behind her, the lights of Point Pleasant flickered on against the settling night.

They took off down the hill, sliding through the long spring grass to level ground. They came to a stop in front of one of the rounded, grass-covered bunkers — the concrete domes that everyone called “igloos” — its doors graffitied with neon lines cast grayscale by the evening. Watching her cousin silhouetted against the berm, Ros sighed; Nova’s eyes were filled with a familiar, determined light. They were up to something.

Nova wasn’t tall, but they stood like they could be, shoulders back and chest out like that pose alone could defy the whole world. It was the same way they had stood at the eighth-grade science fair, in which Nova had tried to build a Faraday cage. It hadn’t worked, and they had to evacuate the gym when the display table caught fire, but Nova would do it all over again if they were given the chance. There was no stopping them once they had set their mind to something.
Ros glanced at her watch and then at the thunderheads collecting on the horizon. “This better be quick. I don’t want to get caught in the rain.”

Nova smiled. “This way,” they said, and moved past the igloo, down toward the wilder woods on the other side. Ros followed them beyond the edge of the igloos, sighing as they turned away from the road, weaving through the basswood and beach trees until they paused at the edge of a ravine. Together, they looked down the slope, and though it was not steep at first, it tilted at a dizzying angle just below the small wooden bridge that spanned its length. It was only about fifteen feet deep, as this slice of landscape was more wash than canyon, but Ros imagined that the climb from the bottom, riddled with roots and clay, was daunting.

Nova was immediately busy on the bridge, kneeling and scratching at its surface with chalk. The bridge’s angles were strange, and the gaps between boards were uneven. It was made mostly from pallet wood scavenged from newspaper drops.
“Hey,” Ros said, turning to where they sat criss-cross at the bridge’s center, “did you build this?”
Nova shrugged, continuing to draw.
Ros waited, eyes wide, but Nova offered no further explanation. “Why are we here?” she asked.
“Just give me a minute,” they replied, digging in their bag.

Ros pressed on the boards with her foot. They seemed solid enough, though every screw head was different and the entire contraption creaked under her weight. She sat behind Nova and pulled a library book from her backpack. Sometimes, it was best to just let Nova get something out of their system. So she half-heartedly listened to Nova work on whatever they were doing for a while, their back to her, shuffling and shifting along the bridge. But eventually, she grew bored.

“Did you know foxes can hear mice and other prey animals beneath up to three feet of snow, from 25 feet away?” Ros asked, a little too loudly, reading the caption beneath a photo. In the picture, a red fox was perched intently, listening to movement beneath its feet. In the following frames, it pounced upward in a perfect arc before plunging straight down through the snow.
Nova made a noncommittal noise.

“It’s called fox-diving, or mousing,” Ros continued louder. “Without seeing their prey, they can tell where to dive just by listening, and look,” Ros turned the book to face Nova, “they come up with a mouse in their teeth. They seem to be better at this when facing north though.”
Nova glanced over their shoulder. “I am trying to focus, you know,” they said. Then, in a mumble, “How do they know which way is north?”

“I dunno. Scientists think they can sense magnetic poles or something,” Ros replied, glad to have gotten Nova’s attention back. It was one thing to hike out here, but she was irritated at the thought of camping out on a homemade bridge. She needed to finish her project. A section on mousing would nicely complete her portfolio board for display tomorrow. She shoved the book into her bag. “Would you please tell me why we’re here now ?”
Nova shuffled a few items on the ground, got to their feet, then stepped away with a flourish.

A series of small items were arranged in a loose chalk circle. Ros shuffled closer on her palms and knees. She saw small toy cars at the circle’s edges, pinning down dark photocopies of what looked to be newspaper clippings. And in the center, Nova had sketched out a rudimentary shape, like an oval with wings. In the middle of its mass were two large red dots, like eyes.
“I never was an artist,” Nova said, watching Ros as she took in the display.

Ros looked up at Nova. She had seen them draw this symbol before, inside their notebooks and on sticky notes. They had once spray-painted it on a boulder. Ross sighed — Nova had promised this was for school — but they were out here looking for the Mothman. Again .

It was a hobby of some locals (and even more tourists) to go searching for the mysterious creature that plagued Point Pleasant back in the 60s, and Nova was no exception. They had always been obsessed with the Mothman legend. Something about the humanoid creature with dark wings and red eyes fascinated them. They had never missed a year of the Annual Mothman Festival, and practically every one of their social media profile pictures was of them beaming in front of the town’s 12-foot metallic statue of the Mothman.
“ Nova ,” Ros said. Her cousin flinched at her tone. “You said we were coming out here to do research for your project.”
“Well, we’re supposed to do something that interests us, aren’t we? Something that fits the local culture and environment theme.”

Nova grinned. Ros knew that look. It was the same look that had convinced Ros as a child that it was perfectly appropriate, even wise, to run away with Nova to the woods and build a life of their own as monster hunters. This new life began with a treehouse they assembled from scavenged branches, optimism, and a limited understanding of weight-bearing construction. When Ros had grown tired of an uncomfortable treehouse with no television or snacks, she used her “for emergencies only” phone and called her then-frantic parents (without telling Nova) to come pick the two of them up. Ever since that day, Ros was declared the sensible cousin, the responsible one, with Nova as her charge. They were older now, but Nova hadn’t outgrown their ambition nor beliefs about monsters.
The cloudburst on the horizon, creeping nearer as night settled around them in earnest, rumbled threateningly, as if to punctuate Nova’s announcement. “I’m going to summon the Mothman,” Nova said.
Ros burst into laughter, clutching her sides. The bridge groaned beneath them, as if it were also amused.
Nova waited until Ros came up for air.

Ros wiped her eyes and grabbed her bag, still chuckling. She threw a strap over one shoulder. “That’s really funny, Nova. I can’t believe you dragged me out here for this nonsense when I have work to do.”
Nova flushed. “It’s not nonsense.”

Ros rolled her eyes pointedly and turned to walk back toward the igloos. Nova grabbed her backpack, hurling her off balance and pulling her backward across the bridge’s misaligned boards. They froze as the whole structure seemed to tilt. After a moment of silence passed with no buckling or creaking, Ros shook Nova off and tried to leave again.
“Wait!” Nova said, hands up and pleading. They ducked down to gather the clippings they had arranged around their drawing. “Don’t go yet . I just need some proof.”
Taking a deep breath, Ros tried to channel both of their mothers and several dowager aunts into her next words. “Nothing is going to happen ,” she replied. “Mothman isn’t real .”

Nova stood with a handful of papers and waved them in front of Ros’ face like a fan, a blur of headlines and small print. “Look,” they said, shuffling the clippings and presenting one after another like an analog slideshow. “First, the gravediggers in Clendenin saw him, and then here in Point Pleasant — here in the TNT, when the army built all these bunkers for munitions storage — at least a hundred sightings between November 1966 and December 1967. And then — well — ” They stopped, gesturing to the bridge beneath them and taking a breath to continue before Ros interrupted.
“And then the Silver Bridge collapsed into the Ohio River,” Ros interjected, her voice a blend of ire and disbelief. “It broke and people died .”

Nova nodded. “But there were other accounts. Couples were chased in their cars on Route 62 by the Mothman, and even a mining accident in Germany in 1978. He scared the miners out right before it collapsed.” They held up a series of images, all clearly printed from the Internet: a black-and-white photo of a city in Japan, a run-down ferris wheel with the word “Pripyat” scrawled underneath, a darkly scanned tabloid clipping of two towers in a plume of smoke. “He’s seen at disaster sites.” Nova’s face was expectant, like this was sufficient for them to reach a shared consensus about their purpose on this handmade bridge in the middle of the woods on a school night.
Ros rubbed her temples. “Okay, so let’s say you’re crazy enough to actually believe that the Mothman caused all those tragedies. Why on earth would you want to summon him?”
Nova shook their head. “He doesn’t cause disasters; he just shows up wherever terrible things are about to happen.”
“I maintain,” Ros replied flatly, “that we should therefore not want to see him.”

Nova rolled their eyes and held up the camera dangling around their neck. “Well, I do. This isn’t just a project. I’m going to get legitimate evidence and tell my first-person account of seeing the Mothman.”

Ros had stood beside Nova during all manner of misguided experiments in their lives. But this wasn’t a treehouse near their childhood homes; this wasn’t searching the crawlspace for tiny civilizations or playing hooky to research famous hauntings in the computer lab. They were alone in the woods, in the dark, miles from home with a storm clipping towards them, wasting their time with something completely futile.
“That is the dumbest idea you have ever had,” she said coldly.
“Imagine if I actually get the shot,” they said, clutching the camera in their hands.

“Yeah, and then go ahead and make a fool out of yourself?” Ros shook her head. “It’s not going to work. But even if it did, no one is going to take you seriously. No one’s gonna think, ‘That’s a genuine photo of the Mothman, yes, siree!’”

Nova cut their eyes at their cousin. “You talk but you don’t get it. Why would you? You get on with everyone. The way people treat me at school, like, like I’m some sort of...” Their voice sounded choked and wet. “At least this way, I’ll have proof that I’m not.”

In a place like Point Pleasant, Nova stood out as different. They got teased for their pronouns, for their clothes, for being too quiet, for not having many friends. Ros often forgot that Nova was both smaller and shier than her. She was privy to a side of them — a boisterous, imaginative side — that was often hidden behind mediocre grades, mediocre test scores, and malicious schoolyard gossip. Ros intervened whenever she had a chance, but there was no way to protect Nova completely.
Ros placed her hands on her cousin’s shoulders. “I do get it,” she said, trying to temper her tone, “or at least I want to. I’m sorry.”
Nova was not looking at Ros. Instead, their gaze was angled up, eyes huge and jaw slack. Blood had already rushed from their face, rendering them pale and ghoulish.
Curious, Ros turned.

Looking around, she scanned the skies and tops of trees and didn’t see anything. Was Nova trying to trick her? If this was all some sort of prank, she could at least admit that they were committed. Nova stepped forward, elbowing Ros out of the way, camera already raised halfway to their face. They pressed the camera’s shutter-release button repeatedly, taking the same picture from the same angle over and over again.

Ros stared harder at where Nova’s lens was aimed. Her eyes found the shape, the dark silhouette hidden in the branches, before her brain registered what it was that she was looking at. And even then, she couldn’t find the words. Whatever it was, it was bulky, huge, and blacker than the shadows in which it lurked, a kind of dark velvet without texture or shade. Like a black hole, it seemed to absorb light from the space around it.

With a snap, enormous sails sprouted from each side, a dozen or more feet long. Wings, Ros realized. Confusion and panic washed through her. Was this some kind of prank? It had to be a kind of bird — just a bird. Some weird, gigantic bird. The creature pushed off of the branch on which it was perched, nearly ripping the limb from the trunk, and shot upwards, a dark spot against the gray thunderclouds listing across the sky. Nova hollered and whooped when it took off, still furiously taking photos.
When the shadow disappeared behind the blanket of clouds, Nova lowered the camera and was quiet. Ros felt her legs give out beneath her, and she landed on her knees with a solid thump .
The bridge strained and shook beneath them.
Nova snapped out of their daze. “Okay, now we can go,” they said.
But before either could say another word, it crumbled, and they fell, tumbling into the chasm below.
NOVA

When Nova awoke, the thunderheads had burst and were scattering chilly April rain across the landscape, and for a moment, Nova could only hear the white noise of droplets hitting the ground around them. They lied there, eyes closed, head pounding, trying to remember how they got there.

More sounds broke through into their consciousness: rattling trees in the wind, the shake of thunder promising more serious weather. They pried open their eyes and looked up at where the bridge had been. The ravine was steep, its sides a maze of soil and roots, and water ran in rivulets downhill toward the central channel.

The fog in their head began to clear. A couple of days ago, Nova had overheard some kids in study hall talking about a bridge near the TNT area. In a ravine, unsafe. Just the sort of place that the Mothman would appear. Nova had listened intently. It was the excuse they needed; a project on the Mothman and a chance to prove he was real.

The memory of the thing in the forest, how it soared into the air right before the collapse, came back to them in pieces. In the moment, they had been filled with such exhilaration, but also trepidation. There was no real reason for them to have dragged Ros along. She had always been there as their defender and confidant and sometimes their only supporter. When they were kids, that was enough. But they were getting older, and Ros had her own interests and social circles that didn’t always include Nova. She joined a volleyball team early in the year and had made a lot of new friends. Unmoored and alone, Nova had latched on to something for themself, paranormal and misguided though it may be.
Now, lying at the bottom of the ravine, Nova sat up and took stock of their body. Thankfully, nothing seemed broken, but when they pressed a hand against their hairline, it came away crimson.

They curled forward, pressing their palms against their face. They had tried to summon the Mothman many times before, always by themselves, always unsuccessfully. They had hoped that this time would be different; that the bridge, something with a little risk and some symbolic meaning, might do the trick. And the fact that it did — Nova wasn’t really sure what to make of that.

They took a deep breath, thoughts bleary with shock. Slowly, they stood and surveyed what they could of the wreckage. Broken boards were scattered around Nova and down the sides of the wash. Nova spotted their backpack and various clippings on the ground nearby, the latter having almost dissolved. “Ros!” they called. Their shoes squelched through the mud, and their eyes strained against the dark. Ros had landed on the side of the wash. Her body was twisted strangely, partially obscured by a board that crossed over her right side. Slipping on the mud, Nova skidded to their knees beside Ros. They pushed the board off of her and lightly patted her cheek. “Ros, wake up!”

Ros’ eyelids fluttered slightly as she stirred. When she did not respond, Nova forced themself back into a sitting position, knees curled up toward their forehead, eyes cast down in the dark. They tried to breathe, tried to plan. Tried to remember if they’d ever been told what to do if you summon a paranormal being on a shoddy bridge and accidentally kill your cousin.

They shouldn’t have brought Ros along. They had been willing to put their cousin and best friend in harm’s way for what? Something to show off to kids in school, a small sliver of notoriety? For some form of acceptance, even if that meant only among the school’s contingent of Mothman fans. Any acceptance, Nova had thought, would do.
Ros groaned, shifting in the wet earth. Nova lurched closer, hovering over their cousin. Her eyes opened slowly and focused even slower.
“Ros,” they said softly. “Are you okay? Are you hurt?”
Ros grunted in a way that Nova was unsure how to interpret. Then, she gestured at Nova’s head wound.
“I’m fine,” they said. “I think I’m fine.”

Ros nodded, flinched, and tried to sit up. With Nova’s help, she pressed up onto her elbows. Then she let out a choked gasp, and curled forward. Her right foot was in an unnatural position, her ankle had completely twisted in the fall. It was nauseating to look at.
“You have to get help,” she said, teeth gritted against the pain.
Panic flooded Nova, a white crush of terror. “I can’t leave you here alone,” they said. Thunder shattered the air around them.
“You have to,” Ros huffed. “I can’t walk.”
“No!”
“What else are you going to do?” Ros’ voice was strained as she pulled herself up into a full sitting position. “Carry me?”

They stared at each other, considering. Ros was a head taller and considerably more athletic than Nova, muscular and lean. Nova was slender, too, but neither so strong nor so fit as their cousin. In times of crisis, adrenaline could do miraculous things: make people stronger, faster, more resilient. Nova would need as much of it as they could get to carry her out of a fifteen-foot ravine, even in good weather conditions. The rain was slanting down in sheets, with the water rising beneath them. The sides of the ravine above were slick and wet, not ideal for climbing.
“I’ll get you out,” they said, trying to force the waver out of their voice.

Ros stared at them, considering. Nova wasn’t going to leave her, that was for certain. So they could both stay down here until the wash absorbed what it could and flash flooded them out, or she could cooperate.

“Fine,” she said. “But we’re going to need to stabilize it. Give me my bag. I have a bandana in there we can use.” Even with the rudimentary first aid training they had taken at school, neither of them were truly prepared to treat Ros’ injury. Sprained ankles were one thing; this was clearly broken. Nova wrapped and then rewrapped the bandana around Ros’ ankle like a bandage, wincing in sympathy while Ros swallowed small cries of pain. The fabric wasn’t long enough and too taut to tie tightly, let alone in the dark, but it was better than nothing.

The rain surrounded them in curtains, obscuring anything more than a few feet away. Kneeling in the mud, Ros’ arms around their neck, Nova used a root to haul themself upright, straining against the extra weight and the slippery ground. They knew the way out of the ravine to the igloo road wasn’t unsurpassably steep, but there was no clear path from the bottom up, and the thunder that rumbled above them made every warning about lightning strikes they’d ever heard echo in their head.

The first step, Nova told themself, would be the hardest. Ros’ breath was wheezing in their ear, her arms grasping each other at the elbow, trying not to rest against Nova’s throat and mostly succeeding. Nova had one arm slung under Ros’ right knee in a strange, lopsided piggy-back, and their thighs shook with strain. For a moment, Nova thought that maybe they should go for help alone — but lightning lashed across the clouds, its forking path illuminating the river, already several inches further up the bank, encroaching on the spot where Nova woke up. They were running against the clock, and they were losing.
So they took a deep breath and walked.

They were wrong; the first step was far from the worst. Every step was an ordeal, a searing in their lungs not quenched by the deluge that continued as they worked up the hill. As they labored, one foot slipping in front of the other in the mud turning to sheeting sludge, they felt acutely aware of their heart in their chest, hammering against the effort.

Nova tried not to think about anything as they pushed onwards, especially not about what they would do if they stumbled or how guilty they felt for bringing them here in the first place. Should’ve stayed home. Should’ve done a project on actual moths, something safe.
It wasn’t supposed to be this dangerous.

That was what no one ever got — not their parents, not the kids at school, not even Ros. Things like the Mothman and Bigfoot weren’t monsters. They were creatures, living beings, possibly with an intelligence on par with or greater than humans. Just because people were afraid of something did not make it a monster.

Nova scowled. Maybe it was arrogant to assume that they could be the one to find him, to drag him out of the dark forests of Appalachia where he belonged. Who knew if what they saw actually was the Mothman?

The washes peppering the woods were no marvels of nature. The hills here were modest, receding into more significant topography further from town, but Nova thought they’d never climbed a steeper hill. Fire raced through their thighs and back, and whenever they were able, they reached through the rain to grab a root as leverage, pulling them both foot by foot away from the rising water below. They were making progress, but every dozen steps, Nova would stumble or move too close to a root, jarring Ros’ injury, and Ros would stifle a cry.

Halfway up the climb, Nova stopped and braced themself against a large rock. The icy rain had numbed their skin, but they still felt the tiny hairs on the back of their neck rise. Pure instinct told them that they were being watched.

Losing some ground in the mud, they turned to glance behind. Below them, in the wash, near where Ros had landed, a large, black shape stood. Its enormous red eyes observed them intently. The creature stood motionless in the mud, its huge black wings up, shielding it against the rain. Waiting.
Nova’s breath caught in their throat. This was the thing cryptid enthusiasts dreamed of: a perfectly still subject, close enough to get a high resolution shot that left no room for ambiguity or hoax.
If Nova didn’t have to hold up Ros, they could get that photo. Maybe they could set her down on the rock.
Lightning flashed in the sky, lighting up the ravine. Nova spotted an object on the ground at the creature’s feet. It was their camera! It had been torn from their neck during the fall.

The creature shrieked: a shrill, hawkish noise, loud as a jet-engine. It wormed its way into their skull and started tearing at the wallpaper inside. It was a sound, but it was also a thing, something they could touch and taste and smell, an encompassing experience red in tooth and claw.
When the lightning faded, the Mothman had vanished.

Breathing hard from the combination of terror and exhaustion, Nova tried to think. Up was the only way out, and the climb was slow and hugely difficult. If they went down for the camera, for the photos that they had already taken and the proof they so desperately wanted, then Ros would be left alone. It might take several more minutes for Nova to slide down the ravine, scramble back to Ros, and pick her up again. They still had a long way to climb and a mile or so of walking to get home.
The Mothman had given them a choice. Go back and lose precious time; keep moving and leave behind a chance to make a name for themself.

Nova turned to face the slope again, Ros’ weight leaden on their back. They thought about the camera sinking beneath the water and being carried away, some stranger discovering it miles away, considering it useless and trashing it, or, worse still, it sinking to the bed of the river to be never discovered — a myth, a legend, a truth buried for all time.
Ros’ shallow breaths tickled the back of Nova’s neck: a small comfort. They took another step.

Nova wasn’t sure how long they’d been in the wash, but they were soaked with rain and mud, their fingers coated in sticky clay, their skin and joints aching with cold. Ahead, Nova saw the slope tilt sideways, rising more gently upriver, whereas the part directly above them rose steeply into the woods. Two paths diverged, they thought, shifting their feet with relief to the gentler part of the slope. Instantly, it was easier to balance Ros’ weight against the muddy ground, and they could stand straighter. When they glanced back at the spot where the camera laid, Nova saw only rising water.
Ahead, a shriek burst from the trees. It was the same cry, the creature’s eldritch howl, pumping ice through Nova’s veins.

The Mothman glared down at Nova from the treeline before them, brake lights in the dark. It was difficult to determine where the wings ended and the forest began. For a long moment, they stared at each other.

The creature launched itself upward through the canopy and into the storm. Nova pictured the fox from Ros’ book, head tilted down at the snow like a satellite dish, arching up in a perfect bow, spearing downward through the snow and surfacing with a mouse in its teeth. Instinctually, they raised a hand in front of their face, held it there and then slowly lowered it when nothing dove toward them. He doesn’t cause disasters. He shows up wherever terrible things are about to happen.
Lightning flashed, and once again, it was gone.

Nova stared up through the trees, shaking with cold and exhaustion. The wash was sliding in sections down toward the floor of the ravine, dirt loosened by the rain. The climb continued, slippery and slow, with only strangled roots as ladder rungs toward the top. Whatever the creature was trying to warn them about — the bridge, the camera, the easier path — they weren’t going to let it go to waste.

Groaning with the strain, they grabbed a root and hauled their bodies up, sideways, towards the steeper wall of the wash. They moved as quickly as they could away from the gentle hill that rose into the trees. With each step, the sliding earth betrayed them, pushing them down toward the water as they tried to outrace it. At last, with a primal cry, Nova felt their hand grab a sapling instead of a root, and they hauled both their bodies out of the wash, pressing into the sharp edges of the forest. Nova sprawled across the spring undergrowth, Ros flopping to lie down beside them. They were out!

A thick, fast, rushing sound, accompanied by the dense crack of shattering branches began to gain ground against the storm, drowning it out. Nova struggled to sit up and see the water racing toward them. It had found the path of least resistance through the hills, razing the gentle slope below them on its way to the wash. The speed of it was astounding. Roots tore from the walls of the wash and glided down the hill like spears on the roiling surface. Entire trees were splintered as the flood, foul with sediment and loam, overwhelmed the place where Nova had stood minutes ago and faced the creature.

And then it was gone: first a deluge and then a trickle, the wash ravaged with debris and cut with new canyons down its middle. It was a totally transformed landscape, and the force of its passing made one thing very clear. Nova thought about the camera, and the gentler slope and how anyone or anything in that path would’ve been caught in that flash flood with zero chance of survival.
They turned to Ros, “I’m sorry. I shouldn’t have done this. I put us in danger, and you got hurt, and... I’m sorry.”
Ros, prone on her back and not meeting Nova’s eyes, said with deliberation, “Thank you for the apology.” A deep sigh. “You’re right. You absolutely shouldn’t have done this.”
Nova swallowed thickly and nodded. They deserved that.

Tired, Nova laid back down beside their cousin and looked up at the sky where the storm was abating. They could see nothing but clouds, but they were softer, grayer. The rain had receded to a soft drizzle. In a few minutes, they would get up again and start the long trek home. But right now, they needed to rest, plan what to do next. They thought about the fox listening through the snow, perceiving something no one else could hear.
“You know it saved us, right?”
Ros sat up a little. “Wrong. It didn’t save us.”
Nova smiled.
“You’ll be able to tell everyone, now,” Ros continued. “Give them something to talk about at school.”

Nova almost laughed and looked back into the trees. “I’m thinking it’s best that this stay between you and me. It doesn't matter what you give people at school. They’ll always find something else to talk about anyway.”
“No extraordinary phenomenon for your school project then?”
“I dunno,” Nova said, turning to their cousin again, “flash floods are pretty extraordinary.”

options.label: Being true to yourself means not allowing what others think to control your actions.
Being around people who you are related to is the best way to overcome loneliness.
Being a believer in the paranormal will only ever lead to destruction and hurt.
Being accepted by others will always come at a very heavy cost.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses a theme of the story?

options.label: Ros provides insight into how she and other people view Nova.
Ros gives readers an alternative theory on the Mothman.
Ros is more knowledgeable about the Mothman.
Ros knows the TNT area much better than Nova.

questions.prompt: Why does the author most likely include a section with Ros' point of view?

options.label: "There was no stopping them once they had set their mind to something." (Paragraph 5)
"Nova was not looking at Ros. Instead, their gaze was angled up, eyes huge and jaw slack. Blood had already rushed from their face, rendering them pale and ghoulish." (Paragraph 49).
"Just because people were afraid of something did not make it a monster." (Paragraph 87).
"He doesn't cause disasters. He shows up wherever terrible things are about to happen." (Paragraph 104).

questions.prompt: Which statement provides the best evidence for how Nova and the Mothman are alike?

options.label: misunderstood and lonely
outgoing and optimistic
anxious and pessimistic

articles.title: Indian Residential Schools
articles.author: Kids Britannica
articles.description: Kids Britannica discusses the effects of the Indian Residential School system in Canada, as well as the country's efforts toward healing and repair over the last two decades.
articles.intro: Indigenous people are the people who first lived and may continue to live in a particular country or region. From the mid-1800s until the late 1900s, Indigenous children in Canada and the United States were forced to separate from their families and attend residential schools. This informational text discusses the effects of the Indian Residential School system in Canada, as well as the country's response to the schools in recent times.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the connections the author makes between truth and healing.
articles.permissions: Excerpted with permission from Encyclopædia Britannica, © 2023 by Encyclopædia Britannica, Inc.

blocks.text: Introduction

Beginning in the 1880s, the Canadian government set up a school system for Indigenous children. The main goal of these schools was to make Indigenous children learn how to live according to white customs and beliefs. This policy for dealing with the Indigenous population was called forced assimilation. It was also used by the Australian government and the U.S. government.

The children were taken from their homes and forced to attend the schools. At the schools they were mistreated, and many died. The last schools closed in the late 1900s. In the early 2000s the Canadian government established a committee to look into the abuses at the schools. In 2008 the government issued a formal apology for the harm caused by the school system.
Indian Residential School System

Canada’s first school for Indigenous children was established in 1828. It was called the Mohawk Institute and was in Brantford, in what is now Ontario. The Mohawk Institute was run by the Anglican church. In 1876 a law called the Indian Act was passed in order to assimilate First Nations people into white society. A later amendment to the Indian Act created the Indian Residential School (IRS) system. The system was organized so that the schools were funded by the Canadian government but were run by various Christian churches: Roman Catholic, Anglican, Methodist, and Presbyterian.

Indigenous children were forcibly removed from their homes and sent to an IRS. At the school they were often separated from their siblings. They were not allowed to speak their language or to practice their culture in any way. If they did, they were severely punished. The education at the IRS was not the same education the non-Indigenous population received. The girls were taught domestic skills (laundry, sewing, cooking, and cleaning), and the boys were taught farming, carpentry, and maintenance. By the time they were 18 and sent away from the school, most students had reached only the fifth-grade level. While at an IRS, many Indigenous students experienced some form of abuse. They were also likely to get sick because of the living conditions. Thousands of Indigenous children died during the years of the IRS system. It was common for the dead children to be buried in unmarked graves somewhere on the school site.

The number of residential schools grew quickly after 1883. A government medical inspector reported in 1907 that high numbers of Indigenous children were dying at the boarding schools. However, church and government officials did not do anything about it. Beginning in 1920 every First Nations child between the ages of 7 and 16 was required to attend an IRS. (The policy was not consistently applied to Métis and Inuit children.) By the mid-1950s the government began taking over church-run residential schools in the Arctic. In 1969 the government took over the remaining residential schools from the churches. In 1979 only 28 residential schools remained open, but thousands of Indigenous students were enrolled in them at the time. The last schools closed at the end of the 1900s.

In 1990 a residential school survivor who was also a First Nations chief spoke out about the abuse he suffered and called for a public investigation. The government began the inquiry the following year. The Royal Commission on Aboriginal Peoples released its report in 1996. The report made clear the harm that the Indian Act did to Aboriginal and Indigenous peoples and their cultures.
Truth and Reconciliation Commission

For many years former IRS students (IRS Survivors) tried to get the Canadian government and the churches that ran the schools to acknowledge the abuses and provide some kind of payment. The result was the Indian Residential Schools Settlement Agreement in 2007. The settlement included financial payments for all IRS Survivors. The prime minister at the time, Stephen Harper, made a public apology on June 11, 2008. The settlement also created the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC). Starting in 2008, the TRC interviewed IRS Survivors, their families, and residential school staff and shared those stories with the rest of the country.

After speaking with more than 6,000 people, the commission released its report in 2015. The report included 94 calls to action. The calls to action included creating a National Day for Truth and Reconciliation and creating school curriculum so all students learn about residential schools, treaties, and Indigenous peoples’ contributions to Canada. The report also called upon the pope, as the head of the Roman Catholic Church, to issue an apology to IRS Survivors and their families. A large section of the report focused on missing children and unmarked burials. It called upon the federal government to help find and protect the location of deceased residential school children. In 2021 the remains of more than 1,000 people, mostly children, were discovered on the grounds of three former residential schools in Saskatchewan and British Columbia.

The TRC created the National Centre for Truth and Reconciliation (NCTR) at the University of Manitoba. The center was established to continue the research of the TRC, to promote public education and understanding of the residential schools, and to help foster reconciliation and healing around the subject.

September 30, 2021, was the first National Day for Truth and Reconciliation. The day honors the lost children and IRS Survivors. All Canadians are encouraged to wear orange on September 30 as a way to show respect and remembrance.

In April 2022 Pope Francis apologized for the role of the Roman Catholic Church in the abuse and cultural repression of the residential schools. In July he traveled to Canada as part of the healing and reconciliation process.

options.label: Canada was the only country in North America to create an Indian Residential School system.
Residential schools provided an option for poor Indigenous children to receive a quality education.
Residential schools were harmful to some Indigenous students, but they were helpful to many others.

There is an ongoing effort by the Canadian government to heal the wounds caused by residential schools. E. The Indian Residential School system was designed to destroy and erase Indigenous cultures, languages, and familial bonds. F. Indigenous people have asked the government to take responsibility for damage caused by the schools, but their requests have been ignored.

discussionQuestions.prompt: How do you think Canada's efforts to deal with its painful history compare with the United States' efforts to heal its own historical wounds? Explain your response using examples from history, current events, and real life.

What are the benefits and challenges of learning about painful — and sometimes shameful — events in our country's history? Is it better to confront a painful history and learn from it, or ignore it and leave it in the past? How might one's personal, familial, and cultural histories impact their willingness to learn about — and from — history?

According to the article, both former prime minister of Canada Stephen Harper and Pope Francis apologized for their organizations' role in the forced assimilation of Indigenous people in Canada. Are official acknowledgements and apologies after historical wrongdoings important? Why, or why not? Use examples from current events, history, or real-life to support your response.

Imagine being forcibly separated from your family and sent to an institution whose goal was to erase all aspects of your culture? How do you think that would feel? What parts of your current "self" might be erased forever? How do you imagine that would impact the rest of your life and future generations of your family?

articles.title: Voices on the Trail: Women Moving West
articles.author: Marcia Amidon Lusted
articles.description: On the trails to the American West in the 1800s, women faced many untold hardships.
articles.intro: In the early 1800s, some Americans decided to leave their homes in search of new lives in the American West. This period of time is known as Westward Expansion. In this text, Marcia Amidon Lusted discusses the often untold stories of women on these journeys.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the struggles that women faced on the journey west.
articles.permissions: "Voices On the Trail: Women Moving West" by Marcia Amidon Lusted. Copyright © 2023 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: The story of pioneers who headed west to build new lives for themselves is often told through the mouths of men. We have countless tales of cowboys and ranchers, sheriffs and outlaws. Meanwhile, women traveled in covered wagons, coping the best they could and trying to take care of their families in unfamiliar circumstances. Though no less important, their stories have been overlooked in the larger story of the Western Frontier. That is, until recently. For a long time, the history of pioneers has "Untitled" by Dana Davis is licensed under CC0. focused on the dangers and hardships faced by men, written in their words. But historians began filling in the gaps by studying the experiences of women and families on the trails. They specifically looked at the challenge of maintaining family routines in the face of harsh conditions. Most of the women who traveled west were married, as it was rare for single women to go west on their own. They often didn’t know how to saddle a horse, hitch a team of oxen to a wagon, or drive the team. It wasn’t considered ladylike. Instead, women followed the cultural patterns of their time: they were wives, mothers, and housekeepers, while men worried about safety and livelihoods. Men were also usually the ones eager to go west for a better life. When husbands made this decision, their wives usually had no choice but to accept it, no matter how they felt. Margaret Hereford Wilson, wife of Dr. Benjamin Wilson, a man who would one day become mayor of Los Angeles, shared her reluctance: “I am going with him, as there is no other alternative.” Some women, however, were excited by the opportunity. One such woman, Miriam Thompson Teller, found herself “possessed with a spirit of adventure and a desire to see what was new and strange.” But what was life on the trail like for these women? The day had a strict schedule, one that started and ended in the dark. The day began at 4 a.m. with a signal from the night guards to wake up the camp and didn’t end until late at night. Dangers on the journey west ranged from golf ball-sized hail to buffalo stampedes to treacherous river crossings. For their safety, most pioneers traveled in groups with other families. Families packed everything they owned into their wagon and traveled between 15 to 20 miles every day. Generally, the women and children walked beside the wagon, plodding through dust and over rough terrain. At each meal, the wagons stopped, and the women cooked over an open fire, using the food supplies the family had brought with them. If there was no sagebrush or wood available to make a fire, women and children gathered the most readily available kindling they could find: dried buffalo manure. When food supplies ran low, they had to make do with whatever could be found nearby or whatever the men could hunt. It was a tedious routine, especially as the journey continued. Luzena Stanley Wilson, wife of a Gold Rush entrepreneur, wrote, “Day after day, week after week, we went through the same weary routine of breaking camp at daybreak, yoking the oxen, cooking our meager rations over a fire of sagebrush and scrub-oak; packing up again, coffeepot and camp-kettle; washing our scanty wardrobe in the little streams we crossed; striking camp again at sunset, or later if wood and water were scarce. Tired, dusty, tried in temper, worn out in patience, we had to go over the weary experience tomorrow.” If you have ever been on a long road trip with family, or even friends, it’s easy to imagine Wilson’s weariness as the journey west could take between five to six months to complete.

But sometimes the routine would be completely upended. Emergencies and disasters could happen in a moment and were often left for the women to handle. Illness and injury were common on the trail. Wagons broke down, cattle died, diseases such as cholera swept through wagon trains, and starvation could strike in an instant if supplies ran out and no other sources of food could be found. Furthermore, many women gave birth during their journeys. It was common for women to die in childbirth, and newborn infants often did not survive. Catherine Haun, who traveled to California in 1849, wrote of traveling through a treeless, arid area of the Great Basin region of Nevada. “Our only death on the journey occurred in this desert… Mrs. Lamore suddenly sickened [after childbirth] and died, leaving her two little girls and grief stricken husband. We halted a day to bury her and the infant that had lived but an hour, in this weird, lonely spot… away apparently from everywhere and everybody.” Graves were spotted often on the trail. Elizabeth Keegan, who traveled west at 18, wrote, “We did not meet any sickness nor see any fresh graves until we came in on the road from St Joseph. From that out, there was scarcely a day, but we met six and not less than two fresh graves.” Life on the trail was difficult for all who traveled it. To the best of their abilities, women carried on, but there were times when it felt like more than they could handle. Lavina Porter later wrote, “I would throw myself down on the unfriendly desert and give way like a child to sobs and tears, wishing myself back home with my friends and chiding myself for consenting to take this wild goose chase.” The difficulty women faced on the trail has largely been invisible, their voices muffled by stories of cowboys and outlaws. Even those who traveled west could only see the suffering clearly in hindsight. Martha Morrison Minto, who moved west as a child in the 1. a person who starts and runs their own business mid-1800s, reflected, “It strikes me as I think of it now — of course, I was a girl, too young then to know much about it — but I think now the mothers on the road had to undergo more trial and suffering than anybody else.” But despite the hardships and heartaches of the journey west, women persevered, finding a way to fulfill their domestic responsibilities even when the only place they had to call home was the trail.

options.label: On the trail west, women's daily tasks were less important than men's.
On the trail west, women were in charge of their family's money and spending.
Women had important roles on the journey west despite the lack of stories told about them.
Many women in the 1800s decided to take the journey west despite being inexperienced at travel.
